articles.title: Don’t Look Back in Anger: Humor, Self-Ironic Reflection, and the Structural Transformation of Retrospective Rage
articles.authors: Koichi Hiraoka
articles.abstract:
This paper theorizes how memories of harm and interpersonal conflict are transformed 
when the past is repeatedly recalled through anger, and how alternative pathways 
through humor, Self-Ironic Reflection, and structural understanding may become 
possible. Anger is an important emotion for detecting injustice, humiliation, and 
violations of dignity. However, when the past is viewed through anger alone, the target 
person may become cognitively magnified: no longer experienced as an ordinary human 
being who acted within a specific situation, but as a continuing threat that occupies the 
present psychological space. This paper conceptualizes this process as Retrospective 
Rage and Anger-Based Magnification. 
In response, the paper proposes Humor-Based Deflation as an alternative cognitive 
process. Humor, in this framework, does not mean trivializing harm or excusing the 
harmful actor. Rather, it refers to a practice through which the anger-magnified target is 
returned to human scale and the event becomes available for structural understanding. 
The paper further distinguishes between two pathways of inferiority and shame 
processing: one that converts inferiority into anger, domination, silence, and exclusion, 
and another that converts it into self-relativization, humor, learning, and structural 
reflection. In doing so, it connects anger memory to workplace harassment, 
organizational silence, and psychological safety. 
The central claim is that “Don’t Look Back in Anger” does not mean forgetting the 
past, suppressing anger, or excusing harm. Rather, it means refusing to let anger become 
the only form of retrospective understanding. By transforming memories of anger into 
humor, Self-Ironic Reflection, and structural analysis, personal pain can become public 
knowledge. The paper offers a theoretical framework for reconsidering workplace 
conflict, silence, harassment, and psychological safety through the paired processes of 
anger-based magnification and humor-based deflation.

articles.title: Salted Manananggal: A Cultural-Psychological Review of Behavioral Entrapment, Mixed Signals, and Emotional Limbo in I'm Drunk, I Love You (2017)
articles.authors: Benjamin Abalos Lorenzana
articles.abstract: This conceptual paper investigates the structural and psychological anatomy of romantic limbo by analyzing contemporary Philippine cinema through a localized, cultural-psychological lens. Specifically, this study utilizes the narrative framework of JP Habac's indie film I'm Drunk, I Love You (2017) to explore the subtle mechanisms that sustain unrequited love among contemporary Filipino youth. By stripping away localized horror tropes, the paper posits the mythological manananggal as a profound cultural allegory for unrequited love, representing permanent emotional bisection and physical fragmentation. It argues that a lover's suspension in this emotional limbo is driven by a powerful behavioral duality: intermittent reinforcement, where ambiguous mixed signals function as an addictive conditioning mechanism, and behavioral entrapment (the sunk-cost fallacy), where individuals remain trapped to justify their long-term psychological investments of youth, time, and effort. Finally, by deconstructing the local glorification of being marupok (soft-hearted), this paper explores how cultural spaces anchored in the core value of Kapwa (shared identity) provide a collective sanctuary for pining, reframing a one-sided existence as a deeply human, socially validated response to the agony of a love that remains perpetually almost within reach.

articles.title: Versions and subversions: the case for an archaeology of subtitling
articles.authors: Myron Tsikandilakis; Pierre-Alexis Mevel
articles.abstract:
Films are not static objects. They undergo continuous transformation. Recuts, remasters, and format shifts (e.g., Blu-ray, 4K restorations, 3D and VR adaptations) alter their visual and auditory composition. These changes may be material and physical (Bignell, 2019). They may stem from intertextual influences and ideological constraints (Marcus, 1993), a characteristic that extends even to film genres (Altman, 1999). This chapter argues that such transformations also apply to subtitles and other modes of audiovisual translation, further complicating the notion of films as fixed entities.
Traditionally, subtitles were perceived as a stable, supplementary feature – fixed translations accompanying a singular cinematic text. In the DVD era, a film was typically released with one set of subtitles, reinforcing the idea of a monolithic filmic entity. However, exceptions such as La Haine (Kassovitz, 1995), which featured two distinct sets of English subtitles across different DVD editions, hinted at the potential for subtitles to reshape audience interpretation. The subtitling of La Haine has been widely discussed in academic literature (Jäckel, 2001; Hamaidia, 2007; Mével, 2007, 2008; Montgomery, 2008; Silvester, 2018), particularly in relation to the new subtitles introduced for the film’s 10th anniversary DVD edition, which marked a clear departure from the original translation (Mével, 2009).
Today, the proliferation of releases on disc or on streaming platforms has led to films being routinely re-subtitled, further challenging the idea of a singular, definitive film experience. La Haine is both a precursor and an example of this phenomenon, as it is now available with multiple sets of subtitles, including recent releases by the British Film Institute and as well as on Apple TV. By using La Haine as a case study, this chapter explores how subtitling subverts the notion of a singular, authoritative film text. We highlight the ways in which translation, distribution, and technological shifts contribute to the ongoing redefinition of cinematic works, reinforcing the idea that a film is never just one film, but many incarnations of itself.

articles.title: Non-Robustness in Log-Like Specifications
articles.authors: Jack Fitzgerald; Joop Adema; Lenka Fiala; Essi Kujansuu; David Valenta
articles.abstract: Recent literature shows that when regression models are estimated on variables transformed with 'log-like' functions such as the inverse hyperbolic sine or ln(Z + 1) transformations, one can obtain (semi-)elasticity estimates of any magnitude by linearly re-scaling the input variable(s) before transformation. We systematically re-analyze the replication data of 46 papers whose main conclusions are defended by log-like specifications. Our replication findings motivate new theoretical and simulation results showing that in log-like specifications, unit scale can be used to overfit data, creating an uncontrolled multiple hypothesis testing problem that frequently yields spuriously significant results. In particular, 38% of the estimates we re-analyze sit in a 'sweet spot', where both upward and downward re-scalings of variables' units before transformation shrink test statistics. Consequently, published estimates in this literature are statistically significant over 40% more frequently than in the general economics literature. We find that modest changes to model specification yield different statistical significance conclusions for 14-37% of estimates defending papers' main claims. We also show that for 99.8% of estimates, variables transformed with log-like functions do not meet data requirements for log-like specifications from a methodological recommendation cited by all papers in our replication sample. We synthesize and harmonize methodological guidelines and advocate for more robust alternative specifications, including normalized estimands, Poisson regression, and quantile regression.

articles.title: PREIS: A Modular, Reproducible and AI-Assisted Framework for Epidemic Intelligence Workflows in Africa
articles.authors: Raogo Hyacinthe ZABRE
articles.abstract:
Background. Epidemic intelligence increasingly requires the integration of official surveillance information, event-based signals, open-source information, scientific evidence and operational context. Many public health teams in Africa would benefit from locally maintainable analytical layers that can transform heterogeneous inputs into reproducible, auditable and decision-oriented products while preserving expert review, institutional data governance and official communication channels.
Objective. This manuscript describes PREIS (Pan-African Real-time Epidemiological Intelligence System), a modular and reproducible workflow architecture designed to support signal capture, harmonisation, risk-prioritisation, dashboard generation, situation summary preparation and conditional notification templates for epidemic intelligence use cases in Africa.
Methods. This is a methodological system-design paper. PREIS is organised around five interacting layers: data-source configuration, harmonisation, intelligence processing, output generation and review-to-action feedback. The framework follows a CORI cycle - Capture, Organise, Review and Inform - and emphasises modularity, reproducibility, provenance tracking, human-in-the-loop review, cautious risk communication and data protection. It is intended to be implemented using open analytical environments such as R, Python and Shiny, with configuration files defining disease themes, geographic scope, source permissions, classification rules, quality checks and notification thresholds.
Results. The proposed framework defines a minimum intelligence data model, quality gates, configurable scoring dimensions, dashboard-ready outputs and governance safeguards. Proof-of-concept development has shown the feasibility of standardised data append workflows, country and regional classification, quality-control logging, prototype dashboards, illustrative risk maps and notification templates. These outputs are methodological demonstrations; they are not official outbreak alerts and have not yet undergone prospective external validation against verified epidemiological ground truth.
Conclusions. PREIS is best understood as an adaptable analytical layer that may complement, rather than replace, established surveillance frameworks and platforms. Its contribution is to document a reproducible workflow model that can help public health practitioners organise heterogeneous signals into transparent and reviewable intelligence products. Future work should focus on co-design with authorised institutions, legal data-sharing arrangements, prospective validation, performance evaluation and disease-specific peer-reviewed studies.
Keywords: epidemic intelligence; event-based surveillance; public health surveillance; Africa; reproducible analytics; dashboard; risk prioritisation; open-source intelligence; data governance; human-in-the-loop; PREIS

articles.title: The International Component of the Unified State Register of Scholarly Publication Channels: New Questions and Unresolved Issues
articles.authors: Denis Kosyakov; Dmitry Kochetkov
articles.abstract: This article analyses the methodology used to construct the international component of the Unified State Register of Scholarly Publication Channels (USRSPС) and considers the results of its application to a corpus of international publication channels. The empirical analysis draws on the normalisation of files submitted by the Russian Centre for Scientific Information (RCSI) for review by the divisions of the Russian Academy of Sciences, the merging of duplicate records across subject categories, and the comparison of the 2026 USRSPС recommendations with the international component of the 2023 Whitelist. The findings show that the methodology merges, within a single final tier, indicators of a journal's scientific visibility, technical observability in bibliographic infrastructures, authors' institutional affiliations, geographic profile, and legal and political status. Treating these different dimensions as one category makes the tiers difficult to interpret and the resulting ranking decisions hard to reproduce. Based on this analysis, we propose revisions to the scoring rules and, more generally, to the design of the national list of scholarly journals.

articles.title: The social transmission of linguistic alignment: the role of observational reinforcement learning
articles.authors: Yichi ZHANG; Zhenguang Garry Cai
articles.abstract: People align their linguistic use in conversations and it has been proposed that such linguistic alignment facilitates mutual understanding and social rapport. However, the mechanisms through which perspective alignment is socially transmitted across individuals remain unclear. Here, we combined behavioral experiments and computational learning models to investigate whether, and if so, how observing aligned and non-aligned responses in others affects the perspective alignment of observers. Participants completed a three-phase design: they first played a referential game with a child interlocutor (baseline), then completed an observation phase in which, on each trial, they watched an adult demonstrator, who exhibited either high or low alignment with the child’s referential perspective and predicted the demonstrators choice before observing it, and then played a round with the child themselves (observation), and finally played with a new child (generalization). Observing high alignment increased observers’ own alignment, whereas observing low alignment decreased it, and these changes carried over to the new child, indicating a transferable communicative strategy rather than a partner-specific adjustment. To identify the underlying mechanism, we modelled behaviour trial by trial. A Rescorla–Wagner model first confirmed that participants learned to predict the demonstrator’s choices. We then used Bayesian model comparison to adjudicate three accounts of how alignment changed. A model driven by observational prediction errors (i.e., the discrepancy between participants’ predictions and the demonstrator’s actual choices) outperformed both an imitation account (copying the most recent choice) and a priming account (the cumulative frequency of the demonstrator’s aligned responses). In the high alignment group, both positive and negative prediction errors in the second half of the observation phase predicted alignment changes, whereas in the low alignment group, negative prediction errors predicted decreased alignment. Exploratory analyses further suggested that alignment may function as a social signal: observers rated high-alignment demonstrators as more perspective-taking than low-alignment demonstrators. Together, these findings show that perspective alignment can be socially transmitted through prediction-error-driven observational learning, neither passive copying nor mere exposure, and offer a computational account of how communicative strategies spread across individuals.

articles.title: Supporting Children on Therapy Waitlists: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Web-Based Parent-Focused Single Session Intervention for Child Anxiety
articles.authors: Madelaine Abel; Jessica L. Schleider; Jenna Yejin Sung; Alayna D'Amico; Dina R. Hirshfeld-Becker
articles.abstract: Many children experience significant, impairing anxiety, yet most never access evidence-based support; among those who do, most face long wait-times to be seen. This disconnect reflects a need for intervention approaches that provide rapid access to interim care. Single-session interventions (SSIs) may help mitigate the adverse impacts of long waitlists. This pilot parallel group RCT evaluated the effectiveness and acceptability of a self-guided, web-based SSI delivered to parents of children ages 4-13 (Mage=8.84) with anxiety or OCD on a waitlist at a hospital-based Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT) clinic. The SSI, Project EMPOWER, targets parental accommodation, a family factor that can worsen child anxiety and impede efficacy of CBT. Parents were randomized to receive the SSI (N=24) or waitlist as usual (N=23). Parents completed a baseline diagnostic interview about their child and self-report questionnaires about their accommodation of child anxiety, child anxiety symptoms, and perceived ability to manage their child’s anxiety (parent agency) at baseline, 2-week follow-up, and CBT intake. Relative to controls, parents who received the SSI reported significant reductions in their accommodation (d=0.59, p=.046) and child anxiety (d=1.25, p<.001) and significant increases in parent agency (d=0.68, p=.02) from baseline to 2-week follow-up. Effects were maintained until children started CBT (mean time =13.54 weeks). Additionally, parents rated the SSI as highly acceptable in accordance with preregistered benchmarks. Results support the effectiveness and acceptability of a web-based, self-guided SSI for parents of children on therapy waitlists, offering a scalable approach to support families while they wait for formal treatment.

articles.title: Reduced Visual Imagery Vividness as a Risk Factor for PTSD: Implications for Aphantasia and Imagery-Based Treatments
articles.authors: Diane Swick; Sandy Lwi; Jary Larsen
articles.abstract: Recent theoretical models such as the generative adversarial framework propose that the ability to generate high-fidelity visual images may intensify the severity of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), particularly the intrusive re-experiencing of traumatic events. Meanwhile, individuals with minimal imagery (aphantasia) may be protected. To evaluate this relationship, 312 Veterans completed the Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire (VVIQ) and the PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5). Results indicated that lower VVIQ scores (less vivid imagery) were associated with greater PTSD severity. This inverse relationship was observed across all PTSD symptom clusters, with the strongest association for the negative cognition/mood cluster. Five of the eight Veterans who reported very low VVIQ scores (consistent with aphantasia) had clinically significant PTSD and high levels of intrusive symptoms, further indicating that aphantasia was not protective. These findings have implications for PTSD treatments and suggest that reduced visual imagery is associated with impaired processing of traumatic experiences.

articles.title: Indigenous Perspectives of Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Using Community-Based and Consensual Qualitative Frameworks
articles.authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich; Linda Zheng; Ashley Baumann; Jill Kessler; Lyndsey Ducheneaux; Viann N Nguyen-Feng
articles.abstract:
Objective: Indigenous communities endure intergenerational and ongoing traumas. Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga (TCTSY) is an empirically-supported yoga-based intervention for complex trauma. To become a TCTSY-Facilitator, candidates complete a 20-hour training followed by a 300-hour certification program. This community-based research documented the first Indigenous TCTSY-Facilitator Training Cohort’s perspectives on TCTSY’s acceptability, feasibility, and utility. 
Method: Protocols were developed with community partners, and researchers engaged in reflexivity practices and Indigenous cultural learnings. Twenty interviews were conducted with 15 adult interviewees: 14 program participants (13 women) who completed the 20-hour training and 1 program mentor. From the 14 program participants (11 Great Plains Tribal Nations, with 4 Tribal citizens and 7 descendents), 10 enrolled in the 300-hour training, and 3 completed the full certification. Interviews were analyzed using the consensual qualitative research method. 
Results: Most participants reported the training positively impacted their approach to trauma care and some aspects (e.g., body-based modality) aligned with current healing approaches. Common training takeaways included learning about the effects of trauma and the value of invitational language. Participants endorsed intentions to integrate TCTSY for personal and community healing; examples of integration included smudging/burning of medicines (e.g., sage), land-based practice for connection to nature, and intentional movement within ceremony space. Program improvements included additional emphasis on yoga history/philosophy, collaborations with Elders, and acknowledgment of current community trauma. 
Conclusion: A community-based research framework provided opportunities to explore the feasibility, acceptability, and utility of TCTSY for Indigenous communities in a culturally-relevant and trauma-informed way.

articles.title: Voice as a Cognitive Anchor: The VOCES Framework for Identity Preservation in Early Cognitive Decline
articles.authors: Lisette Diaz-Santiago
articles.abstract: As global populations age, early cognitive decline increasingly affects individuals and the families navigating identity disruption and relational strain alongside them. Contemporary memory-support interventions emphasize visual recall, orientation, and task-based cognitive stimulation, yet emotionally salient relational voice remains underused as a structured intervention mechanism. A growing body of evidence indicates that affectively encoded emotional responses can persist even after declarative memory for the events that produced them has faded. Building on this evidence, the paper advances a conceptual contribution: familiar relational voice — the voice of an attachment figure — is proposed to function as a cognitive anchor that may support emotional continuity, identity preservation, relational connection, and orientation even as episodic memory weakens. The paper introduces the VOCES Framework (Voz Orientada a la Continuidad, Emoción y Sostén), a culturally integrative, dignity-centered model that positions relational voice, emotional encoding, narrative continuity, and ritualized support as stabilizing mechanisms for identity continuity. The framework synthesizes research across auditory neuroscience, emotional memory, narrative identity, person-centered care, attachment theory, and healthcare communication; it does not claim that voice alters disease processes. MemoriGems is presented as one applied illustration of the framework. The paper concludes by outlining the research directions — including mixed-methods, feasibility, and implementation studies — needed to empirically evaluate voice-centered approaches to identity preservation in early cognitive decline.

articles.title: Influence campaigns in a custom social media platform affect beliefs, trust, and engagement
articles.authors: Manikya Alister; Keith Ransom; Andrew Perfors
articles.abstract: Despite growing concerns about how social media use can affect people's beliefs and behavior, estimating its causal impact is difficult. Here we present a novel method for studying social media effects using our own bespoke social media platform built using an isolated Mastodon server. Our platform allowed participants to interact much as they would on typical social media. Critically however, we controlled who could use the platform, which features it exposed, and we measured people's beliefs and attitudes before and after using the platform. This allowed us to more closely approximate the ecological validity of naturally occurring social-media data, while retaining the ability to measure variables and infer causation. Participants interacted over several days in three between-subject conditions (total N=311): a Control condition in which people engaged on the platform with no external influence, and two "troll" conditions (Left and Right) in which a small number of pre-planned posts sharing typical talking points from one political side were covertly made by us. We found that  our minimal influence campaigns resulted in statistically credible effects on engagement, beliefs, perceptions of others, and experience on the platform. More broadly, this work reveals the utility of our novel approach for studying social media effects.

articles.title: Am I Anxious or Rejected?  Measuring Implicit Associations in Social Anxiety
articles.authors: Mackenna Pattison; Dalainey H. Drakes; Eldar Eftekhari; Saray Velert-Jiménez; Nancy Bahl; Cynthia-Maria Kanaan; Allison Ouimet
articles.abstract: Social anxiety research has primarily relied on explicit self-report measures, which are vulnerable to response biases. Implicit measures have been used to examine underlying mechanisms of social anxiety (e.g., automatic associations). However, debate remains about their validity and utility. Prior research suggests that individuals with social anxiety are slower to associate the self with social acceptance-related words. We investigated whether the Implicit Association Test (IAT), using rejection vs. acceptance and anxiety vs. calm words, predicts anxiety during a speech task beyond explicit measures. We also tested whether concurrent facial electromyography (EMG) activity adds validity evidence for the IAT. Undergraduate and community participants completed IATs while recording facial EMG, followed by a speech task. They rated social anxiety at baseline and before and after the speech. Contrary to hypotheses, participants with high social anxiety did not exhibit stronger negative implicit associations nor increased EMG corrugator activity. Corrugator activity did not distinguish between inconsistent and consistent IAT blocks in high social anxiety participants. Self-report measures significantly predicted anxiety during the speech task; the addition of IAT scores did not account for incremental variance beyond self-reports. Our findings add to the literature related to validity problems with the IAT when assessing anxiety.

articles.title: Psychometric Comparability of LLM–Based Digital Twins
articles.authors: Yufei Zhang; Zhihao Ma
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are used as “digital twins” to replace human respondents, yet their psychometric comparability to humans is uncertain. We propose a construct-validity framework spanning construct representation and the nomological net, benchmarking digital twins against human gold standards across models, tasks and testing how person-specific inputs shape performance. Across studies, digital twins achieved high population-level accuracy and strong within-participant profile correlations, alongside attenuated item-level correlations. In word association tests, LLM-based networks show small-world structure and theory-consistent communities similar to humans, yet diverge lexically and in local structure. In decision-making and contextualized tasks, digital twins under-reproduce heuristic biases, showing normative rationality, compressed variance and limited sensitivity to temporal information. Feature-rich digital twins improve Big Five Personality prediction, but their personality networks show only configural invariance and do not achieve metric invariance. In more applied free-text tasks, feature-rich digital twins better match human narratives, but linguistic differences persist. Together, these results indicate that feature-rich conditioning enhances validity but does not resolve systematic divergences in psychometric comparability. Future work should therefore prioritize delineating the effective boundaries of digital twins, establishing the precise contexts in which they function as reliable proxies for human cognition and behavior.

articles.title: What do people believe about artificial intelligence chatbots for mental health support? Evidence from Greece
articles.authors: Aglaia Katsiroumpa; Parisis Gallos; Paschalina Lialiou; Ioannis Moisoglou; Olga Galani; Panagiota Peleka; Angeliki Triantafillaki; Maria Tsiachri; Petros Galanis
articles.abstract: Background: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots into mental health practices has grown substantially over the past few years. Objective: To examine people’s attitudes toward AI chatbots for mental health support. Moreover, we investigated individuals’ attitudes towards AI in general. Additionally, we examined demographic variables that influence these attitudes. Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional study in January 2026. We employed a convenience sample in Greece. We used the Artificial Intelligence in Mental Health Scale (AIMHS) to measure attitudes towards AI chatbots for mental health support. Also, we used the Artificial Intelligence Attitude Scale (AIAS-4) to measure general attitude toward AI. We examined the impact of demographic variables on attitudes towards AI through multivariable regression models. Results: AIMHS scores demonstrated a moderate level of positive attitudes toward AI chatbots for mental health support. Likewise, AIAS-4 scores reflected a moderate degree of favorable attitudes toward artificial intelligence in general. We found a statistically significant positive correlation between AIMHS and AIAS-4. Multivariable linear regression analysis showed that males and higher educated participants had more positive attitudes towards AI chatbots for mental health support. Moreover, increased age was associated with increased positive attitudes towards AI mental health chatbots. Age, daily use of social media/websites and competence in digital technologies were associated with increased positive attitudes towards AI in general. Also, higher educated participants had more positive attitudes towards AI in general. Conclusion: We identified moderate levels of positive attitudes toward AI mental health chatbots and AI in general. Several demographic characteristics affected individuals’ attitudes towards AI.

articles.title: Parenting and children’s ADHD symptoms: A longitudinal twin-difference study
articles.authors: Sophie von Stumm
articles.abstract: ADHD symptoms in children are often attributed to the parenting that they experience, despite strong evidence for genetic influences. Here, we tested whether associations between parenting and children’s ADHD symptoms reflected shared family-level confounding. At the population level, random-intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) suggested significant bidirectional processes between children’s ADHD symptoms and their experiences of harsh discipline, constructive parenting, and negative parent-child feelings across the ages 8, 12, and 14 years (N = 7,429, including 2,762 monozygotic twins, 2393 same-sex dizygotic twins, and 4,017 girls). In twin-difference CLPM and RI-CLPM that controlled for family-level confounding, the associations between parenting and children’s ADHD symptoms were attenuated and became non-significant. Our findings challenge claims that parenting can cause ADHD in children and underscore the importance of accounting for family-level confounding when studying parent–child dynamics in families affected by ADHD.

articles.title: Development of an Abbreviated Form of the Extended Strengths and Weaknesses Assessment of Normal Behavior for Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (E-SWAN DMDD)
articles.authors: Juliette Brook; Isabella Stallworthy; Golia Shafiei; Joëlle Bagautdinova; S. Parker Singleton; Brooke Sevchik; Giovanni Salum; Michael Milham; Theodore D. Satterthwaite; Tyler Maxwell Moore
articles.abstract: Few measures assess the specific indicators of disruptive mood dysregulation disorder (DMDD). While the E-SWAN DMDD questionnaire evaluates DMDD symptoms, its length increases response burden, and limits use in resource-constrained settings. Using data from 3,835 participants in the Healthy Brain Network (HBN; mean age = 10.25 years; 63% male), item responses were calibrated using the Graded Response Model and computerized adaptive testing (CAT) simulations were conducted to develop an abbreviated questionnaire. The four most frequently selected items comprise the short form, which correlated highly with the full form (Levy-corrected r = 0.776), and demonstrated moderate associations with related behavioral constructs (ρ = 0.120 - 0.424, p < .001). Compared to the full form, the short form demonstrated good specificity (a weaker association with the general psychopathology factor) while maintaining strong associations with externalizing behavior. Taken together, the short form offers a brief assessment with strong validity that decreases response burden.

articles.title: Putting the brain back in its body: Integrating internal bodily signals in multimodal language research
articles.authors: Simone Gastaldon
articles.abstract: The embodied turn in language science has established the visible body (gesture, gaze, mouth and brow movement) as integral to how meaning is predicted and built in face-to-face conversation. We argue that this turn should extend inward, to each interlocutor's internal body, and across both comprehension and production. Self-generated visceral signals (cardiac, respiratory, gastric, and arousal-linked) act as internal control rhythms that serve three functions a theory of conversation requires: gating the uptake of incoming signals, regulating the timing of outgoing action including turn onset, and coupling across interacting individuals. These functions are not channel-symmetric, with respiration most directly tied to speech and cardiac and arousal signals setting the gain. Cast as neural gain on weighted signals, this view adds concrete, falsifiable predictions to current processing accounts, opening a largely unexplored frontier for the science of human communication.

articles.title: Individual differences in bilingual experience predict response times during language switching via functional reactive brain responses
articles.authors: Zlatomira Ilchovska; Ali Mazaheri; Andrea Krott
articles.abstract: Switching between two languages during speech production has been found to be costly, especially when switching back into the dominant language. The costs seem to be related to individual bilingual factors such as language proficiency, but findings are mixed. We investigated whether bilingual experience factors - composed of measures such as language switching behaviour or language proficiency - predict bilingual language switch costs via functional brain processes during language switching. Eighty-nine young healthy adult bilinguals completed the Switching Experience and Environment Questionnaire and engaged in a cued picture naming language switching task, during which neural activity was recorded using electroencephalography. Behaviourally, we found global slowing for the dominant language as well as asymmetric switch costs in that switch costs were larger for the dominant language than the weaker language In terms of the EEG responses, cue locked ERPs showed switch related posterior positivity (P3 like) and L1 specific anterior negativity, indicating proactive task set reconfiguration and greater control demands for L1, while target locked ERPs revealed a late switch positivity and enhanced L2 effects, reflecting carry over control and higher lexical/production costs for the weaker language. Cue locked alpha suppression showed a greater switching cost for L1, reflecting increased inhibitory and attentional control demands when reactivating the dominant language. Structural equation models indicated that questionnaire-derived bilingual experience factors selectively predicted cue locked neural effects. These neural effects in turn predicted the behavioural global slowing effect for the dominant language. They also predicted behavioural switch cost, though only for the weaker language. Results suggest that variations in language switching frequency, as well as increased proficiency and use of the weaker language, lead to differences in behavioural language switch costs via a complex system of neural control processes operating over whole languages rather than individual words. These findings are consistent with the Inhibitory Control Model (Green, 1998), which posits that bilingual language control is achieved by inhibiting the non-target language, with stronger inhibition of the dominant language giving rise to asymmetric switch cost. In addition, to our knowledge, this study provides the first evidence that asymmetric switch costs decrease with greater L1/L2 balance and usage, and that this reduction is associated with reduced neural control demands during L2 switching.

articles.title: Trait absorption as predictor of altered states of consciousness across three relaxation induction methods
articles.authors: Marc Wittmann; Amelia Lacassagne; Clara Koinegg; Marvin Hottenbacher; Sophia Saad
articles.abstract: This study investigates the effects of three variants of Restricted Environmental Stimulation Techniques (REST)—guided meditation, multimodal Ganzfeld (MMGF), and a control resting condition—on subjective experiences related to altered states of consciousness (ASC). Specifically, the study examines emotional responses, time perception, and perceived body boundaries, while also assessing the role of trait absorption as an individual difference variable. In a within-subjects design, 48 healthy participants without prior meditation experience completed three counterbalanced 25-minute sessions across the three conditions. Measures included the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM), Subjective Time, Self, and Space Scales (STSS), Perceived Body Boundaries Scale (PBBS), and the Tellegen Absorption Scale (TAS). Results revealed that guided meditation significantly reduced arousal and time-related thoughts compared to both the control and Ganzfeld conditions, indicating decreased mind wandering and enhanced relaxation. Correlational analyses demonstrated that positive affect and low arousal were associated with fewer time-related thoughts and faster perceived time in the control condition. The Ganzfeld condition linked reduced body boundary salience to positive emotional states as well as positive affect with a faster passage of time. In the meditation condition lower arousal was associated with reduced sense of body boundaries. Regression analyses further indicated that higher trait absorption predicted diminished body boundary salience in both the control and Ganzfeld conditions, suggesting that individual differences in absorptive capacity modulate susceptibility to ASC-like experiences. Overall, the findings highlight both shared and distinct effects of REST variants on experiences and underscore the importance of personality traits in shaping subjective responses to reduced environmental stimulation.

articles.title: Prediction and prediction error in autism: A meta-analysis of functional magnetic resonance imaging results
articles.authors: Hjalmar Nobel Norrman; Yating Huang; Annelies van't Westeinde; Tessa Marije van Leeuwen; Peter Fransson; Sven Bölte; Janina Neufeld
articles.abstract:
BACKGROUND: In autism spectrum condition (ASC), altered predictions (priors) or prediction errors have been hypothesized to increase the influence of bottom-up sensory input, relative to top-down prior knowledge. Such alterations could account for several autistic features, but their empirical basis is unclear. In neurotypical individuals (NT), multiple neuroimaging meta-analyses have aimed to outline domain-general ‘prediction networks’ of the brain. However, there has not been a similar meta-analysis in autism.
METHODS: We searched the literature for functional magnetic resonance imaging and magnetoencephalography studies with autistic participants. The contrasts that explicitly or implicitly involved the processing of priors/predictions and prediction errors were selected. Contrasts were divided into those that reported stronger activation in the ASC group compared to the NT group, and vice versa. We then tested the convergence of differences between the groups in two activation likelihood estimation meta-analyses.
RESULTS: Activation differences in eight contrasts reporting stronger activation in ASC compared to NT (139 ASC; 150 NT) did not result in significant convergence. Activation differences in 13 contrasts reporting stronger activation in NT compared to ASC (261 ASC; 289 NT) converged in a cluster in the medial frontal gyrus/cingulate gyrus. Additionally, we identified 37 contrasts without significant group differences.
CONCLUSION: We found converging activation differences (from contrasts reporting stronger activation in NT compared to ASC) in an area associated with error monitoring and uncertainty estimation. Our results are generally consistent with notions of altered priors/predictions or prediction errors in autism, pointing to differences at high levels of the information-processing hierarchy. However, we recommend a cautious interpretation, given the limited number of available contrasts and the high proportion of null results.

articles.title: Arousal does not enhance the dominant spatial scope of attention
articles.authors: Martin Kolnes; Sander Nieuwenhuis
articles.abstract: A fundamental aspect of attention is its spatial scope: whether attention is narrowly focused or more broadly distributed across the visual field. Here, we examined whether the spatial scope of attention is modulated by tonic arousal level. Based on findings that arousal amplifies the selectivity of attention and memory, we hypothesized that arousal would enhance the currently dominant spatial scope of attention (narrow or broad). Participants (N = 39) performed a visual search task designed to measure the breadth of attention. Auditory white noise was presented in half of the blocks to increase tonic arousal. Each trial started with a procedure that aimed to induce a narrow or broad breadth of attention. Pupil size measurements and visual search task performance revealed that the manipulations of arousal and breadth of attention were successful. However, we found strong Bayesian evidence against an effect of arousal on attentional scope. We discuss the implications of this finding for arousal-biased competition theory.

articles.title: When do bad apples spoil the barrel? Multiple Actors Matter More Than Repeated Exposure for Social Learning
articles.authors: Ansgar D Endress
articles.abstract: Prior research suggests that mere repeated exposure to moral transgressions can shift moral norms (e.g., Effron, 2022; Pillai, Fazio, & Effron, 2023), implying that repeated violations by a single, prominent actor can erode moral norms. Here, I show that moral judgments depend more on how many different individuals engage in a behavior than on mere repeated exposure. Participants viewed stories about an observer witnessing repeated, morally relevant behaviors that were performed once by six different individuals, six times by the same individual, or once by a single individual. Participants rated each behavior on third-party acceptability (acceptability for the actors) and first-party acceptability (acceptability for themselves), and prevalence. Repeated behaviors increased third-party acceptability and prevalence, but more so for multiple-actor than repeated single-actor behaviors. Critically, only multi-actor behaviors, but not single-actor repeated behaviors, influenced participants’ own moral judgments. Moral norms thus shift primarily when transgressions are performed by multiple individuals.

articles.title: Detection of Prior Conscious Observation With Subliminal Priming: Exploratory Evidence Across Alternative Stimulus Designs and a Pooled Analysis of Six Experiments
articles.authors: Richard Lucido
articles.abstract: This article reports two new experiments designed to test the Consciousness Causes Collapse (CCC) interpretation of quantum mechanics via a subliminal priming paradigm. A radioactive source was used to determine stimulus primes.  Reaction times were compared across two conditions: one in which primes were previously observed by a conscious agent and another in which they remained shielded from observation.   The CCC model predicts that unobserved primes will persist in superposition, thereby diminishing their subsequent priming effect.  These new data collections were conducted as exploratory extensions to test alternative stimulus designs: an "X vs. blank" format and a "parallel vs. perpendicular" design. Both configurations demonstrated a descriptive attenuation of the priming effect in the unobserved condition (a 59% reduction for X vs blank; a 40% reduction for parallel vs perpendicular), with the effect reaching statistical significance in the latter design. Finally, the two new datasets were combined with four previously published experiments in a pooled analysis across six experiments.  This analysis revealed a significant interaction: observed primes were associated with larger priming effects than unobserved primes (p = 1.9 × 10⁻6).  This pattern is consistent with predictions derived from the CCC interpretation of quantum mechanics, although independent replication is required to determine robustness and generalizability.

articles.title: Zero-shot learning of naturalistic concepts via conceptual systems alignment
articles.authors: Marco Ciapparelli; Marco Marelli; Sharon L. Thompson-Schill
articles.abstract: Words that are used similarly tend to point to referents that share perceptual features. Because humans are sensitive to these second-order similarities or systems alignments, they might be able to leverage representations available in one system (how a concept is talked about) to infer those lacking in another (how it might look like). Testing this form of zero-shot learning in humans is, however, tricky: concepts that are truly novel not only are unseen, but also lack words to express them. Here we rely on compounding, a productive word formation process, to define concepts that, while novel, can be interpreted on the fly by combining their familiar constituents (e.g., “tiger collar”). We generate synthetic images depicting different interpretations of the same ambiguous compound (e.g., “striped collar”, “collar for tigers”) and different instances of the same interpretation. We show that patterns of word usage and combination, coupled with simple models of language-to-vision mapping, can predict which interpretations are preferred and the speed of their selection, but fall short of accounting for finer-grained visual preferences.

articles.title: Strategic Morality
articles.authors: Jordan W Moon; Adam B. Cohen; Michael E. W. Varnum; Kurt Gray; Constantine Sedikides
articles.abstract: Moral psychology has long assumed that morality is a cooperative phenomenon that suppresses self-interest in the service of group welfare. But do people also use morality in self-interested ways? We argue that, although moral judgments must rely on cooperative principles, they are not always driven by impartial concern for cooperative welfare. Rather, people routinely invoke these same principles to advance their own interests, even as they genuinely experience those positions as principled. We refer to this tendency to deploy cooperative moral principles in the service of self-interest as strategic morality. We present a model of strategic morality, considering how distinct moral arrangements afford varying opportunities to different perceivers, and how perceptions of these affordances calibrate individuals’ moral positions in ways that serve their interests. We review evidence for our model across multiple domains of moral judgment, including mating strategies, economic interests, vulnerabilities, and kin interests, and examine how preferences for others’ moral character are similarly calibrated to self-interest. Finally, we consider how strategic morality complements rather than competes with existing approaches in moral psychology. Understanding morality as both a social ideal and a strategic instrument generates new hypotheses about how moral thought varies across individuals, cultures, and ecologies.

articles.title: A Stochastic Block Prior for Clustering in Graphical Models
articles.authors: Nikola Sekulovski; Giuseppe Arena; Jonas M B Haslbeck; Karoline Huth; Nial Friel; Maarten Marsman
articles.abstract: Graphical models facilitate the representation of psychological variables as complex systems of interacting variables structured as a network. However, their current statistical estimation procedures overlook the assumption of clustering, which refers to the grouping of subsets of variables that are more densely connected within the network, despite this assumption playing a central role in many psychological theories. We address this gap by proposing the use of the Stochastic Block Model (SBM) as a prior distribution on the network structure of a graphical model for binary and ordinal data. The SBM assumes that variables belong to latent clusters, where the probability of an edge depends on the cluster membership of the nodes. Embedding this prior in a Bayesian graphical modeling framework allows researchers to formally incorporate theoretical expectations about clustering, test hypotheses about the number of clusters, and estimate cluster assignments from cross-sectional data. We demonstrate the benefits of this approach in a simulation study and reanalyze 30 openly available empirical datasets to test for clustering. This work highlights how the Bayesian framework can embed theoretical assumptions into network models via priors and introduces a new tool for latent cluster inference in psychological network analysis.

articles.title: An intraclass correlation coefficient is a correlation: A tutorial
articles.authors: Tzu-Yao Lin; francis tuerlinckx; Sophie Vanbelle
articles.abstract: The intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) is widely used to assess reliability, agreement, and consistency for quantitative measurements. Despite being termed a ``correlation'', its definition and interpretation can be ambiguous because the ICC is commonly computed using variance components, a procedure that may obscure its conceptual foundations as a measure of correlation. In this tutorial paper, we aim to clarify the conceptual foundations of the ICC and its relationship to correlation by establishing a consistent way of conceptualizing ICCs through simulations. We illustrate various forms of ICCs under the one-way nested, two-way crossed, and three-level fully nested random-effects models, and show how they can be interpreted as correlations between two hypothetical replicates of the measurement process. To make this concept intuitive, we introduce a visual grammar that maps out the data-generating mechanisms. We then use Monte Carlo simulations to empirically verify that this correlation approach perfectly converges with variance-based ICC formulas. Ultimately, this tutorial provides researchers with an intuitive conceptual compass to seamlessly understand, select, and interpret appropriate ICCs across diverse experimental designs.

articles.title: Exploring diagnosis of Developmental Coordination Disorder and  Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function: a secondary data analysis using Connected Bradford
articles.authors: Lucy H Eddy; Matthew Warburton; Cara E Staniforth; Megan L Wood; Liam Hill; Mark Mon-Williams
articles.abstract: Background: Approximately 5% of children are affected by a neurodevelopmental disorder of their sensorimotor skills. DSM-V and ICD-10, the two most widely used classifications systems, define this diagnostically as either ‘Developmental Coordination Disorder’ (DCD) or ‘Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function’ (SDDMF, respectively). Being diagnosed with DCD/SDDMF has been found to have a detrimental impact on long-term health and education outcomes. Research repeatedly highlights parental dissatisfaction with the pathways for assessment and support of DCD/SDDMF, however, there has been a lack of research quantitatively exploring the diagnostic landscape. This study therefore aimed to evaluate the incidence of DCD/SDDMF in a diverse District (Bradford, UK) from 1950 -December 2024. Methods: Clinical codes related to sensorimotor skill assessments and diagnoses were searched for within the Connected Bradford dataset, which houses routinely collected primary and secondary healthcare data for over 800,000 residents. Results: There are 182,978 children currently in the Connected Bradford primary care records, suggesting there should be ~9149 children with a diagnosis (given estimated prevalence rates of 5%). But over 74 years of records available, there were only 152 recorded childhood diagnoses of SDDMF (the ICD-10 used in the UK and Europe for diagnosis). When exploring related diagnoses and findings there were 1037 cases (~14 per year), however this amount over 74 years is still nine times lower than would be expected within current childhood populations in Bradford. Moreover, there were low levels of recorded assessments and a lack of clarity regarding how children are being assessed. Conclusion: Diagnosis is the current pathway to accessing support across UK services, yet the current study highlights that children with DCD/SDDMF are not being diagnosed within Bradford. This likely reflects national and international challenges. Further research is needed to investigate the barriers and facilitators to assessing, diagnosing, and supporting children with DCD/SDDMF.

articles.title: Tonic Pupil-Linked Arousal Modulates Global Response Efficiency but Not Spatial Orienting
articles.authors: Ana-Maria Barosan; Simon van Gaal; Shriya Kashyap; Jakub Jurák; Timo Stein
articles.abstract: Arousal fluctuations shape behavioural performance, with optimal performance often observed at intermediate arousal levels, but it remains unclear whether these effects primarily arise during stimulus encoding, attentional orienting, decision formation, or motor execution. Here, we asked whether arousal modulates spatial orienting specifically, or instead affects broader components of task performance. Across four exogenous and one endogenous Posner cueing experiments, we tested whether spontaneous fluctuations in tonic arousal, indexed by pre-stimulus pupil size, modulate spatial orienting. Overall response speed followed a robust U-shaped relationship with pupil-linked arousal: responses were fastest at intermediate pupil sizes and slower at both smaller and larger pupil sizes. In contrast, spatial cueing effects were not consistently modulated by pupil-linked arousal. Together, these findings reveal a dissociation between arousal-related changes in response speed and spatial orienting. More broadly, these results suggest that tonic arousal can modulate task performance without necessarily altering selective attention.

articles.title: Mastering the Moment: How Target Distance, Target Speed and Additional Load Shapes Affordances for Interceptive Actions in Footballing Scenarios
articles.authors: John Oyewole; Wiktoria Moskal; Hatty Jones; Helen Nuttall; Christopher Gaffney; Trevor J. Crawford; Sally Linkenauger
articles.abstract: To perform well, athletes must perceive both individual and social affordances within sporting environments. Interceptive action is often crucial to sporting success, yet research investigating interceptive affordance perception in open-play competitive sporting environments is extremely limited. This novel research investigates how dynamic factors such as target distance, target speed, and additional load influence affordance perception for interceptive actions against opponents. Across three pre-registered studies, university level footballers (n = 26) made action-based judgments on whether they could successfully intercept a target moving perpendicularly towards the space between themselves and an opponent. The position of the target systematically varied in its proximity to the participant versus the opponent. Participants consistently overestimated their interceptive ability, persisting with manipulations to target speed, and additional load applied to players and opponents. However, exploratory mixed-effects modelling revealed that participants successfully updated their interceptive affordances with increases to relative target distance, and additional load. Participants were less sensitive to increases in target speed. These findings have wide ranging implications across many sporting and non-sporting contexts, providing new insights to understanding affordance perception in competitive sporting situations and other fast-paced environments. Additionally, this work has practical applications to coaching, highlighting opportunities to enhance interceptive decision-making during matches.

articles.title: Relative Taste Contributions in Sweet-Sour-Salty Mixtures Are Influenced by Concentration and Taste Interactions
articles.authors: Dilara Böke; Laura Pozzan; Sonja A. Kotz; Lars Hausfeld
articles.abstract:
Interactions among the basic tastes shape taste perception, yet the relative contributions of individual tastes within mixtures remain unclear. This study examined how varying concentrations of sweetness, sourness, and saltiness influence perceived intensity, pleasantness, and each taste’s relative contribution to mixture perception. To assess these relationships, participants rated solutions using a Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and a novel method, the Taste Triangle.
Participants (N = 25) completed two experimental sessions. In the first session, sensitivity to sweetness, sourness, and saltiness was assessed. In the second session, participants received individualized taste solutions containing one, two, or all three tastants. 
For overall intensity (VAS ratings), increasing individual taste concentrations generally increased perceived intensity. However, when two tastes were present at high concentrations, these increases became less pronounced, indicating perceived intensity compression in complex mixtures. For relative taste contribution (Taste Triangle ratings), increasing the concentration of a taste shifted mixture perception toward that taste and reduced the contribution of the other tastes. Furthermore, the influence of sweetness and sourness on mixture perception depended on the saltiness concentration, suggesting that saltiness modulated the contribution of the other tastes. Notably, adding a low level of sweetness altered the perceived composition of mixtures, whereas adding a low level of sourness or saltiness had little effect.
Overall, these findings demonstrate that overall intensity and relative taste contribution capture distinct aspects of taste perception in complex mixtures. Together, VAS ratings and the Taste Triangle revealed how concentration influences perceived intensity and how tastant interactions shape the relative taste contributions, even at low concentrations.

articles.title: Faith and the Other. Revisiting the Transcendence of the Other in Psychology
articles.authors: Heinz Streib
articles.abstract: This article is an exercise in reconsidering ‘faith’ and ‘transcendence’ in psychology without sacrificing philosophical precision. In contrast to a widespread, thoughtless equation of ‘faith’ and ‘religion,’ we revisit the following questions: What is faith? Is faith different from religion? In what ways? What does it mean defining faith as “ultimate concern”—as some psychologists do? Answers are inspired by the philosophies of Paul Tillich and Emmanuel Levinas. Tillich’s philosophy of religion suggests a precise distinction of ‘faith’ from ‘religion’ and reveals that faith as “ultimate concern” is a highly critical concept that, exactly by pointing beyond the finite and concrete to the Ultimate, entails a specific, radically semiotic understanding of transcendence. This opens up very critical perspectives on the danger of idolatry in society and politics. Levinas emphasizes that ethics is grounded in the primacy of the Other, the transcendence one encounters in the face of the Other, and in the diachrony of the Good. Taken together, the insights from Tillich and Levinas suggest conceptualizing ‘faith’ as “ultimate concern” by which we are “grasped and turned to the infinite” (Tillich) and respond to the experience of transcendence that speaks to us in the face of the Other (Levinas). How can psychology operationalize such experiences of transcendence of the Other? While I argue that the model of faith styles, which is based on James Fowler’s theory of faith development, can be regarded as a beginning, the article concludes with the suggestion of a research program with focus on xenosophia.

articles.title: Nature in Culture: A dimensional perspective on the positive and negative effects  of visual cultural products on nature communication
articles.authors: Massimo De Acetis; Sjoerd Ebisch
articles.abstract: Cultural products share information from which people can derive knowledge. From this knowledge, a common vision can emerge to achieve a goal. Information shared by cultural products can concern topics related to the natural world and, benefitting from scientific knowledge, could contribute to the achievement of the so desired sustainable development. However, information about nature in cultural products may also have negative effects and accentuate the psychological distance from environmental issues. This article aims to review and discuss studies that highlight both the positive and negative effects of nature-related communications in the cultural entertainment products of visual mass media. The focus is on visual media because they have the potential to effectively and intuitively communicate complex concepts such as environmental issues. We essentially point out the negative psychological effects related to the representations of nature disseminated by such products, and propose a classification of these effects based on the Model of the undesirable effects of communication campaigns. Our results highlight a categorization of a wide range of negative psychological effects, of social and personal character, that can be generated by cultural products that aim to raise awareness of environmental problems. The present review provides a new dimensional perspective that can be used, on the one hand, by researchers of nature representations to frame the phenomenon and, on the other hand, by producers of visual cultural products to avoid the inclusion of involuntary messages with potentially negative effects.

articles.title: Structural priming as a model for testing language change in bilingualism‎
articles.authors: Giuseppe Dario Benigno; Pedro Guijarro Fuentes; Estela García Alcáraz; Marta Rivera
articles.abstract: This study examined the interplay between cross-linguistic influence, structural priming, and language variation. Specifically, it investigated whether Spanish–Majorcan Catalan bilinguals process expressed and unexpressed object constructions similarly, and whether they show evidence of cross-linguistic influence and synchronic change arising from sustained contact between the two languages. While previous research has examined null-object representations in monolinguals, much less is known about how bilinguals of languages that (dis)allow object omission, process such constructions. To address this gap, forty-three Spanish–Majorcan Catalan bilinguals with varying dominance profiles completed a comprehension-based structural priming task in Spanish. The task included both grammatical and ungrammatical Spanish sentences, the latter incorporating constructions modeled on the prescriptive Catalan partitive clitic en to introduce Catalan-influenced syntax into Spanish. Results revealed differential priming effects for expressed vs. unexpressed objects. Priming effects with ungrammatical constructions further supported an implicit learning account of structural priming. Finally, although not mediated by priming, cross-linguistic influence from Catalan to Spanish indicated ongoing synchronic change in the bilingual grammar of this population. This study underscores the role of individual linguistic experience in shaping bilingual syntactic processing and highlights the need for more experience-sensitive approaches to understanding how contact-induced grammatical convergence develops in bilingual speakers.

articles.title: Can fluoxetine’s diminished efficacy in pediatric depression be explained by study sites, baseline severity, age, and psychological interventions? A secondary exploratory meta-regression.
articles.authors: Martin Plöderl; Richard Lyus; Florian Naudet
articles.abstract:
Background: Fluoxetine’s efficacy estimates in pediatric depression trials have declined to the range of placebo equivalence. We investigated whether changes in trial characteristics (number of study sites (NOSS), age, baseline severity, or added psychological interventions) could explain this trend.
Methods: We conducted a secondary exploratory analysis based on our recent meta-analyses of pediatric fluoxetine depression trials. The outcome was symptom change on the Children’s Depression Rating Scale Revised (CDRS-R). We used meta-regression to explore the association between NOSS, age, depression severity, and the addition of psychological interventions with efficacy and placebo response.
Results: NOSS increased over time and was not significantly associated with outcomes. When log-transformed, NOSS was associated with decreased efficacy (B = 1.57, 95% CI 0.24 to 2.90, p = 0.02) and greater placebo response (B = -1.90, 95% CI -3.41 to -0.40, p = 0.01). However, these associations were driven by an outlying single-center trial rated as high risk of bias and did not survive p-value adjustment. Age, baseline severity, and psychological interventions were not significantly associated with efficacy or placebo response. 
Conclusion: NOSS increased over time but the apparent associations with decreased efficacy or increased placebo response were driven by an outlying trial at risk of bias. Age of participants, baseline severity, or added psychological interventions also failed to explain fluoxetine’s diminished efficacy. Limitations include the potential ecological fallacy in analysis of age and baseline severity. Overall, these findings do not support the assumptions that fluoxetine’s meta-analytic efficacy estimates are substantially influenced by these trial characteristics.

articles.title: Bringing together health and education with lived experience to co-produce a new pathway of assessment and support for Developmental Coordination Disorder/ Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function: a Double Diamond approach
articles.authors: Lucy H Eddy; Cara E Staniforth; Maryam I. Shuaib; Nell Schofield; Nat K. Merrick; Jade Jukes; R E Murray
articles.abstract: Background: Research has shown high levels of under-diagnosis of Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD). National and international research has shown that pathways for assessment and support for DCD are convoluted, difficult to navigate and are ultimately not fit for purpose. This research therefore aimed to bring together all stakeholders involved in supporting children with motor skill difficulties to co-produce a new pathway. Methods: 13 participants attended a half day workshop (January 2025) spanning lived experience (adult and young person), paediatrics, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, school leadership, special educational needs teaching, school nursing, physical needs specialists and reducing inequalities management. A Double Diamond approach was implemented in which participants were taken through four phases cycling through divergence (big picture thinking) and convergence (consensus). Discussions were recorded and transcribed for thematic analysis. After the workshop, participants completed an online questionnaire to understand (i) acceptability; (ii) potential barriers; and (iii) modifications. Results: consensus was reached on a new pathway for identifying and supporting children with DCD, and those with sensorimotor difficulties that do not meet thresholds for diagnosis. Participants highlighted the need for mobilisation of key leaders in health and education as well as the development of evidence-based training to facilitate a new pathway. The new pathway includes two tiers – universal provision (school and community) and targeted provision for children that would benefit from specialist clinical support and diagnosis. Conclusion: A Double Diamond approach to co-production was effective for re-designing the DCD pathway. More research is needed to understand the feasibility and efficacy of the new pathway in practice.

articles.title: Feature-Based and Spatial Involuntary Internal Attention Differentially Shape Action-Oriented Working Memory Selection
articles.authors: Águeda Fuentes-Guerra; Fabiano Botta; Juan Lupiáñez; Carlos González-García; Elisa Martín-Arévalo
articles.abstract: While attention is known to prioritize stimuli held temporally in our working memory (WM), it remains poorly understood how different features (and primarily, spatial vs. non-spatial) involuntarily select complex WM contents to guide action. Building on the Binding and Retrieval in Action Control (BRAC) framework and the Theory of Event Coding (TEC), we hypothesized that any encoded feature should be capable of reactivating an Stimulus-Response (S-R) event file in WM. Across six experiments (N=199), divided into two Experimental Series—in which we systematically manipulated timing, perceptual salience, and attentional engagement—we pre-registered and tested the hypothesis that non-predictive, color-based retro-cues could involuntarily select and prioritize complex WM events associated with S-R bindings. Contrary to our expectations, we found no significant involuntary (facilitatory) effect of color retro-cues and observed moderate meta-analytic evidence against such an effect. This comprehensive lack of an effect stands in stark contrast to meta-analytic evidence from previous studies using the exact same design but with spatial retro-cues, which demonstrated a small but robust effect. This provides compelling evidence that not all retro-cues can interact with WM representations with the same efficacy, challenging the assumption that all features hold equal status within an event representation. Instead, our findings suggest a critical bias in involuntary WM selection: color-based retro-cues fail to involuntarily select complex WM contents containing integrated S-R associations, while spatial retro-cues do. This distinction implies that the functional relevance of a perceptual feature to action planning is the critical determinant for its ability to trigger the prioritization of complex internal representations, upholding the essential link between action and memory in adaptive behavior.

articles.title: The Intellectual Justice Scale: Measuring How Fairly People Engage With Knowledge
articles.authors: Yara Daamen; Vlasta Sikimić; Daniel Lakens
articles.abstract: Intellectual justice, the disposition to engage fairly with knowledge and those who produce it, has received growing attention in philosophy and education, yet no validated self-report measure exists. We developed and validated the Intellectual Justice Scale (IJS), a 9-item measure with three factors (Testimonial Justice, Hermeneutical Justice, and Intellectual Action) grounded in Fricker’s framework of intellectual injustice. An initial item pool was refined through expert review and pilot testing. In Study 1 (N = 404), exploratory factor analysis supported a three-factor structure, with independent statistical and conceptual item selection converging on the same nine items. In Study 2 (N = 401), confirmatory factor analysis supported good model fit, and the IJS showed expected associations with intellectual humility, personality traits, and social justice attitudes while remaining empirically distinguishable from each. These findings offer the first validated self-report measure of intellectual justice attitudes, providing researchers with a tool to study individual differences.

articles.title: Forensic Outpatient Cognitive Understanding Study (FOCUS): A study protocol
articles.authors: Juliette Hutten; Hilde Geurts; Tim Ziermans; joan van horn
articles.abstract:
Background: There is growing evidence that cognitive dysfunction plays a role in the emergence and persistence of antisocial behavior, and it is increasingly implicated as relevant for risk assessment and treatment within the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) framework. However, this evidence comes predominantly from prison and inpatient populations. Whether similar patterns hold in forensic outpatient settings remains largely unknown. Moreover, existing studies often rely on normative groups that are not matched for age, sex, and education, and tend to focus on single cognitive domains rather than administering comprehensive test batteries.
Methods: This protocol paper describes the design and methods of the FOCUS study, which aims to map cognitive functioning in forensic outpatients and examine its associations with aggression and treatment outcomes. The study compares forensic outpatients (n = 100) to a normative group (n = 100, matched on sex, age, and educational level). All participants complete a comprehensive computerized neuropsychological test battery covering seven cognitive domains: intelligence, attention, language, executive functioning, social cognition, memory, and decision making. Aggression is assessed using both a self-report questionnaire (Reactive Proactive Questionnaire; RPQ) and a behavioral task (Competitive Reaction Time Task; CRTT). We have four research aims: (1) to compare the cognitive profile of forensic outpatients to a matched normative group; (2) to examine the correspondence between self-reported and task-based aggression; (3) to examine associations between neuropsychological test scores and aggression; and (4) to examine associations between neuropsychological test scores and treatment outcomes, including recidivism risk, treatment non-attendance, dropout, and actual recidivism. 
Discussion: The FOCUS study is the first to administer a comprehensive neuropsychological test battery in a forensic outpatient sample with a matched normative comparison group. Findings are expected to advance understanding of cognitive functioning within the RNR framework and to inform more individualized forensic treatment planning.

articles.title: Targeting coping strategies, emotion regulation, and self-esteem in children with dyslexia to support mental health: A randomised-controlled trial of Clever Kids
articles.authors: Mark Edward Boyes; Kirsten Melville; Mandy Nayton; Hannah Pizzey; Suze Leitao
articles.abstract: Background: Children with dyslexia are at elevated risk of mental health difficulties, both internalising (emotional) and externalising (behavioural) problems. Clever Kids is a nine-week socio-emotional wellbeing programme developed specifically to support upper primary school children with dyslexia. Aims: We conducted a pre-registered randomised-controlled trial to test the efficacy of Clever Kids. Method: Seventy-eight children (Mage = 10.19 years, 54% female) with clinically diagnosed dyslexia took part in the trial. Children were randomised to either attend Clever Kids (n = 38) or to a wait-list control condition (n = 40). Coping skills, emotion regulation, self-esteem, perseverance, assertiveness, as well as mental health (internalising and externalising symptoms) were measured at pre-programme, post-programme, and at three-month follow-up using well-validated measures. Results: The predicted interactions between intervention condition and time were significant for six outcomes. Attending Clever Kids was associated with significant improvements in child-reported coping, emotion regulation, academic and social self-esteem, as well as parent-reported anxiety and depression. Effects were all maintained at three-month follow-up. Unexpectedly, being assigned to the wait-list condition was associated with improvements in perseverance at follow-up assessment. For all other outcomes, interactions between intervention condition and time were non-significant. Conclusion: Clever Kids appears a promising programme for supporting socio-emotional wellbeing of children with dyslexia. Future research should consider incorporating caregivers more explicitly into the programme and alternative delivery modes, which could expand the reach of the programme.

articles.title: The Topology of Grammar: Hierarchical Inversion, Numerical Magnitude, and a Shared Attentional Resource for Syntactic and Numerical Indexing A Registered Report
articles.authors: Rakesh Sengupta; Sayantan Mandal
articles.abstract:
\citet{greenberg1963} documented a robust cross-linguistic preference for the
nominal order Dem $>$ Num $>$ Adj $>$ N, and \citet{cinque2005} derived the
attested orders from a universal functional hierarchy together with principled
phrasal movement. We ask why violations of this hierarchy should be costly to
\emph{process} even where a speaker's grammar permits them, and propose that
the answer is a third-factor cognitive constraint
\citep{chomsky2005}: long-distance dependency resolution and attentive numerical
enumeration draw on a common, bounded resource of top-down indexing
\citep{tsotsos2011,pylyshyn1989,burr2010}. If so, the cost of an order that
linearly inverts the functional hierarchy should grow \emph{super-additively}
with the magnitude of an accompanying numeral. We will test this with a
moving-window self-paced reading study in English and in Bengali. Bengali is
critical because its grammatical reduplicated-adjective construction lets us
dissociate the distributive/pluractional cost of \emph{individuation} from the
cost of hierarchical \emph{inversion} proper. The diagnostic prediction is a
positive inversion-by-magnitude interaction term ($\delta>0$) that is moderated
by individual Multiple-Object-Tracking capacity but not by verbal working
memory. We pre-register the cost model, the confirmatory tests, the
outcome-neutral quality checks, and a simulation-based power analysis. All
materials are implemented as a single-file \texttt{jsPsych}~8 experiment,
publicly deployed and version-controlled. This is a Stage 1 submission; all
methods and analyses are described in the future tense.

articles.title: Forbidden Causes: Science as a Restrictive Cognitive System
articles.authors: Ze Hong
articles.abstract: Most of what people in modern societies believe about the world is acquired through deference to scientific institutions rather than through independent verification. This paper asks what such institutions transmit to the lay public beyond particular findings, and argues that they instill a restrictive sense of what kinds of things can exist and what kinds of explanation can be taken seriously. I specify this restrictive sense as four admissibility constraints that narrow the range of candidate explanations before any evidence is considered: explanations should be impersonal, physically mediated, nomologically uniform, and sparsely connected. Rather than identifying which explanations are false, these constraints filter out whole classes of explanation in advance, marking them as the wrong kind of thing to be true. They are most fully entrenched among the scientifically acculturated and operate more unevenly in the general population. I show that the constraints are historically contingent rather than essential to science, since early modern inquiry was considerably more permissive about what might exist. I illustrate their operation through the reception of Newtonian gravitation and the rhetoric of contemporary pseudoscience, and draw out the implications for science education.

articles.title: The Other-Race Effect in Implicit Memory for Face Combinations
articles.authors: Yoko Higuchi; Kiwako Shimada
