articles.abstract: Other-race faces are remembered less accurately than own-race faces, a robust phenomenon known as the other-race effect or cross-race effect. However, most evidence for the other-race effect comes from tasks that require explicit encoding or recognition of individual face identities, leaving unclear whether the effect is confined to explicit memory demands. Here, we report evidence for an other-race effect not only in explicit memory, but also in implicit memory for repeated face combinations. In Experiment 1, Japanese participants intentionally encoded Asian and Caucasian faces and later performed an explicit recognition task; recognition sensitivity was higher for own-race Asian faces than for other-race Caucasian faces. In Experiment 2, Japanese participants performed an implicit memory task in which repeated face combinations were presented without awareness of the repetitions; implicit memory for repeated face combinations developed more reliably for own-race Asian faces than for other-race Caucasian faces. Taken together, these results suggest that the cognitive difficulties associated with other-race faces should be understood not only as a problem of explicit face memory, but also as a problem of implicit memory formed through repeated face exposure in everyday life.

articles.title: People Choose to Read Fact-Checks, But Assume Others Don’t
articles.authors: Jae Kwon; Nadia M Brashier
articles.abstract: Presenting people with third-party fact-checks reliably reduces belief in falsehoods. But in the real world, people can ignore corrective messages they are not interested in, and they might misremember these choices later. In three experiments (total N = 760), participants in the U.S. read true and false news headlines with the option to see corresponding fact-checks. Later they reported how many fact-checks they viewed and judged how their engagement compared to the average participant. On average, participants chose to read fact-checks for 79% of headlines even though they had no incentive to do so (Experiments 1 and 2). Engagement was high for both Democrats and Republicans, and regardless of whether or not news stories aligned with participants’ politics. Even when headlines appeared with ‘confirmed’ and ‘disputed’ labels, participants chose to read additional context about 59% of them (Experiment 3). Crucially, people also exhibited a better-than-average effect, rating their own engagement as higher than that of the average participant. These data counter the assumption that demand for fact-checks is low and suggest that people underestimate others’ interest in corrections of political news. Our findings inform theories of belief revision and have practical implications for the future of fact-checking on social media platforms.

articles.title: Regulation Without Regulating? Exploring Misalignment Between Self-Reported Emotion Regulation Attempts and Strategy Use
articles.authors: Aya Uchida; Katharine Helen Greenaway; Valentina Bianchi; Maya Tamir; Elise Katherine Kalokerinos
articles.abstract: Theory suggests that in the process of emotion regulation, people form an intention to regulate, and then act on this intention. Although theory posits these are distinct yet sequentially linked stages, most research focuses on each stage in isolation. We addressed this gap in the literature by exploring how, and to what degree, self-reported attempts to regulate emotions are linked with self-reported regulatory behaviors in the form of commonly studied emotion regulation strategies. Across three experience sampling studies (Ntotal=683), we empirically tested the link between emotion regulation attempts and behaviors. We were particularly interested in occasions when these stages are disconnected: when people report making no attempt to regulate emotion, and yet report engaging in an emotion regulation strategy. We term this phenomenon emotion regulation misalignment and found it occurred in all three studies on 13-42% of occasions. We also explored whether misalignment was more or less prevalent for certain strategies and found that, although misalignment occurred for all strategies, it was most likely for suppression and social sharing, and least likely for reappraisal and distraction. In all, we provide evidence to suggest that behaviors intended to capture emotion regulation strategies can occur independent of intentional attempts at emotion regulation. This work has implications for how researchers measure emotion regulation and emotion regulation strategies, and how we theorize about what emotion regulation is.

articles.title: Evidence that positive outcomes elicit a stronger sense of agency is not robust: a commentary on, and re-analysis of, Mariano et al. (2025)
articles.authors: Robrecht van der Wel; Shaheed Azaad; Pernille Hemmer; John Michael
articles.abstract: In a recent meta-analysis, Mariano et al. (2025) conclude that people experience a stronger sense of agency over positive than negative outcomes, indexed by temporal compression. In this commentary, we evaluate that claim and identify several concerns about its robustness. First, we were unable to reproduce their main valence effect: our reconstruction of the three-level meta-analytic model yielded a smaller, non-significant difference. Second, we identified data integrity issues, including implausibly small standard deviations that appear to reflect computational errors. Third, we argue that temporal compression effects should be standardized before being combined, because values in milliseconds are not necessarily comparable across different paradigms. When we analyzed standardized effects, evidence for a valence effect was weak, and absent once adjusting for publication bias. Overall, our re-analysis suggests that stronger agency for positive outcomes is not yet robustly supported.

articles.title: From Thought to Senses: Assessing the Relationship Between the Generation Effect and Multisensory Facilitation
articles.authors: Michaela Ritchie; Jonathan Wilbiks
articles.abstract: The current study investigated whether the generation effect, a memory advantage for self-generated verbal information, is enhanced in multisensory conditions. Such a finding would be consistent with the multisensory facilitation effect, a phenomenon wherein multiple sensory inputs may reduce the cognitive load required to process and respond to co-occurring stimuli from multiple senses. Although extensive research has explored the generation effect, the multisensory aspect of this task has been overlooked. In the current study, we aimed to determine if the generation effect is modulated by the multisensory facilitation effect. Multisensory facilitation involves the brain’s ability to efficiently process information from diverse sensory sources simultaneously, enhancing cognitive performance. We employed a 2 (Task Type: generate, read) X 3 (Sensory Modality: auditory, visual, multisensory) factorial design, utilizing word pair lists and a cued recall test. A generation effect emerged for the auditory and audiovisual condition, but not for the visual condition. The hypothesis that the magnitude of the generation effect would be greater for the multisensory encoding condition was not supported. Overall, the hypothesis that generation effect will be enhanced for multisensory learning was not supported. The current study addresses a gap in existing literature by examining the multisensory component of generation tasks and extends the current understanding of multisensory learning by incorporating verbal stimuli as the visual component.

articles.title: Identification of mental illness in maternity settings and care pathways to mental health services during pregnancy in a diverse urban UK population
articles.authors: Sam Burton; Claire Wilson; Fiona L Challacombe; Paul Seed; Jane Sandall; Louise Howard; eLIXIR Born in South London Partnership; Abigail Easter
articles.abstract:
Background: Mental disorders affect 27% of UK women during the perinatal period (pregnancy and the first postpartum year) and are associated with increased risk of adverse maternal and child outcomes. Despite the societal burden, access to mental health services remains inconsistent, particularly for certain ethnic groups. The aim of this study was to characterise pregnant women, from an ethnically and socioeconomically diverse UK antenatal urban population, with identified risk of mental disorders in the perinatal period,  and to evaluate  the proportion in contact with mental health services We also explore potential inequity and barriers to access, as measured by referral and engagement with care.  

Methods: This study utilised linked maternity and mental health records from the eLIXIR Born in South London database, comprising 67,308 pregnancies from 2018 to 2023. These records provided information about: antenatal mental health assessments through three areas of enquiry: Whooley (depression screening) questions, 2) self-reported mental health conditions, 3) family history of severe mental illness, as well as referrals to, and engagement with mental health services during pregnancy. 

Findings: Among those in contact with mental health services, 38.39%, 24.73%, and 6.14% had been identified by one, two, or three areas of antenatal mental health enquiry, respectively. A positive relationship was observed between the number of questions endorsed and referral to and engagement with treatment. However, ethnic disparities were evident: Asian and Asian British women had lower odds of referral to mental health service (OR=0.51, 95% CI [0.42, 0.61]) and engagement (OR = 0.73, 95% CI [.59, .91]), compared to White women, while Black, African, Caribbean, and Black British women had higher odds of referral (OR=1.45, 95% CI [1.32, 1.60]), but lower odds of engagement (OR=0.73, 95% CI [0.59, 0.91]). Women requiring an interpreter had lower odds of referral (OR = 0.69, 95% CI [0.56, 0.82]).

Interpretation: The study highlights significant disparities in identification and treatment of mental disorders in the perinatal period, perinatal mental disorders. Improved referral and care pathways, and culturally sensitive assessments are indicated to redress these disparities.

articles.title: Comparação entre LLMs e avaliadores humanos no julgamento de sotaque em inglês-L2 por brasileiros
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske; Laura Zorzi
articles.abstract: Este estudo investiga se LLMs com capacidade de processamento de áudio categorizam o sotaque em inglês-L2 de maneira semelhante a avaliadores humanos. Comparam-se avaliações do Google Gemini Flash e Gemini Pro com julgamentos de avaliadores nativos de inglês norte-americano, utilizando gravações de falantes brasileiros do CSLU: Foreign Accented English Corpus (Lander, 2007). A partir de 36 enunciados avaliados em uma escala de quatro pontos, as distribuições foram comparadas por meio de modelos mistos de ligação cumulativa (CLMM), coeficientes kappa e matrizes de confusão. A análise revelou divergências sistemáticas: ambos os LLMs superestimaram o grau de sotaque, recorrendo à categoria 4/sotaque forte em proporções ausentes na linha de base humana. O Gemini Pro apresentou o maior viés (OR = 8,25), contrariando a premissa de que maior sofisticação computacional produz avaliações mais alinhadas à percepção humana. Os baixos coeficientes de concordância confirmam que a variabilidade entre avaliadores supera a convergência entre eles. Os resultados indicam que os modelos analisados parecem carecer da tolerância sociofonética que caracteriza o julgamento humano, não devendo ser utilizados como substitutos na avaliação de sotaque em L2.

articles.title: Addressing climate change skepticism and inaction using human-AI dialogues
articles.authors: Gabriela Czarnek; Reed Orchinik; Hause Lin; Henry George Xu; Thomas Costello; Gordon Pennycook; David Gertler Rand
articles.abstract: We ask whether facts and evidence - tailored by an AI model to address each person’s specific concerns - can address climate skepticism and inaction. Participants first described their main climate change reservation. The most prevalent were the belief that climate change has natural causes (15%), feeling overwhelmed by the problem (10%), and concerns about the economic consequences of climate policies (8%). Participants were then randomized to (1) have a conversation with a Large Language Model (LLM) that was given the goal of addressing their climate reservations, (2) discuss an irrelevant topic with the LLM (i.e., control), or (3) receive static information about the scientific consensus around climate change (i.e., “standard-of-care”). The LLM treatment significantly and substantially reduced participants’ conviction in their specific reservations, while consensus messaging did not. Both treatments had significant, albeit small, effects on general pro-climate beliefs/attitudes. Critically, however, the LLM treatment was significantly more effective - particularly for increasing willingness to make sacrifices to address climate change and donations to a pro-climate charity. The LLM primarily presented facts, evoked positive emotions, reduced psychological distance, and fostered motivation to act. It rarely invoked values or ingroup sources, and when it did, their use was associated with reduced belief change. The treatment substantially reduced Republicans’ reservations (although  less than for Independents or Democrats), and roughly 35% to 40% of the LLM treatment effect persisted after one month. These findings demonstrate that it is possible to reach many climate skeptical or hesitant people with the right facts and evidence.

articles.title: Emotional Composition Of Sleep-Related Out-Of-Body Experiences In Relation To Sleep Paralysis And Lucid Dreaming
articles.authors: Nerea L. Herrero; Cecilia Forcato
articles.abstract:
Sleep-related out-of-body experiences (OBEs) occupy an ambiguous position between sleep paralysis and lucid dreaming. OBEs are often considered part of the vestibular-motor component of sleep paralysis, but they also share features with lucid dreaming, including vivid internally generated experience, heightened awareness, and perceived agency. Here, we examined whether the emotional composition of sleep-related OBEs is closer to sleep paralysis, closer to lucid dreaming, or distinguishable from both.
We analysed 666 participant-by-experience-type emotional reports corresponding to OBEs, sleep paralysis, and lucid dreams. Emotional variables were coded as binary presence/absence indicators. The main multivariate analyses focused on reports containing at least one discrete emotion, yielding 658 reports: 184 OBEs, 210 sleep paralysis reports, and 264 lucid dream reports. Emotional composition was assessed using Jaccard dissimilarity, non-metric multidimensional scaling, PERMANOVA, multivariate dispersion, and emotion-by-group association analyses. Responses indicating absence of emotion were analysed separately.
Emotional composition differed significantly across OBEs, sleep paralysis, and lucid dreams. Descriptively, the OBE–lucid dream contrast showed the smallest observed pairwise effect size, whereas contrasts involving sleep paralysis showed larger observed effects. Sleep paralysis was associated with high-arousal negative emotions, particularly fear, terror, and anxiety, and showed the most homogeneous emotional profile. Lucid dreams were associated with happiness, love, hope, and sexual energy. OBEs were distinguished by peace/tranquility and, to a lesser extent, euphoria. Absence-of-emotion responses were rare and did not account for the multivariate pattern.
Sleep-related OBEs were not emotionally reducible to sleep paralysis. Their emotional profile was consistent with greater affective similarity to lucid dreams than to sleep paralysis, while remaining distinguishable from both. These findings suggest that emotional composition provides an informative axis for characterizing sleep-related altered states of consciousness.

Keywords: out-of-body experience; sleep paralysis; lucid dreaming; dream emotions; altered states of consciousness.

articles.title: Diagnosing Disagreement in Dehumanization Research
articles.authors: Alexander Landry; Ben Phillips; Katrina Fincher
articles.abstract: An expansive multidisciplinary literature uses dehumanization to explain violence, yet scholars profoundly disagree about what dehumanization is, where it operates, and how it works. The result has been a fragmented and conceptually unstable field: theoretical progress has stalled, and growing critiques question whether dehumanization offers a coherent explanation for violence at all. We argue that this fragmentation reflects, not a fatal flaw in the concept of dehumanization, but a lack of precision in how scholars interpret disagreement about it. Much of what appears to be a single conceptual morass is better understood as three fundamentally different kinds of disagreement that have been conflated with one another. Two kinds of “disagreement” are actually illusory, arising from conceptual miscommunication or misalignment, while only one reflects genuine theoretical conflict. Building on these distinctions, we introduce a framework designed not only to classify these disagreements, but to help researchers address them. It provides concrete diagnostic questions for evaluating apparent conflict in the literature and identifying the appropriate next step: When do accounts that appear to disagree address different problems, and therefore should be separated? When do they capture complementary parts of a broader phenomenon, and therefore can be integrated? When do they advance genuinely competing claims, and therefore must be adjudicated? Answering these questions reveals what is genuinely at stake in debates about dehumanization, addresses persistent concerns about its conceptual coherence, and provides a clearer foundation for cumulative and integrative theorizing about its role in violence.

articles.title: Distinguishing imagery and perception in generative models of brain function
articles.authors: Nadine Dijkstra; Stephen M Fleming
articles.abstract: Perception is thought to be a generative process, relying on prior beliefs and expectations to shape what we experience. This generative architecture also enables the internal generation of sensory activity in the absence of input, in the form of imagination. It remains unclear how imagery and perception can be distinguished within generative models of brain function. Here, we use Bayesian computational models to test several alternative hypotheses. We first show that imagery and perception cannot be separated within first-order Bayesian inference. Next, we show that a model which formalizes reality inferences as higher-order evaluations of sensory precision can separate imagery and perception and can explain a range of empirical observations about the relationship between perception, imagery, reality monitoring and aphantasia. We further generate novel hypotheses highlighting directions for future research. Our approach offers a simple yet powerful framework for developing a computational neuroscience of reality monitoring.

articles.title: Self-reports of personality functioning and depression are practically fungible. A commentary on Kerber et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Josh Miller; Colin Vize; Nathaniel Luke Phillips; Donald R Lynam
articles.abstract: Kerber and colleagues (2026) examine the prospective correlates of depression (DEP) and personality functioning (PF) in a very large sample of individuals followed via an ecological momentary assessment framework.  Using sophisticated bifactor modeling to control for the problematically large latent correlation between the two constructs (the authors report correlations of r = .75 and .87, depending on measurement approach), the authors suggest that PF and DEP have distinct affective signatures with important ramifications for how treatment is tailored. We argue in this commentary that the very strong latent correlations between PF and DEP, along with nearly identical regression weights in predicting various outcomes (profile agreement across outcomes, r = .95), indicates that there is no practical difference between these two constructs as typically measured. The authors believe the bifactor models allow them to disentangle PF and DEP but this raises the “perils of partialing.” The empirical profiles manifested by the original and residualized latent scores for PF bear no relation to one another (profile agreement, r = -.06) and 53% of their regression weights change direction when comparing the two. We also raise concerns with the viability of their bifactor models, particularly for PF. The residualized PF factor is relatively weak, changes fairly dramatically across analyses, and is unlikely to replicate across samples. Ultimately, we believe Kerber and colleagues suggestion that PF and DEP are distinct with differing treatment implications is unsupported by their data.

articles.title: Task Configuration and Encoding Duration Jointly Shape Human Performance in a Matsuzawa-Inspired Visuospatial Working Memory Task
articles.authors: Nadine Charanek; Olessia Jouravlev
articles.abstract: Rapid visuospatial serial-recall tasks place substantial demands on working memory (WM) because they require encoding item identity, spatial location, and serial order under severe time pressure. The present study examined how task configuration and encoding duration shape human performance in a Matsuzawa-style visuospatial WM task commonly used in comparative research. Two hundred eighty-eight participants completed two different task configurations, sequential build-up and simultaneous high-load configurations, in which presentation duration was systematically varied. Longer encoding durations improved recall accuracy, but the magnitude of this benefit depended strongly on task configuration. The sequential build-up configuration supported substantially greater task progression with increasing difficulty, whereas the simultaneous high-load configuration resulted in early, steep task failure. Within the sequential build-up configuration, performance declined sharply once digit load exceeded approximately five to six items. These findings suggest that performance in rapid visuospatial WM tasks depends strongly on task configuration, particularly how information is presented, bound, and retrieved under time pressure.

articles.title: Detection of Extraneous Visual Signals is Not Affected by the Syntactic Structure of German Sign Language (DGS)
articles.authors: Patrick C. Trettenbrein; Matteo Maran; Jan Pohl; Thomas A. Finkbeiner; Emiliano Zaccarella; Angela D. Friederici; Markus Steinbach; Nina-Kristin Meister
articles.abstract: Sentences are not just mere strings of words or signs, but manifest a complex internal structure. Linguistic research has demonstrated that sign languages and spoken languages both exhibit hierarchical constituent structure which determines how individual elements in a sentence relate to each other. Here, we report the first adaptation of the psycholinguistic click paradigm, which aims to demonstrate an impact of hierarchical constituent structure on cognitive processing load during auditory language processing, to the visuo-spatial modality of sign languages. We performed two independent online experiments: The main experiment with a group of 53 deaf signers using German Sign Language (DGS) as their primary means of communication and a control experiment with a group of 53 hearing non-signers. Both groups were shown videos of syntactically complex sentences in DGS. A white flash (mimicking the click in the auditory domain) to which participants had to respond could occur as an overlay to the video at different levels in the constituent structure. Our pre-registered inferential analyses yielded no effect for our syntactic manipulations, neither in the group of signers nor in the group of non-signers. Additional exploratory analyses suggest general effects of attention during the processing of communicative signals, as even the group of non-signers’ behaviour was influenced by non-manual cues (i.e., eyebrow raising), despite their lack of knowledge of DGS. We conclude that the simultaneous and time-shifted presence of different syntax-relevant cues (i.e., hands, mouthings, and non-manuals) makes the processing of the sign stream robust against disruption by extraneous visual signals and argue that non-signers attend to some non-manual cues, due to their resemblance to communicative gestures independent of language.

articles.title: Gaming Disorder:  A qualitative meta-synthesis of related case studies
articles.authors: Tiina Auranen; Matus Adamkovic; Marcel Martončik; Solip Park; Joona Ville-Valtteri Kauraoja; Yaewon Jin; Ville Malinen; Mikhail Fiadotau; NA; Anna Urbancová
articles.abstract: Several meta-analyses and reviews have been published on gaming-related health problems, mainly in the context of addictive behaviours and gaming disorder in particular. However, none of those reviews have been qualitative or focused on individual cases. The present meta-synthesis provides such evidence from 88 published case studies with 133 individual cases. Surprisingly, almost no case study evidence was found from high-prevalence countries like China and South Korea. Using deductive coding, the study identifies and synthesises a total of 845 problems and 35 comorbidities along with reasons for seeking help—most of treatment-seeking continues to happen due to social compliance. Further, we applied thematic synthesis to qualitatively organise the data into a network, represented by connected areas where a spectrum of vulnerabilities sets the individual on a high-stress life path; self-medication through gaming becomes both the cure and the problem; and prolonged gaming or critical life events lead to treatment or even hospitalization. After sensitivity analyses, primary findings generally hold throughout levels of informativeness across studies. We find many of the reported problems and their contexts aligning with coping theories of gaming disorder. Next to the above thematic areas, which characterises a large part of the published studies, we highlight the great variety of content across cases: most studies did not address, diagnose, or mention gaming disorder formally at all, but rather represented instances of life where gaming was related to health problems in other ways.

articles.title: Digitized and AI-Assisted Scoring of Cognitive Assessments in ALS and Neurodegenerative Diseases: A Scoping Review of Consistency, Reliability, and Cross-Site Applicability
articles.authors: Indu Navar Bingham; Anusha Rao; Natalia Luchkina; Sharon Abrahams; Ammar Al-Chalabi
articles.abstract:
Background. Standardized cognitive assessment across clinical sites remains a major challenge in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and related neurodegenerative diseases. The Edinburgh Cognitive and Behavioral ALS Screen (ECAS) is the most validated and widely-used ALS-specific cognitive assessment tool, yet paper-and-pencil manual scoring introduces inter-rater variability that can significantly limit reliability and reproducibility in multi-site clinical trials. Digitized administration and artificial intelligence-assisted scoring may improve consistency and scalability, but the current landscape across ALS and related diseases has not been systematically mapped.

Objective. To map existing evidence on digitized and AI-assisted scoring of cognitive assessments in ALS and other neurodegenerative and neuroinflammatory diseases, with emphasis on inter-rater reliability and cross-site consistency.

Methods. We conducted a comprehensive scoping review following Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. Eligible studies evaluated neuropsychological or cognitive assessment tools using automated, computerized, digital, or AI-assisted scoring in ALS and related conditions, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, dementia, and neuroinflammatory disease. An initial broad PubMed search was refined to focus on records explicitly addressing automated or AI-assisted scoring in titles and abstracts. Records were loaded into Rayyan for independent blinded review by three reviewers, with discrepancies resolved through discussion until consensus was reached. Data were extracted using standardized templates.

Results. The initial search identified 1,503 records and was refined to 168 records. After duplicate removal, 166 records underwent title, abstract, and full-text screening against predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Seven primary studies met inclusion criteria: two in multiple sclerosis, two in the Alzheimer's disease continuum, and three in dementia or mild cognitive impairment. AI and digital approaches included tablet-based digitization with automated scoring, convolutional neural networks, and large language models. All seven studies reported high agreement with human or paper-based reference standards. No primary study evaluating AI-assisted scoring of the ECAS or ALS-specific cognitive screens was identified.

Conclusions. This review maps AI-assisted and digitized cognitive assessment scoring in neurodegenerative and neuroinflammatory diseases. Findings reveal a critical gap in ALS-specific cognitive tools and ECAS scoring. Methodological approaches from related diseases, especially automated scoring of verbal and written responses, may inform future development of standardized AI-assisted ECAS protocols. Such tools could improve consistency, reliability, and scalability across multi-site ALS clinical trials.

articles.title: Gaming Disorder:  A qualitative meta-synthesis of related case studies
articles.authors: Tiina Auranen; Matus Adamkovic; Marcel Martončik; Solip Park; Joona Ville-Valtteri Kauraoja; Yaewon Jin; Ville Malinen; Mikhail Fiadotau; NA; Anna Urbancová
articles.abstract: Several meta-analyses and reviews have been published on gaming-related health problems, mainly in the context of addictive behaviours and gaming disorder in particular. However, none of those reviews have been qualitative or focused on individual cases. The present meta-synthesis provides such evidence from 88 published case studies with 133 individual cases. Surprisingly, almost no case study evidence was found from high-prevalence countries like China and South Korea. Using deductive coding, the study identifies and synthesises a total of 845 problems and 35 comorbidities along with reasons for seeking help—most of treatment-seeking continues to happen due to social compliance. Further, we applied thematic synthesis to qualitatively organise the data into a network, represented by connected areas where a spectrum of vulnerabilities sets the individual on a high-stress life path; self-medication through gaming becomes both the cure and the problem; and prolonged gaming or critical life events lead to treatment or even hospitalization. After sensitivity analyses, primary findings generally hold throughout levels of informativeness across studies. We find many of the reported problems and their contexts aligning with coping theories of gaming disorder. Next to the above thematic areas, which characterises a large part of the published studies, we highlight the great variety of content across cases: most studies did not address, diagnose, or mention gaming disorder formally at all, but rather represented instances of life where gaming was related to health problems in other ways.

articles.title: A Large-Scale Multi-Site Study of Mediators and Moderators of Visual Perceptual Learning
articles.authors: Jaap Munneke; Marcello Maniglia; Boris Penaloza; C. Shawn Green; Aaron Seitz
articles.abstract:
Background: Visual Perceptual Learning (VPL), which relies on cortical plasticity, has shown great potential for clinical rehabilitation and basic science. However, translational research is held back by small-sample studies (N < 12), replication failures, and methodological heterogeneity across various labs. The current multi-site clinical trial aims to create an adequately powered, standardized dataset and testing platform for VPL, designed to investigate the mechanisms of successful PL and its generalization (i.e. transfer) to other perceptual and cognitive tasks.

Methods: This is a randomized, double-blind, parallel-group and crossover clinical trial with data collection occurring at four sites: Northeastern University (Boston); University of California, Riverside; University of Wisconsin–Madison and Mills College at Northeastern (Oakland). A large cohort of younger adults (planned N = 1080) are randomized across 44 crossover conditions, evaluating 12 distinct contrast-based training interventions (mediators), while an older adult cohort (ages 60 and up; planned N = 300) is tested at three sites (all above except Boston). Visual training and a visual and cognitive transfer battery are delivered using validated, cross-platform UNITY applications. Visual acuity, contrast sensitivity functions, complex visual search tracking, and cognitive batteries establish near/far transfer as well as individual-difference moderator profiles.

Discussion: By fully standardizing stimulus delivery, parameters, task structure and outcome assessment, using consumer-grade tablets, this trial minimizes the environmental and hardware variations that confound cross-study comparisons. Because data collection is ongoing, this protocol further outlines the specific methodological and statistical plan to map out perceptual learning and visual plasticity, as well as the impact of visual perceptual learning in adult populations.

articles.title: Introducing LexTurk: A quick lexical test for learners of Turkish
articles.authors: Lara Maite Kelly-Iturriaga; Levent Uzun; Umut Muharrem Salihoğlu; Ugur Recep Cetinavci
articles.abstract:
This manuscript is a pre-print (methodology detailing the creation and validation of LexTurk) and has not yet undergone peer review.

In the current study, we developed an objective measure of L2 Turkish lexical proficiency, LexTurk. We followed similar procedures to the original LexTALE (Lemhöfer & Broersma, 2012) to build an analogous, quick and easy test of Turkish vocabulary. We tested both native Turkish and L2-Turkish participants who were living in Turkey at the time of testing.

articles.title: Too Little, Too Much: Evidence for Quadratic Patterns in Emotion Regulation Strategy Use in Daily Life
articles.authors: Dominique Maciejewski; Yasemin Erbas; Tak Tsun (Edmund) Lo; Egon Dejonckheere; Merlijn Olthof; Andrea Bunge; Eeske van Roekel
articles.abstract: Emotion characteristics, like emotion intensity and perceived controllability, shape how individuals regulate their emotions. While the link between emotion characteristics and emotion regulation strategies seems clear for some strategies, findings are mixed for others. This study proposes that individuals are most likely to engage in emotion regulation at average levels of emotion intensity and controllability, and less likely at low or high levels (i.e., when emotions are either too weak to require regulation or too overwhelming to regulate). To test this hypothesis, we used data from a 60-day diary study in 79 adults (mean age = 22.29, SD = 6.08, 84% female), who every evening reported on emotion characteristics (emotion intensity and controllability) and strategy use (distraction, rumination, reappraisal, expression, suppression, relaxation). In addition to single strategies, we also examined emotion regulation strategy switching (i.e., the extent to which individuals varied their strategy composition). We replicate some linear findings, including that expression, relaxation, and rumination were used on days with more intense and uncontrollable emotions (for expression only). Importantly, we provide novel evidence for quadratic relations, where suppression and reappraisal were used on days where emotions were of average intensity and controllability, and less on days where emotions were less or more intense and controllable than usual. Moreover, individuals were more likely to switch to strategies they typically do not use on days where emotions were less or more controllable than usual. These findings highlight the value of moving beyond linear relations when studying emotion regulation dynamics in daily life.

articles.title: PLFest a portable, cross-platform tool for reproducible perceptual-learning research: validation across attentional cueing conditions
articles.authors: Boris Penaloza; Marcello Maniglia; Jaap Munneke; C. Shawn Green; Aaron R. Seitz
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Visual perceptual learning (VPL) holds translational promise, yet progress is
constrained by limited reproducibility and accessibility. Having previously introduced PLFest, a
cross-platform, calibrated psychophysical platform, and validated its basic visual-function
measurements, we test here whether PLFest recovers canonical perceptual-learning signatures
and reproduces established effects of attentional cueing on learning across multiple training
paradigms.
Methods: Seventy-seven observers across four sites completed ten daily sessions of a contrast
discrimination task under one of four conditions using different attentional cueing: parafoveal
training without a cue (PT), exogenous cueing (ExAT), endogenous cueing (EnAT), or
multisensory auditory cueing (MF). Targets appeared at the fovea and at 5° eccentricity; contrast
thresholds were measured at each location by independent staircases, and learning was quantified
from exponential fits to log-thresholds. Transfer was assessed with a five-task battery (acuity,
contrast sensitivity, CSF cutoff, spatial-frequency cutoff, visual search) administered before and
after training.
Results: Every condition produced reliable learning at both eccentricities, showing the canonical
learning curve, with peripheral improvements of approximately 43% (PT), 50% (EnAT), 58%
(MF), and 78% (ExAT). Learning magnitude significantly differed across conditions, a
difference driven by ExAT, which roughly doubled the learning of the other three. Two
observations emerged descriptively: exogenous cueing specifically collapsed the peripheral–
foveal threshold gap, an effect absent under the equally valid endogenous cue, and observers
with the largest trained learning tended to show broader change across the assessment battery,
although this transfer reached significance only for peripheral spatial-frequency cutoff.
Conclusions: Across four attentional paradigms, PLFest collected reliable, robust, and
theoretically interpretable learning data, reproducing established attention effects. These findings
position PLFest as a platform for large-scale, accessible VPL research and the development of
translational training paradigms.

articles.title: The Role of Interoceptive Awareness and Relationship Satisfaction in Female Orgasm Experience
articles.authors: Maja Paul; Megan Klabunde
articles.abstract: Sexual well-being is an important component of psychological and relational health, yet the factors influencing female orgasm remain under-researched. This study examined how interoceptive awareness and relationship satisfaction predict orgasm frequency and satisfaction. A sample of 60 participants assigned female at birth a series of self-report measures assessing interoceptive awareness, orgasm frequency and satisfaction in both solitary and partnered contexts, and relationship satisfaction. Pearson correlations and multiple regression analyses were performed. Findings indicated that interoceptive awareness was positively associated with solitary orgasm satisfaction, while relationship satisfaction was positively associated with partnered orgasm satisfaction. Neither variable significantly predicted orgasm frequency. Regression analyses supported these findings, revealing that while interoceptive awareness significantly predicted solitary orgasm satisfaction, relationship satisfaction significantly predicted partnered orgasm satisfaction. Exploratory analyses, following Bonferroni correction, identified attention regulation as the only interoceptive subscale significantly associated with solitary satisfaction. These results suggest that female sexual satisfaction is context-dependent, with internal bodily awareness influencing solitary experiences and relational factors shaping partnered experiences. The results highlight the importance of integrating both interoceptive and interpersonal perspectives in understanding female sexual functioning.

articles.title: Still Very Much Dead and Alive: Incoherence in Conspiracism as a Departure from Bayesian Rationality
articles.authors: Alessandro Miani; Nicole Cruz; Stephan Lewandowsky
articles.abstract: Incoherence has long been considered a hallmark of belief in conspiracy theories, though recent research has cast doubt on this claim. We identify theoretical and methodological shortcomings in previous assessments of incoherence and connect this work to current research on reasoning by modeling conspiracism as a set of probabilistic beliefs and computing incoherence as the departure from Bayesian principles of rational belief. In Study 1, reanalyzing data from 8 studies involving 8,590 participants, we examined the relationship between conspiracism and belief in mutually incompatible conspiracy theories (e.g., Princess Diana “was murdered” and “faked her own death”). A robust association with conspiracism emerged, with the strongest conspiracy believers showing a 91% probability of being incoherent. In Study 2, a preregistered experiment with 469 participants, we investigated whether incoherence is specific to conspiratorial contents related to a person’s worldview, or reflects a more general tendency toward inconsistency. We manipulated the content domain (conspiracy vs nonconspiracy) and logical relation (contrary vs contradictory) of incompatible explanations for fictitious vignettes and collected explicit probability ratings. We found evidence for a two-component effect of incoherence: a general component, associated with reduced logical monitoring, that emerges in contradictory pairs, and a content-specific component, associated with worldview-driven endorsement, that emerges in contrary conspiracy pairs. Together, these findings encourage a move from dichotomous judgments of dis/belief to a probabilistic modeling of conspiracy beliefs, grounded in a rigorous definition of logical incompatibility and a Bayesian framework for measuring incoherence.

articles.title: Mastering the Moment: How Target Distance, Target Speed and Additional Load Shapes Affordances for Interceptive Actions in Footballing Scenarios
articles.authors: John Oyewole; Wiktoria Moskal; Hatty Jones; Helen Nuttall; Christopher Gaffney; Trevor J. Crawford; Sally Linkenauger
articles.abstract: To perform well, athletes must perceive both individual and social affordances within sporting environments. Interceptive action is often crucial to sporting success, yet research investigating interceptive affordance perception in open-play competitive sporting environments is extremely limited. This novel research investigates how dynamic factors such as target distance, target speed, and additional load influence affordance perception for interceptive actions against opponents. Across three pre-registered studies, university level footballers (n = 26) made action-based judgments on whether they could successfully intercept a target moving perpendicularly towards the space between themselves and an opponent. The position of the target systematically varied in its proximity to the participant versus the opponent. Participants consistently overestimated their interceptive ability, persisting with manipulations to target speed, and additional load applied to players and opponents. However, exploratory mixed-effects modelling revealed that participants successfully updated their interceptive affordances with increases to relative target distance, and additional load. Participants were less sensitive to increases in target speed. These findings have wide ranging implications across many sporting and non-sporting contexts, providing new insights to understanding affordance perception in competitive sporting situations and other fast-paced environments. Additionally, this work has practical applications to coaching, highlighting opportunities to enhance interceptive decision-making during matches.

articles.title: The Brain of Presence: A Foundational Framework for Social Neuroscience
articles.authors: Driss Boussaoud
articles.abstract: The concept of a “social brain” has been highly productive in social neuroscience, helping identify neural systems involved in social cognition. Yet this framework typically defines sociality at the level of social cognition, even though such processes unfold within presence-sensitive operating conditions established by a more basic operation: the registration of others’ presence. This review asks where sociality begins. I argue that social presence—the mere presence, observation, or represented availability of others—should be treated as a foundational condition of brain function rather than as a secondary aspect of social context. I revisit the origins and limits of the social-brain framework, then review evidence from social facilitation and inhibition showing that the presence of conspecifics alters performance across tasks and species. I next examine neural findings demonstrating that social presence modulates neuronal recruitment, large-scale network organization, and synaptic efficacy across scales. Together, these findings suggest that social presence is not simply an external modifier of cognition, but a fundamental condition shaping how cognition and brain function are organized. Sociality can thus be understood as embedded in the presence-shaped organization of brain function across levels, calling for a rethinking of how it is conceptualized in the brain. This claim is best understood as chronological and architectural together rather than as a choice between the two: presence is continuously estimated, in the manner of a predictive-brain process, ahead of the social-cognitive operations it organizes, which is why models of cognition and brain function that omit it remain structurally incomplete. I distinguish this proposal from second-person neuroscience and from definitions of social cognition that already invoke represented presence, and I outline falsifiable predictions, including tests of discriminant validity, to evaluate its explanatory value relative to existing constructs.

articles.title: Feasible Cognition Requires Latent State
articles.authors: Ian S. Howard
articles.abstract: Intelligent behavior across motor control, speech production, learning, and social cognition operates under uncertainty: sensory feedback is noisy and delayed, actions are imprecise, and observable outcomes often have ambiguous causes. When multiple latent states generate identical or sufficiently similar observations, regulation, learning, and evaluation based on behavior alone become underdetermined. We argue that inference over latent state is therefore a feasibility condition for cognition under partial observability, rather than merely a modeling convenience. Observable signals—movements, sensory inputs, speech outputs, and social actions—should be understood as noisy evidence about hidden causes, including motor state, articulatory configuration, task context, and intentions. This perspective suggests that separate psychological literatures share a common control-theoretic structure: regulation under noise, delay, ambiguity, and partial observability. It also helps explain why diverse domains exhibit robustness, tolerance of variability, context sensitivity, and structured credit assignment. These properties arise from regulation of inferred, task-relevant state, rather than precise control of observable behavior. The account is not proposed as a replacement for belief-state, internal-model, predictive-coding, or active-inference approaches, but as a cross-domain feasibility argument: latent-state representations support control, learning, and evaluation only when task-relevant hidden distinctions are detectable from observations and stabilizable or actionable. Cognition, from this view, depends on latent state estimates that render behavior, learning, and evaluation well-defined under uncertainty.

articles.title: Person-Related Selection Bias in Mobile Sensing Research: Robust Findings from Two Panel Studies
articles.authors: Ramona Schoedel; Thomas Reiter; Michael D. Krämer; Yannick Roos; Markus Bühner; David Richter; Matthias R. Mehl; Cornelia Wrzus
articles.abstract:
In psychology, mobile sensing is increasingly used to record behavior in real-life situations.
However, little is known about the selectivity of samples participating in these new data
collection approaches and thus about potential risks to the validity of research findings. We therefore
investigated coverage error and nonresponse error as two sources of selection bias in
smartphone-based data collections. Specifically, we examined whether smartphone system ownership
(Android versus iOS, i.e., coverage error) and willingness to participate (nonparticipation
versus intention to participate versus actual participation, i.e., nonresponse error) are associated
with socio-demographic, socio-economic, and personality characteristics.

Using two large-scale panel studies, we found replicable patterns for coverage error (N =
1,218; N = 5,123) and nonresponse error (N = 1,673; N = 2,337): The ownership of Android
devices (in comparison to iOS devices) was associated with lower levels of education, income,
and extraversion. The willingness to participate in mobile sensing studies was found to be higher
among younger age groups, males, those with higher levels of openness to experience, and those
with lower levels of neuroticism. Furthermore, different person characteristics played different
roles at different stages of the recruitment process. Taken together, the results show that some selection
bias in mobile sensing studies exists and that the effects were small to moderate in magnitude
as well as comparable to selection bias for other, more common data collection approaches,
such as online surveys.

articles.title: Understanding behavior through permutation-based predictive modeling
articles.authors: Mirko Thalmann; Marcel Binz; Eric Schulz
articles.abstract: Machine learning excels at prediction, while behavioral science aims to explain the causal mechanisms underlying behavior. We combine the strengths of both by introducing a permutation-based method that constrains what a machine learning model can learn. Our approach occupies a space between predictive models and explanatory frameworks such as cognitive models. By comparing predictive accuracy across controlled permutations, the method decomposes behavioral variability into average participant effects, individual differences, sequence effects, idiosyncratic sequence effects, and task-specific variables. Applying our approach to intertemporal and ethical choice tasks, we find that individual differences in decision making are small yet systematically shaped by idiosyncratic history. In the ethical choice task, cultural differences are surprisingly small. In the intertemporal choice task, the method enabled the development of new cognitive models, uncovering overlooked behavioral patterns. These findings highlight the potential of controlled machine learning approaches to generate explanatory insights and inform debates about task design.

articles.title: Adolescents in the Age of Generative AI: A Half-Decade Systematic Review
articles.authors: Anne J. Maheux; Chelly Maes; Rhetta Power; Caitlin Mbuakoto; Anthony G Vaccaro
articles.abstract: Generative AI is the most rapidly adopted technology in human history, with adolescents among its earliest and most active users. This systematic review synthesized 82 peer-reviewed empirical studies on adolescent (defined as mean age between 10–20) AI use published between 2020 and 2025. The literature is characterized by a focus on education, attitudes towards AI adoption, and frequency of AI use, with little attention to diverse use cases of AI (e.g., as a companion, for health information or advice) or diverse socioemotional outcomes (e.g., mental health, peer relationships). Methodologically, the field is characterized by mostly cross-sectional self-report designs (61% of published studies). Only two studies used longitudinal designs, 5 used experimental designs, and 8 focused on scale development and psychometrics. Participant recruitment has been geographically concentrated in Asia (66% of published studies), yet relatively diverse in terms of age within adolescence (29% of studies include early adolescents ages ~14 and younger, 54% include middle adolescents ages ~14–18; 49% include older adolescents ages ~18 and older). Findings were heterogeneous across topics, limiting cumulative conclusions. We synthesize the themes and methods across studies and outline a research agenda to advance a more theoretically grounded, methodologically rigorous, and developmentally informed science of adolescent AI use.

articles.title: A re-analysis of Chiang et al. (2017): Cognitive-behaviour therapy does not have a statistically significant effect on levels of depression or mania in bipolar disorder
articles.authors: David Smailes; Milo Johns; Peter Moseley
articles.abstract: There is an ongoing debate concerning the efficacy of cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT) for bipolar disorder. Chiang et al.’s (2017) meta-analysis concluded that CBT was effective in reducing symptoms of depression and mania severity in patients with bipolar disorder, and that article has been cited more than 200 times. However, Chiang et al.’s conclusions are based, in part, on a pair of implausibly large effect sizes that were extracted from a randomized controlled trial published in 2010. In this paper, we repeat Chiang et al.’s analyses, but replace the pair of implausibly large effect sizes with much more plausible effect sizes taken from a second journal article that reported results of that 2010 randomized controlled trial. Our findings suggest that Chiang et al. should have reported that CBT does not have a statistically significant effect on symptoms of depression and mania severity in patients with bipolar disorder. Data supporting our findings are available at this – doi.org/10.17605/osf.io/x3qwp – page on the Open Science Framework.

articles.title: The Fearbase: a dynamically growing database and collaborative consortium for experimental work on human fear conditioning research
articles.authors: Maria Bruntsch; Tina B Lonsdorf; Fritz Becker; Annalena Witte; Conrad Alting; Joel Beckmann; Katarina Blask; Mana R Ehlers; The Fearbase consortium
articles.abstract: The experimental study of fear learning and memory through fear conditioning paradigms has provided critical insights into the mechanisms underlying threat processing, and the etiology of anxiety- and stress-related disorders. Reliable estimation of effects and generalizability are limited by small sample sizes, in combination with substantial interindividual differences, and methodological heterogeneity. To overcome these challenges, we introduce the Fearbase - a collaborative, harmonized, and continuously growing database that aggregates individual participant data from human fear conditioning experiments. This resource enables well-powered hypothesis-driven and exploratory analyses, as well as mega- and meta-analyses. Here, we outline the Fearbase content, data access procedures, and consortium structure. As a continuously expanding resource, the Fearbase will evolve to incorporate additional measures and experimental designs relevant to fear learning and related processes. The Fearbase aims to accelerate cumulative science and serves as a topical and infrastructural blueprint for collaborative data-sharing initiatives in related fields.

articles.title: Item Features Affect How Well People Can Predict Self-Reported Personality Traces of Strangers
articles.authors: Richard Rau; Sebastian Kueppers; Mitja D Back; Michael P. Grosz
articles.abstract: Previous work suggests that self–other agreement in personality judgment is lower for evaluative than for neutral items. We tested whether this pattern generalizes to judgments collected with Who Knows, a mobile app in which users watch brief video clips of strangers and try to predict how targets answered highly concrete personality statements. The dataset comprised >340,000 predictions from >4,000 judges across 296 items and 75 targets. Contrary to prior findings, item evaluativeness did not reduce prediction accuracy with respect to targets’ self-reports. Instead, evaluativeness was positively associated with prediction accuracy for Likert-type items while no effect was observed for binary items. Other coded item features (e.g., base rate and psychological modality) showed no consistent associations with prediction accuracy across formats. We propose two boundary conditions that may help reconcile these results with earlier work: (a) the present item pool is unusually concrete, which alters the distribution and correlates of desirability/evaluativeness, and (b) judges were instructed to predict targets’ self-reports rather than provide their own impressions. Under this instruction, judges may partly rely on between-target differences in self-protective responding when forming predictions. These findings caution against transferring item-feature effects from adjective-based impression paradigms to concrete items and self-report prediction tasks.

articles.title: Combined eye tracking and electroencephalography during referential selection in dyadic interaction
articles.authors: Ingmar Brilmayer; Philip Georgis; Petra B. Schumacher
articles.abstract: Understanding language in real-world interaction requires methods that integrate auditory, visual, and neural signals. We present a proof-of-concept study combining mobile electroencephalography (EEG) and eye tracking to investigate referential selection during dyadic communication. Using a simplified version of the director task, pairs of participants engaged in naturalistic object-movement instructions while EEG and gaze data were recorded and synchronized via LabStreamingLayer. This approach allowed us to calculate overlap-corrected, regression-based event-related potentials (rERPs) time-locked to spoken nouns and to participants’ fixations, focusing on the N400/P300 (300–500 ms) and later (500–800 ms) time windows. Our results show that gaze behavior and fixation-related potentials provide crucial information for interpreting language-related ERPs: targets competing with occluded referents elicited stronger P300 effects, suggesting higher attentional demands, while fixation timing systematically modulated neural responses. Contrary to predictions, competitors received little visual attention, indicating that participants prioritized direct task-relevant information. These findings highlight the potential of multimodal data integration for understanding attentional and predictive mechanisms in real-world communication. Importantly, we demonstrate that established analytic techniques, such as artifact subspace reconstruction, independent component analysis, and linear deconvolution for ERP calculation, are applicable to noisy, naturalistic data sets. This study thus provides a methodological framework for linking gaze and neural activity during interactive language processing beyond laboratory constraints. All preprocessing and analysis scripts, together with example data sets, are openly available on OSF (https://osf.io/5ds4z; a modified and optimized Python implementation is also available on github: https://github.com/XlinCLab/DGAME).

articles.title: Evidence for a common semantic gateway for words and nonwords across languages
articles.authors: Fabio Marson; Rolando Bonandrini; Simona Amenta; Marco Marelli
articles.abstract: Nonwords are strings of letters that are typically considered to be meaningless. However, recent studies showed that nonwords activate semantic information by means of the elements embedded in their sublexical structure. Whether the process underlying semantic activation for nonwords is the same that underlies word processing is still unclear. To explore this issue, we analyzed data from the English Crowdsourcing Project, a megastudy based on a vocabulary test involving thousands of participants and thousands of stimuli. We analyzed the effect of semantic load measures associated with nonwords (namely semantic neighborhood density and orthography-to-semantic consistency) and their modulation by individual sensitivity to semantic information in predicting behavioral responses. Reaction times for nonword categorization were found to be slower in relation to the estimated semantic load of nonwords and, crucially, this interference effect was furtherly modulated by the individual sensitivity to semantic information estimated from word processing. A consistent pattern of results was found also in the Italian, Spanish, and Dutch datasets, with semantic neighborhood density being the most stable and robust predictor of reaction times across languages. These results point to the existence of a unitary mechanism of semantic processing that exploits systematicity and statistical properties derived from language usage to promote the activation of semantic traces in strings of letters, regardless of their lexicality.

articles.title: Personalizing Chronic Pain Treatment Using Person-Specific Networks: A Single-Case Experimental Design Study
articles.authors: Saskia Scholten; Verena Eunike Hofmann; Felicitas Kininger; Julia Glombiewski
articles.abstract:
Objective: Complex disorders such as chronic pain require individualized clinical decision-making, yet existing treatment frameworks provide limited guidance at the individual level. This proof-of-concept study evaluated the feasibility and acceptability of using person-specific networks to guide treatment target selection and match evidence-based intervention modules.
Method: Twelve patients with chronic pain and comorbidities (MAge = 60.25, 58% identified as female) and nine therapists participated. Qualitative interviews assessing feasibility and acceptability were conducted following treatment. Treatment effects were evaluated using an ABCD Single-Case Experimental Design with randomized baselines. Quantitative outcomes included compliance and individual- and group-level analyses of disability measures. 
Results: Mean observations per participant were 298; ≥80% compliance was achieved in seven participants across phases. Qualitative analyses highlighted participant-centered assessment design for feasibility and acceptability. Self-monitoring and structured data-informed reflection were identified as central mechanisms for increasing awareness of everyday processes and supporting therapeutic reflection. Exploratory individual-level analyses showed mixed treatment effects, whereas multilevel analysis indicated an average decrease in disability scores from baseline to intervention phase (β₁ = -0.45, p = .03). 
Conclusions: The clinical value of data-informed personalization may not lie in algorithmic precision alone, but rather in its ability to facilitate patient-centered, reflective clinical reasoning in complex cases.

articles.title: A resting-state EEG functional connectivity study in informal caregivers of patients with various conditions
articles.authors: Rebecca KPETOU; Filipa Raposo Pereira; Valentina La Corte; nicolas villain; Mathilde Groussard; Thomas Hinault; Hervé Platel
articles.abstract:
Background: Informal caregiving for individuals with Alzheimer’s disease and related 
neurodegenerative disorders represents a major public health issue. Although caregiving is widely 
recognised as a source of chronic psychosocial stress with potential consequences for brain health and 
cognition, its neurofunctional correlates remain poorly understood. Two theoretical frameworks, the 
Stress Process Model and the Healthy Caregiver Hypothesis, offer competing predictions regarding the 
cognitive impact of caregiving, yet neuroimaging studies directly addressing these perspectives remain 
scarce. 
Objective: This study aimed to investigate resting-state EEG functional connectivity in informal 
caregivers and non-caregivers from the INSIGHT-preAD cohort, and to explore whether caregiving 
status is associated with specific connectivity patterns and cognitive performance. 
Methods: Resting-state EEG data were analysed in 41 self-reported caregivers and 39 matched non
caregivers. Functional connectivity was estimated using the weighted Phase Lag Index across five 
frequency bands: delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma. Analyses focused on posterior regions of the 
default mode network, including the posterior cingulate cortex and precuneus, as well as prefrontal
parahippocampal networks. Neuropsychological assessments targeted episodic memory and 
executive functioning. 
Results: Exploratory findings revealed increased beta-band functional connectivity in caregivers, 
particularly within posterior default mode regions and prefrontal-parahippocampal coupling. These 
connectivity patterns were associated with slightly poorer cognitive performance. 
Conclusion: These preliminary results suggest that informal caregiving may be associated with specific 
resting-state EEG connectivity patterns in cognitively unimpaired older adults. They provide new 
insights into potential neurofunctional mechanisms underlying vulnerability or resilience in caregivers. 
Keywords: Informal caregivers, EEG, functional connectivity, cognition, chronic stress

articles.title: Semantic and episodic contributions of long-term memory to working memory in young and older adults
articles.authors: Lea M. Bartsch; Eda Mizrak; Vanessa M. Loaiza
articles.abstract: Previous research has shown that episodic and semantic long-term memory can support working memory performance in young adults. When remembering word pairs, performance improves when pairs have been previously learned (episodic long-term memory) or are semantically related (semantic long-term memory, e.g., table–chair). However, these types of long-term memory support are typically examined in isolation, and it remains unclear whether they contribute to working memory via distinct mechanisms or whether their roles change with age. Across three experiments, younger and older adults completed word-pair working memory tasks that independently manipulated episodic and semantic long-term memory support (Experiment 1A: N = 56 younger adults, N = 56 older adults; Experiment 1B: N = 55 younger adults; Experiment 2: N = 59 younger adults, N = 60 older adults). We examined whether older adults may benefit from both forms of long-term memory within the same task, how episodic and semantic support interact, and which mechanisms underlie the semantic benefit to working memory. The results show that both younger and older adults reliably benefited from episodic long-term memory support. Semantic relatedness also improved working memory performance across age groups. Critically, analyses targeting underlying mechanisms indicated that the semantic benefit arose from facilitated formation of episodic traces within the activated portion of long-term memory, rather than from strengthened bindings maintained within working memory itself. Together, these findings demonstrate that working memory flexibly draws on episodic and semantic long-term memory, and that this capacity remains largely intact in older adulthood.

articles.title: Rethinking Age Differences in ERP Word Predictability Effects During Reading: Evidence from Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Ellen Spencer; Elaine Mercer; Ascension Pagan; Kevin Paterson; Victoria A McGowan
articles.abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) studies of reading often report smaller and delayed neural responses to word predictability in older adults, particularly in the N400 and later frontal positivity components of event-related potential (ERP) measures. These findings have been interpreted as evidence for age-related declines in the use of contextual information during language comprehension. However, this contrasts with eye-movement research, which typically shows preserved or enhanced use of context in older readers. One possible explanation is methodological. Many EEG studies use rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP), where words are presented one at a time at a fixed pace. This limits natural reading behaviors that may be especially important for older adults. To evaluate this possibility, we conducted a meta-analysis of 12 EEG studies examining age differences in word predictability effects during sentence reading. RSVP studies were compared with studies using more naturalistic presentation methods. On average, older adults showed smaller and delayed N400 predictability effects than younger adults, replicating the standard ageing pattern. Critically, presentation method moderated these effects. RSVP studies consistently showed smaller and delayed predictability effects in older adults, whereas more naturalistic paradigms showed comparable effects across age groups and sometimes larger effects in older adults. No reliable age differences were observed in late frontal positivity effects. These findings suggest that widely reported age-related reductions in EEG word-predictability effects may reflect experimental constraints rather than a general decline in contextual processing during reading. Under more natural reading conditions, older adults appear to retain the ability to use prior context effectively.

articles.title: Internal action representations can emerge without motor execution
articles.authors: Stephan Frederic Dahm; Carla Czilczer; Marcos Moreno-Verdú; Pierre Sachse
articles.abstract: Humans routinely reason about actions they have never performed, yet it remains unclear whether the internal representations supporting such simulations require prior motor execution. Here, we test whether recent execution experience is necessary for constructing functional action representations in a mental paper folding task. A total of 196 participants completed an experience phase involving either imagery, execution, or a structurally matched execution control task, followed by a mental paper folding test. Both imagery and execution experience led to shorter response times compared to the control condition, while no differences emerged between imagery and execution groups. Response times and error rates increased systematically with fold complexity, indicating sequential transformation processes independent of experience type. Notably, imagery experience increased the subjective ease of simulation without enhancing objective performance beyond execution. These findings demonstrate that internal action representations can be constructed without prior motor execution. Rather than serving as a prerequisite, execution experience might refine representations that can already be established through imagery. More broadly, the results challenge accounts that view close sensorimotor experience as necessary for effective internal simulation and support the view that cognitive systems can generate task-relevant representations through representational processes.

articles.title: Running ahead of the evidence? Rethinking the exercise-cognition consensus after five decades of research
articles.authors: Daniel Sanabria; Luis F. Ciria; Darias Holgado; František Bartoš; Antonio Luque-Casado; Miguel A. Fernández-del-Olmo; Pandelis Perakakis; Rafael Román-Caballero
articles.abstract:
The Internet is flooded with short news, feature articles, videos, and podcasts extolling
the goodness of physical exercise. For a few years now, these claims have
increasingly emphasized the cognitive benefits of practicing physical exercise
regularly, statements and beliefs that are seemingly backed up by hundreds of
randomized controlled trials (RCTs), dozens of meta-analyses synthesizing this
empirical work, and even endorsements from the World Health Organization (WHO).
This consensus, however, deserves closer scrutiny.

articles.title: What’s Brown and Sticky? Peering Into the Ineluctable Comedic Mystery of Dad Humor with a Handful of Machine Learning Models, Hundreds of Humans, and Tens of Thousands of Dad Jokes
articles.authors: Paul Joseph Silvia; Meriel I. Burnett
articles.abstract: A stick, of course. This masterpiece of human creativity is an example of a dad joke, a clean, good-natured joke founded on puns, literalism, and wordplay. After unpacking the structures, contents, and comedic devices that distinguish dad jokes from related genres, the present research explored both the features of dad jokes that make them funny and the kinds of people who find them funny. Study 1 coded over 32,000 jokes scraped from the Reddit r/dadjokes community. Ridge regression models found that a joke’s upvotes and comments were modestly predictable from its text and topic features (test sample R2 = 4.02% and 4.42%). After curating a hypercube subsample of 501 dad jokes, Study 2 found that both text features (e.g., concreteness, semantic distance, question framing) and topic features (e.g., moms, pregnancy, and numbers) predicted the funniness ratings made by adults (n = 621) much more effectively (cross-validated R2 = 28.2%). Study 3 found that individual differences in people’s funniness ratings were associated with cultural conventionality (e.g., parenthood, more education, affluence, religiosity, and conservatism) and social boldness (e.g., need for uniqueness, low honesty-humility, and boldness; cross-validated R2 = 32.4%), and that dads found the dad jokes funnier. Seemingly simple wordplay humor illuminates the psychology of verbal creativity and extends the psychology of aesthetics into art encounters that provoke feelings of amusement, playfulness, and absurdity. The coded dad jokes are freely available for researchers interested in studying humor or hassling their teenagers.

articles.title: The Expression of Self-Conscious Emotions and Their Motivational and Communicative Function in Early Development
articles.authors: Milica Nikolic; Robert Hepach
articles.abstract: Self-conscious emotions, such as embarrassment, guilt, shame, and pride, play a central role in managing reputation and interpersonal relationships. They are thought to emerge in the first years of life, with children’s developing cognitive capacities and their growing appreciation of social norms and rules. Recent years have seen a rapid increase in research on self-conscious emotions in early life, supported by advanced methods including manual and automated micro-coding of non-verbal behaviour, depth sensor imaging, and physiological measures. In this narrative review, we summarize this emerging evidence from developmental science, which suggests that self-conscious emotions may be observed earlier than previously thought—within the first months and years of life, and that they seem to serve both motivational and communicative functions from early on. Specifically, they motivate socially appropriate behaviours, such as helping others, while communicating adherence to social norms and rules. Further, we highlight current limitations in the field and recommend more experimental and longitudinal studies especially in infancy and toddlerhood, the integration of multi-modal data, and the use of naturalistic tasks to advance our understanding of self-conscious emotions in early development.

articles.title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of efficacy beliefs in pro-environmental behavior change
articles.authors: Christina M. Juchem; Karen R.S. Hamann; Susanne Buecker; Lisa Marie Warner; Saskia Baumgardt; Abira Suthakaran; Leonie C. Bandurski; Eva Asselmann
articles.abstract: Efficacy beliefs – individuals’ conviction that their actions can make a meaningful difference – are widely regarded as key psychological drivers of pro-environmental behavior and promising targets for climate interventions. Yet it remains unclear whether changing these beliefs translates into action. We conducted a preregistered systematic review synthesizing longitudinal and interventional evidence (129 studies; N ≈ 65,000) to evaluate whether, and under what conditions, efficacy beliefs promote pro-environmental behavior change. After correcting for publication bias, efficacy interventions showed no meaningful effect on intention and behavior (d = 0.01-0.04), largely because most interventions failed to reliably increase efficacy beliefs. Multi-session interventions showed greater promise, whereas brief, message-based interventions were ineffective in altering pro-environmental action. These findings challenge assumptions about the malleability and behavioral impact of efficacy beliefs and redirect research toward understanding how efficacy beliefs can be durably strengthened and interact with social and structural factors to promote climate action.

articles.title: Psychometric Evaluation of a 10-Item Korean Adaptation of the Perceived Vulnerability to Disease Scale (PVD-Kr-10)
articles.authors: Gyeongbae Son; Sungeun You; Jong-Woo Paik; Hanson Park
articles.abstract:
Background: The Perceived Vulnerability to Disease (PVD) scale measures individual differences in perceived susceptibility to infectious diseases and germ aversion. Although adapted in several countries, no Korean version is currently available. This study aimed to evaluate the psychometric properties of a Korean adaptation of the PVD (PVD-Kr).
Methods: Data from 1,150 Korean participants (1,065 adults, 85 adolescents; M age = 42.43, SD = 14.78) were collected via an online survey during the COVID-19 pandemic (March–June 2021). Translation equivalence, cultural appropriateness, and content relevance were reviewed by a multidisciplinary expert panel. The sample was randomly split for exploratory factor analysis (EFA; n = 575) and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA; n = 575). Measurement invariance across male and female respondents was examined using multigroup CFA. Convergent validity was assessed using the Korean Disgust Scale–Revised (K-DS-R), and hypothesis-based construct validity using the GAD-7, DASS-21, COVID-19 Concern Scale, and PHQ-15.
Results: EFA yielded a 10-item two-factor solution comprising four Perceived Infectability (PI) items and six Germ Aversion (GA) items after five reverse-scored items were removed, four of which formed a separate keying-related factor. CFA supported this model, which showed acceptable-to-good fit (χ²/df = 3.95, CFI = .98, TLI = .97, RMSEA = .07, SRMR = .05). Internal consistency was acceptable (PI: α = .84; GA: α = .76). The K-DS-R correlated more strongly with GA than with PI, consistent with the original scale. PI showed stronger associations with the GAD-7, DASS-21, and PHQ-15, whereas GA was more strongly associated with COVID-19 concern. Measurement invariance across male and female respondents held up to the strict level.
Conclusions: The PVD-Kr-10 showed acceptable psychometric properties and theoretically consistent validity patterns.
