The removal of reverse-scored items aligns with findings from other cross-cultural adaptations, suggesting that the difficulty posed by reverse-scored items may recur across cultural adaptations.

articles.title: PLFest: A Multi-Site Validation of an Open Platform for Visual and Cognitive Assessment
articles.authors: Boris Penaloza; Marcello Maniglia; Jaap Munneke; C. Shawn Green; Aaron R. Seitz
articles.abstract:
Purpose: To evaluate the feasibility, validity, and scalability of PLFest, an open-source, Unity-based, cross-platform application designed for standardized, multi-site visual and cognitive assessment and training.
Methods: Two hundred sixty participants (mean age = 23 years) were recruited across four university sites in the United States. Participants completed a battery of five visual assessments administered through PLFest, including visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, spatial frequency cutoff, contrast sensitivity at spatial-frequency cutoff, and visual search. Five cognitive assessments measuring visuospatial working memory, verbal working memory, fluid reasoning, inhibitory control, and selective attention were also administered. Descriptive statistics and performance distributions were examined and compared with normative data.
Results: Visual acuity and contrast sensitivity measures closely matched previously reported normative values obtained using established clinical and psychophysical methods. Spatial frequency cutoff and visual search tasks produced stable threshold estimates while showing substantial inter-individual variability. Performance across all cognitive assessments was consistent with published validation studies of the corresponding tasks. Across the full battery, adaptive procedures demonstrated reliable convergence and generated well-distributed performance measures without evidence of substantial floor or ceiling effects. Importantly, these findings were observed across four geographically distributed testing sites using standardized consumer-grade tablet hardware.
Conclusions: PLFest provides reliable and scalable assessment of visual and cognitive function using portable consumer devices. The platform supports standardized data collection across distributed research settings while maintaining performance characteristics consistent with established laboratory and clinical benchmarks. These findings support the use of PLFest as a reliable framework for large-scale studies of vision and cognition.
Translational Relevance: By reducing dependence on specialized laboratory infrastructure and trained personnel, PLFest may facilitate broader access to visual and cognitive assessment, enabling large-scale research, screening, and future rehabilitation applications.

articles.title: A randomized controlled trial exploring dose-response effects of mindfulness meditation practice on wellbeing
articles.authors: Nicholas Ian Bowles; Nicholas T Van Dam; Jonathan Nicholas Davies; Julieta Galante
articles.abstract:
Background/Objectives: Psychological benefits have been demonstrated across a variety of different meditation offerings. There remains, however, limited evidence clarifying how different ‘doses’ of meditation relate to benefits, highlighting fundamental limitations for clinical best practice. The present study aimed to examine dose–response effects across a range of daily mindfulness meditation durations in a well-powered randomized controlled experiment, while also investigating whether actual practice behaviour predicts improved outcomes, independent of assigned dose.
Method: A total of 716 meditation-naïve, healthy adults were randomized to one of four daily mindfulness practice conditions (4-, 10-, 20-, or 30-minutes) over a 28-day online intervention. The primary outcome was wellbeing, and secondary outcomes included mindfulness-related constructs and mental health symptoms. Primary analyses used a constrained longitudinal design within an intention-to-treat approach to test for differences between assigned dose groups. Secondary per-protocol analyses were conducted to reduce effects of nonadherence. Exploratory analyses examined five measures of practice dose, alongside motivation and expectancy variables, as predictors of outcomes.
Results: Significant improvements were observed across all outcomes and arms from baseline to post-intervention (|SMD| = 0.29–0.56, FDR-adjusted p < .05). We found no evidence of a dose–response effect based on assigned practice dose in either intention-to-treat or per-protocol analyses for any outcome (all p’s > .05). While 70.9% of those in the 10-minute group completed 2/3 of sessions, only 65.3% and 48.5% of participants did the same in the 20- and 30-minute groups, respectively. In exploratory analyses, practice engagement was significantly related to 8/10 outcomes ([β] = 0.05-0.14), while goal confidence was significantly related to 9/10 outcomes ([β] = 0.08-0.21), and time was significantly related to changes on all outcomes ([β] = 0.26-0.54; FDR-adjusted p < .05). 
Conclusions: These findings suggest that within the first 30 days of practice among non-clinical, beginner populations there is no evidence that longer doses (up to 30 min) are more beneficial than shorter doses (down to 5 min) of daily meditation, while practice engagement and confidence in the efficacy of meditation may impact effects.

articles.title: Red, blue, and morally alike: Moral reframing of climate change works when we don't make incorrect assumptions
articles.authors: Diego A. Reinero; Alyssa Hannah Sinclair; Danielle Cosme; Emily B. Falk
articles.abstract: Concern about climate change is politically polarized. Some researchers attribute this divide to moral differences between liberals and conservatives, and find that reframing climate messages around moral values presumably aligned with the recipient's ideology (e.g., purity for conservatives) can increase support for environmental action. We tested whether tailoring messages to participants' actual top moral value is more effective than presuming their moral priorities. Across three experiments (N = 3,455; two preregistered, census-matched, politically balanced samples), most liberals and conservatives shared the same top moral value: care and compassion. Personalizing climate messages to each individual’s top moral value modestly increased climate outcomes (e.g., climate concern, efficacy beliefs, policy support) across the political spectrum. Crucially, however, misassuming someone’s top moral value backfired among moderates and conservatives. These findings challenge narratives of deep moral division and suggest that personalized moral reframing may help bridge the political divide on climate change.

articles.title: Psychotic-like experiences and neurobehavioral reward processing in adolescents who use cannabis
articles.authors: Shelley Gresko; Elizabeth Thompson; Meghan A. Gonsalves; Gillian LeBlanc; Elizabeth Lorenc; Jane Metrik; Michael Frank; Sarah Ryan; Emily Olenik; Leslie Brick
articles.abstract: Importance: Cannabis use (CU) in adolescence is linked to increased risk of developing psychotic-like experiences (PLEs) that can lead to lifelong impairment. A candidate mechanism may be disruptions in reward processing supported by dopamine but this association has not yet been tested in adolescents with CU and PLEs. The objective of the study was to assess the interrelation between CU, PLEs, and neurobehavioral markers of reward processing (behavioral reward motivation, tissue iron signal [1/nT2*], an indicator of dopamine-related neurophysiology). Methods: Participants were adolescents (aged 14 - 17) with either >11 lifetime cannabis episodes or <5 lifetime cannabis episodes (controls). CU quantity was assessed with the Daily Sessions, Frequency, Age of Onset, and Quantity of Cannabis Use Inventory. PLEs were assessed with the Achenbach Youth Self-Report Thought Problems Scale. Reward motivation (proportion of hard choices) was assessed using a behavioral task. 1/nT2* was derived from functional magnetic resonance imaging. Hypotheses, formulated during data collection as recognition grew of the risk factors of developing PLEs with CU, were: PLEs will magnify negative associations between CU and reward motivation; the positive association between greater CU and PLEs will depend on subcortical 1/nT2*. Results: 81 participants included 47 control and 34 cannabis group participants: mean [SD] age, 15.53 [1.12]; 52 female (64%). There was a significant interaction between CU and PLEs on reward motivation (OR, 2.41; 95% CI, 1.10, 5.27, p=0.028), depending on reward amount and effort required. At high PLEs, the cannabis group, compared to controls, were less motivated to work for a reward when effort required was low and medium.  Associations between CU quantity and PLEs depended on 1/nT2* (at high 1/nT2*, CU positively predicted PLEs). Daily concentrate hits interacted with 1/nT2* in the left putamen (β=51.58, 95% CI, 12.07, 91.09, p=0.01) and right thalamus (β=70.93, 95% CI, 12.32, 129.54, p =0.02), daily cannabis grams in the left putamen (β=73.34, 95% CI, 12.32, 129.54, p=0.02), right putamen (β=101.69, 95% CI, 41.72, 161.67, p=0.001), and right thalamus (β=102.38, 95% CI, 28.00, 176.77, p=0.01). Conclusions and Relevance: Adolescent CU was associated with alterations in dopamine-relevant neural and behavioral indices as a function of PLEs. Behaviorally, this manifested as less reward motivation and strategic action. Consistent with prior research on psychosis-spectrum vulnerability and dopamine-related neurophysiology, elevated 1/nT2* was observed to moderate the link between CU and PLEs in key subcortical brain regions. Longitudinal studies are needed to understand the timing and mechanism of neurobehavioral reward processing alterations.

articles.title: The Effect of Visual Contextual Information and Social Interaction on the Deceptive Effect of Fakes in Volleyball
articles.authors: Kazato Arie; Takashi Sakurai; Takayuki Murayama
articles.abstract: Head fakes (HFs) effectively bias opponents’ anticipation by directing gaze and conveying highly reliable information. However, the effects of visual contextual information (VCI) and perceived social interaction on this form of deception remain unclear. Therefore, this study investigated how VCI influences the deceptive effects of HFs and beckoning fakes (BKs) in relation to gaze behavior and reliability evaluation. Eleven novice players (NP) and 13 volleyball-experienced players (EP) completed a setter pass-direction anticipation task that simulated blocking. Their eye movements and pupil diameters were recorded during the task. The results indicated that the effect of VCI varied by skill level. For NP, the presentation of VCI significantly amplified BKs’ deceptive effect. Both prolonged fixation on the fake executor (i.e., the setter) and pupil dilation, indicating increased cognitive load, independently increased anticipation errors. In contrast, EP did not show this gaze- and cognitive-load-related increase in errors in the BK-VCI condition. Moreover, although directing an HF toward a spiker produced an upward trend in error rate as cognitive load increased among EP, the mean error rate did not exceed that observed in the isolated HF condition. Thus, for NP, perceived social interaction may capture and bind gaze to the deceiver, making it difficult to inhibit responses and thereby enhancing deception. In contrast, for EP, VCI did not further enhance HF deception. Effective fakes in team sports may rely not only on kinematic precision but also on creating interactions with teammates to manipulate opponents’ responses. In addition, effectively disguising VCI may be crucial for maximizing deception against experienced players.

articles.title: Memory-Based Learning Disabilities: A Syndrome Without a Pigeonhole. Towards a Mechanism-Based Diagnostic Framework.
articles.authors: Jaan Tulviste; Igor Glavatskikh; Elkhonon Goldberg
articles.abstract: Memory-based learning disabilities represent a distinct and underrecognized clinical entity that current diagnostic classification systems fail to capture. This theoretical synthesis argues that these difficulties constitute a coherent syndrome defined by neuropsychological vulnerabilities in declarative memory systems, particularly those dependent on hippocampal function. Evidence from cognitive neuroscience, developmental neuropsychology, and educational psychology converges to identify a distinctive phenotypic profile that differentiates memory-based learning disabilities from other developmental learning disorders. Five etiologic pathways are identified, including hypoxic ischemic, toxic metabolic, epileptic electrical, genetic congenital, and perinatal traumatic pathways, which converge on hippocampal and related medial temporal networks to produce a common phenotype. Classification systems organized by academic domain systematically overlook children whose primary difficulty lies in memory-based learning rather than phonological processing, attention, or general intellectual ability. A mechanism-based taxonomy is proposed that classifies learning disabilities according to impairments in encoding, consolidation, retrieval, or relational binding. This framework provides a basis for more precise diagnosis and targeted intervention. Memory-based learning disabilities should be recognized as a distinct syndrome, with important implications for assessment, intervention, and future research.

articles.title: Factors Shaping Autistic Adults' Experiences and Perceived Benefits of a Co-Designed VR Sensory Application.
articles.authors: Connor McCabe; Nigel Newbutt; Amanda Hutchinson; Tobias Loetscher
articles.abstract: Sensory processing differences are highly prevalent among autistic adults, yet evidence for sensory rooms and similar environments remains mixed. One likely reason is that sensory processing varies widely between individuals and fluctuates within a person across time and context, which fixed environments cannot accommodate. We co-designed a customisable, nature-based virtual reality (VR) sensory application with autistic adults to provide a portable sensory experience that users can tailor to their own needs. This study reports an ecologically valid evaluation of that prototype. Seven autistic adults used the application at home over seven days on a standalone Meta Quest 3 headset, completing per-use and daily experience-sampling ratings of stress, mood, and sensory stress, followed by semi-structured exit interviews. We analysed rating data descriptively at the participant level and interview data using qualitative content analysis. Participants varied in perceived benefit, and we characterised this through three response profiles: consistent, partial or context-dependent, and minimal perceived benefit. Crucially, benefit also varied within individuals across occasions: participants reported the application as most valuable during sensory stress or overwhelm and least valuable near baseline. Qualitative analysis identified four factors shaping this variation: whether use met a genuine regulatory need, fit between a person's sensory profile and the content, the degree of control and customisation available, and the learning curve and cybersickness. These findings suggest that the value of VR sensory tools is dynamic and individual, and that controlled or fixed-use designs risk missing the conditions under which such tools help most.

articles.title: Subjective Effects and Characteristics of Salvia Divinorum Use from a Retrospective Largescale Survey
articles.authors: Spencer W. Wheeler; Jonathan Dinsmore; Natalie L. Dyer
articles.abstract: Salvia divinorum (salvia) is a powerful, unique psychedelic, with its psychoactive compound salvinorin A acting as a kappa opioid receptor agonist. The purpose of this study was to investigate the subjective effects of salvia in a large sample and to gather early data regarding the validity of the salvia experience checklist (SEC). Four hundred participants were recruited online through forums and mailing lists. Participants answered questionnaires related to their salvia use history and their most recent salvia experience, including type and strength of extract, administration method, and inhalation time. They then rated their most recent salvia experience using the Hallucinogen Rating Scale (HRS) and the SEC, a 58-item list of experiences common to salvia. Participants provided a report of their most recent salvia experience for mixed-methods analysis of salvia phenomenology. A total of 321 participants completed the survey. The mean number of items selected on the SEC was 16.5 (SD = 10.5) and the total HRS score was 186.36 (SD = 40.48). Scores on all subscales of the HRS were significantly correlated with scores on the SEC. Tetrachoric exploratory factor analysis of the SEC resulted in 10 factors along with several indicators of adequate model fit. Manifest content analysis yielded 16 major themes. SEC scores were positively correlated with salvia extract potency and smoking as administration method. The results of this study contribute to further characterizing the unique phenomenology of salvia and advancing development of the SEC, though more work is needed, including with prospective samples.

articles.title: Modeling the temporal dynamics of cognitive control
articles.authors: Jacob Sellers; Han Zhang; Wei-Jie Zhou; John Jonides; Taraz Lee
articles.abstract: Cognitive control is a collection of processes that enable goal-directed behavior when it conflicts with habitual or automatic actions. Resolving this conflict requires sufficient time to allow goal-directed processing to override habitual responses. We explore a recent computational account of cognitive control, which proposes that the asynchronous preparation of habitual and goal-directed responses shape the temporal dynamics of cognitive control in resolving response conflicts. Across multiple experiments using a forced-response method that yields empirical speed-accuracy tradeoff functions, we show that: (1) The response preparation model (RPM) can accurately account for behavior in conflict resolution tasks; (2) This model can uncover and dissociate distinct forms of cognitive control adjustment; (3)  Parameters estimated from forced-response data generalize to predict performance in free response time (RT) tasks; (4) The RPM demonstrates strong parameter recovery; and (5) The RPM leads to qualitatively different inferences than an evidence accumulation model for conflict resolution tasks extended for use with forced-response data. Together, these findings highlight that modeling the temporal dynamics of conflict resolution can offer a powerful approach for understanding mechanisms of cognitive control.

articles.title: Against Anonymising Meta-Scientific Data
articles.authors: Malte Elson; Ian Hussey; Beth Clarke; Sabrina Fay Norwood; Sandra Grinschgl; Ruben C. Arslan; Jack Wilkinson; Matthew Page; Ioana Cristea; Pim Cuijpers
articles.abstract: There is an emerging obstacle to the replicability, credibility, and value of meta-science: the practice of anonymising study-level datasets, such as published articles, describing research artefacts. In this paper, we 1) highlight how this trend in meta-science is at odds with norms established in the broader field of research synthesis, 2) explain how this practice greatly reduces our capacity to verify, reproduce, or build on meta-scientific research, 3) explore common arguments and justifications for anonymisation, 4) provide counterpoints to these common justifications, and 5) demonstrate the adverse practical implications and epistemic costs for both meta-science specifically and science generally. If anonymisation continues as a practice, meta-science risks normalising a standard for itself that it would rightly criticise in any other area of empirical research, undermining its capacity to guide reforms as the underlying evidence is rendered unverifiable.

articles.title: Detecting facial signals in conversational corpora using OpenFace compared to manual annotation: Challenges and recommendations
articles.authors: Naomi Nota; James Trujillo; JUDITH HOLLER
articles.abstract: There has been an increased interest in studying language in a more ecologically valid context over the last years. This includes an increase in investigations of language in its natural home, namely, in multimodal face-to-face social interaction, where visual behaviour, such as facial signals, contribute to the meaning of an utterance. An approach that is often used to investigate the contribution of visual signals in language is to manually annotate visual behaviour. However, this process is extremely laborious, therefore requiring considerable resources for the creation and analysis of a large multimodal corpus. One way to overcome this problem is to use software for the automatic detection of visual behaviour. For instance, OpenFace is a popular tool for extracting facial signals from video recordings. However, many studies using OpenFace do not perform any form of quality checking, and surprisingly few peer-reviewed publications are based on validated algorithms when applying automatic facial signal analysis using OpenFace. In this paper, we point towards potential challenges and provide recommendations for using OpenFace for automatically detecting facial signals in conversational corpora, based on a direct comparison with an annotation manual for facial signals applied by trained annotators. We give potential suggestions for making use of OpenFace data while minimizing the negative impact of detection noise. This paper therefore offers additional tools for improving reliability of multimodal annotations when using OpenFace, as well as an annotation manual for coding facial signals by hand, which often still is the most reliable method in many research contexts.

articles.title: Task Configuration and Encoding Duration Jointly Shape Human Performance in a Matsuzawa-Inspired Visuospatial Working Memory Task
articles.authors: Nadine Charanek; Olessia Jouravlev
articles.abstract: Rapid visuospatial serial-recall tasks place substantial demands on working memory (WM) because they require encoding item identity, spatial location, and serial order under severe time pressure. The present study examined how task configuration and encoding duration shape human performance in a Matsuzawa-style visuospatial WM task commonly used in comparative research. Two hundred eighty-eight participants completed two different task configurations, sequential build-up and simultaneous high-load configurations, in which presentation duration was systematically varied. Longer encoding durations improved recall accuracy, but the magnitude of this benefit depended strongly on task configuration. The sequential build-up configuration supported substantially greater task progression with increasing difficulty, whereas the simultaneous high-load configuration resulted in early, steep task failure. Within the sequential build-up configuration, performance declined sharply once digit load exceeded approximately five to six items. These findings suggest that performance in rapid visuospatial WM tasks depends strongly on task configuration, particularly how information is presented, bound, and retrieved under time pressure.

articles.title: Current distress does not moderate the efficacy of cognitive reappraisal
articles.authors: Rachael Major; Robert E. Fite; Andero Uusberg; James Gross; Lorenzo Lorenzo-Luaces
articles.abstract: Background: Successful emotion regulation is a critical component of psychological health.  One of the most effective emotion regulation strategies, “cognitive reappraisal”, consists of adjusting the way a situation is perceived to alter its emotional response. Extant literature suggests that cognitive reappraisal is not only effective for reducing negative affect in subclinical populations but is also a component of clinical interventions like cognitive-behavioral therapies. Nonetheless, concerns have been raised about the efficacy of cognitive reappraisal in situations of high distress. We tested whether general emotional distress symptom severity and pre-emotion regulation distress moderates the efficacy of cognitive reappraisal (vs. expressive writing) in predicting post-regulation distress. Methods: We recruited a sample of adults (N=2,449), representative of the U.S. on age, sex assigned at birth, and race-ethnicity. After describing a recently upsetting event, participants were randomized to reappraisal or expressive writing conditions. Distress symptoms were measured with the K6 and pre- and post-regulation distress were measured with a 100-point visual analogue scale. Results: Higher levels of both pre-regulation distress and general emotional distress were positively associated with higher levels of post-emotion regulation distress. However, there was no interaction with condition and either distress variable in predicting post-regulation distress. Conclusion: Despite a large and representative sample, we did not find that cognitive reappraisal is less effective than a comparator when distress is elevated. These findings suggest that cognitive reappraisal may be efficacious even at heightened levels of distress.

articles.title: From Seconds to Seasons and Days to Decades: Timescales in Development
articles.authors: Aidan G.C. Wright
articles.abstract: Developmental processes unfold across timescales ranging from seconds to decades, yet temporal assumptions often elude direct theoretical scrutiny. This review illustrates that decisions about timescale, including how time is defined, the density and duration of observations, the temporal windows encoded in measures, and the role time plays in statistical models, are essential, substantive theoretical decisions that determine what kinds of change a study can detect and what inferences it can support. The review first examines what time represents in developmental research and how it can be operationalized, including clock time, process-based time, event-based time, and subjective time. It then considers how study design features encode temporal assumptions. Finally, it organizes statistical models around three roles time can play: predictor, outcome, and moderator. Throughout, the review emphasizes the necessity of synchronizing theoretical models, data acquisition plans, and statistical models around coherent temporal frameworks.

articles.title: Believing What We Want? Myside Bias and the Role of Open Science Practices in Public Trust in Science
articles.authors: Hilmar Brohmer; Fabian Gehm; Sarah Annabelle von Götz
articles.abstract: Public trust in science may depend not only on the quality of the research but also on laypersons’ attitudes towards the underlying topic. Although Open Science practices are meant to signal credibility, their influence on laypersons remains unclear, especially for scientifically controversial topics. In this preregistered Austrian study (N=1,083), general trust in science and myside bias strongly predicted trust in certain popular-scientific summaries, while explicit Open Science cues did not show an effect. Exploratory analyses showed that participants’ optimistic conceptions of science were also linked to higher trust. Overall, biased beliefs appear more influential than transparency-related cues for trust in science.

articles.title: Individual differences in Speech-in-Noise perception in adults with healthy hearing: auditory, cognitive, and neural mechanisms
articles.authors: Emily Tomasino
articles.abstract:
Speech perception in noisy environments is a fundamental yet highly variable human ability. While some listeners can understand speech with relative ease in adverse listening conditions, others experience substantial difficulty despite having clinically normal hearing thresholds. This review examines the factors underlying individual differences in speech-in-noise (SPIN) perception among young normal-hearing adults. The review first examines auditory factors associated with SPIN perception, including hidden hearing loss, temporal processing, figure-ground segregation, pitch perception, and musical experience. It then considers cognitive contributors, such as working memory, vocabulary knowledge, attention, and inhibitory control, which may support the perception and interpretation of degraded speech signals. Finally, neuroimaging findings are reviewed to explore the distributed neural systems involved in successful speech-in-noise perception, including the superior temporal gyrus, inferior frontal gyrus, speech-motor regions, and domain-general networks such as the cingulo-opercular and frontal-parietal systems. Collectively, the literature suggests that individual differences in SPIN perception arise from complex interactions between auditory processing abilities, cognitive resources, and neural mechanisms rather than from hearing sensitivity alone. Although several theoretical frameworks have highlighted these interactions, the review highlights the need for integrative approaches that combine behavioural and neuroimaging measures to develop a more comprehensive understanding of speech perception in challenging listening environments.
Keywords: Speech-in-noise perception, Individual differences, Working memory, Figure-ground segregation, Temporal processing, Vocabulary, fMRI, Neural correlates of speech-in-noise perception, Young healthy-hearing adults

articles.title: Believe It, Achieve It? Self-Control Beliefs in Predicting Life Outcomes
articles.authors: Jinyao Li; Marleen Gillebaart; Tim van Timmeren; Denise De Ridder
articles.abstract:
Objective
Self-control is an important predictor of many life outcomes across multiple domains. Previous research has suggested that it is not only self-control ability, but also what individuals believe about self-control that impacts outcomes. However, so far, research has mapped a limited number of self-control beliefs. The present study investigates a broader set of self-control beliefs in predicting various life outcomes.
Method
We used a previously derived set of self-control beliefs and identified four higher-order core beliefs, namely beliefs about how important, effortless, proactive, and learnable self-control is. Next, we tested the predictive value of these four higher-order beliefs on a variety of life domains, including health, work or school performance, relationships, finance, and well-being (N = 485). 
Results
Self-control beliefs significantly contributed to the prediction of outcomes, although the predictive power did not go beyond trait self-control and self-control metacognition. Notably, the various self-control beliefs showed distinct associations with different life domains, potentially reflecting their context-dependent nature. 
Conclusions
We conclude that self-control beliefs are an overlooked factor for understanding and improving self-control.

articles.title: Improving Multi-Stakeholder Cooperation, Information Sharing and Decision Making on European Asylum and Migration among the 30 EU+ Countries
articles.authors: Karin S. Moser
articles.abstract:
Europe faces immense challenges in the areas of migration and asylum that can only be 
addressed if the 30 EU+ countries (27 EU countries, Norway, Switzerland, UK) collaborate 
closely and share information regarding security, migration and asylum-seekers across the 
continent. Professor Moser’s research expertise in multi-stakeholder cooperation, decision
making and information-sharing has directly helped the European Asylum Support Office 
(EASO) Strategic Network to improve communication and collaboration among its 30 member 
countries. The research identified common ground and established new ways of communicating 
that has reduced conflicts, supported more equal and effective decision-making, and increased 
information-sharing. This has enabled EASO to lay the necessary foundations to move forward 
with new initiatives to improve harmonisation of migration and asylum policies across Europe.

articles.title: Autism and ADHD prevalence estimates: balancing concerns about overdiagnosis with the history of under-detection
articles.authors: Rachel Moseley; Mary Barker; Shahar Bar Yehuda; Hannah Belcher; Joy Benatov; Monique Botha; Sarah Cassidy; Blandine French; Eynat Gal; Aimee Grant
articles.abstract: Where global prevalence of autism and ADHD is assumed stable, increasing incidence of referrals, referral requests and recorded diagnoses have evoked concerns that autism and ADHD are overdiagnosed, i.e. that diagnoses are bestowed without sufficient justification to individuals who might be incentivised to seek diagnosis. While there is presently little evidence to support these assertions, methodological limitations demonstrably contribute to the systematic under-detection of certain sub-groups, like older adults, marginalized minorities and people assigned female at birth, within these diagnostic categories. As such, rising demand for autism and ADHD diagnosis can be understood in light of historic underdiagnosis. While strains on diagnostic systems require urgent solutions, failure to diagnose legitimate cases precludes provision of appropriate support and has severe individual and socioeconomic repercussions. Given the limitations which underlie current prevalence estimates, we draw attention to the equally problematic issue of  underdiagnosis and the accompanying individual and societal costs this brings.

articles.title: An illusion of absence in a VR traffic scenario: How A-pillar blind zones can trick you like magic
articles.authors: Subhankar Karmakar; Melika Miralem; Pierre-Pascal Forster; Lars Even Egner; Rob van Lier; Vebjørn Ekroll; Marcin Czub
articles.abstract: Previous research suggests that small or narrow obstructions of view can evoke a convincing perception that the occluded space is empty, namely the illusion of absence. Research also indicates that magicians employ this phenomenon in their tricks to create the illusion of objects appearing from nowhere. It has been proposed that A-pillars or windshield pillars of a car can also evoke this illusion of absence, apart from simply occluding what is in the blind zone, reinforcing the belief that the occluded space is empty. To test these hypotheses, we performed a VR experiment where the participants saw another car on collision course suddenly emerging from the blind zone created by the A-pillar and reported their level of surprise and understanding of what had happened. We varied the A-pillar’s angular width from 9 to 18 degrees in equal steps of 3 degrees. We found that the surprise evoked by the VR scene was typically high and even surpassed the surprise evoked by a magic trick movie using the illusion of absence. More than half the participants failed to explain the correct trajectory of the cars in the scene and the role of the A-pillar blind zone. Narrower A-pillars slightly heightened the strength of the illusion, leading to a higher surprise and lower comprehension of the VR scene. These findings suggest that the illusion of absence can pose a serious safety hazard in real-life traffic scenarios involving vehicle blind zones, such as the A-pillars.

articles.title: ‘Good Work’ through Good Relationships? A Longitudinal  Field Intervention Study about the Effectiveness of Group Coaching on Well-Being and Resilience at Work
articles.authors: Kathryn Kissell; Karin S. Moser
articles.abstract: The role of relationships for performance and well-being at work have received increasing attention in the past years, but there are very few longitudinal intervention studies addressing the relational dimensions of workplace well-being. We address this gap with a longitudinal, control group intervention study based on the job demands-resource model that explored the impact of a group coaching intervention on 32 Church of England clergy. Pre- and post-measure comparisons indicated that the intervention resulted in a significant decrease in participants’ relational fusion with others and an almost significant decrease in interpersonal emotional cutoff. Additionally, the treatment group reported an increase in supportive interpersonal relations at all levels (peer, management and congregation) compared to the control group. Moreover, results indicated a positive link between these personal and relational resource improvements and affective well-being, where intervention participants, in contrast with the control group, experienced an almost significant decrease in their perception of the severity of work demands and an increase in spiritual resources. This indicates that a person-centred intervention focused on enhancing workplace relationships significantly increases personal and interpersonal resources, reduces the deleterious effects of work demands and promotes work-related psychological health thus offering important practical implications for managing workplace relationships.

articles.title: Identifying safety risks and variances in an Integrated Urgent Care telephone triage system using modified Macroergonomic Analysis and Design (MEAD)
articles.authors: Jill Poots; Jim Morgan; Matteo Curcuruto; Stephen Elliott; Andrew Catto
articles.abstract: Telephone triage services provide care to millions of people worldwide, yet little is known about their safety. Macroergonomic Analysis and Design (MEAD) is a systems thinking method which can identify organisational system components and how they interact to contribute to organisational and patient outcomes, such as safety. This paper presents a modified MEAD method and its application in one NHS 111 telephone triage provider organisation in England. The process included a system scan (document scan and key stakeholder meetings), key informant (health advisor and clinical advisor) interviews, analysis using mapping (Leximancer), and validation of interview findings (thematic analysis and focus group/survey). Two main patient safety risks were identified as availability of services and delays across the system. COVID-19 was often implicated in contributing to patient safety issues encountered. Nascent areas for further study include the physical environment and its effect on cognition.

articles.title: Telling Stories of Resistance – Calling to Ancestral Strength: An Exploration of Mental Health Support Service Access and Adequacy for Indigenous Mothers in Manitoba
articles.authors: Sydney Levasseur-Puhach; Leslie E Roos
articles.abstract:
Background. Indigenous mothers in Canada experience disproportionate adversity related to mental health with increased risk of anxiety and depression (Black et al., 2019; Owais et al., 2020). The mental health disparity between Indigenous and non-Indigenous mothers reveals the need to provide mental health support services that are culturally aligned and meet the specific needs of this community. Indigenous identifying individuals who participated in a family mental health program for mothers of young children were contacted and invited to participate in an individual interview to share their experiences and provide their perspectives on mental health support service seeking. Research Objective and Questions. The aim of this study was to build knowledge surrounding the experiences of Indigenous mothers with mental health support service seeking and participation to inform improvements in mental health programming. This objective was framed around two central research questions. 1. What are the experiences and motivations of Indigenous mothers seeking and participating in mental health programs starting in the perinatal period? 2. How can improvements be made to existing programs? Methods. To lead this study in a culturally appropriate and collaborative way, the first author hosted a meet and greet gathering aimed at developing relationships and fostering a sense of community. The research team conducted seven virtual interviews with individual participants. Data was analyzed using a narrative approach, situating results within social, cultural, kinship, and individual contexts. Results. Findings illustrated key storylines across sub-samples of Anishinaabe (n=3) and Métis (n=4) participants. Core storylines within reflections of Anishinaabe narrators included (1) Taking Care of Me to Take Care of Them (Cycle Breaking), (2) Honouring the Child Within (Stories of Courage and Perseverance), (3) Seeking Connection (Exploring Cultural Identity), (4) Looking at the Bigger Picture (Wholistic Wellness), and (5) Systemic
TELLING STORIES OF RESISTANCE – CALLING TO ANCESTRAL STRENGTH
4
Oppression (Stories of Barriers, Protection, and Survival). Core storylines from Métis narrators included (1) Indigenous Enough (Stories of Connection and Disconnection, (2) Wearing Many Hats (Navigating Roles and Identities within Motherhood), (3) Healing the Self (Stories of Coping and Maintaining Individuality), and (4) Systems Navigation (Stories of Perseverance and Exhaustion). Discussion. Results highlight the value of relationality in the wellbeing of Indigenous mothers, as well as the importance of culturally responsive and trauma informed care in program delivery. This research will offer guidance for future studies and service providers aiming to incorporate Indigenous perspectives into program development and implementation. Findings may be used to create novel, culturally appropriate interventions to support Indigenous women and families.

articles.title: Stereotypes of Epistemic Sources and Conspiracy Beliefs: The Primacy of Perceived Morality
articles.authors: Deniz Bayramoğlu; Sinan Alper
articles.abstract: The present study examines how stereotypes of epistemic sources predict conspiracy beliefs through the lens of the Stereotype Content Model (SCM). Two studies with nationally representative samples from the United Kingdom (N = 290) and the United States (N = 289) assessed perceptions of doctors, academics, journalists, and government officials in terms of sociability, morality, competence, and assertiveness, alongside general and profession-specific conspiracy beliefs. Correlational and random forest regression analyses showed that perceived morality was the most consistent and robust predictor across professions, with lower perceived morality associated with higher conspiracy beliefs. Other dimensions showed more context-dependent associations: Competence occasionally contributed, particularly for academics; sociability varied across professions; and assertiveness was generally a weak predictor. These findings suggest that conspiracy beliefs regarding epistemic authorities are more consistently associated with perceived moral character than with perceived expertise, although competence also played a role, particularly for academics.

articles.title: Nature immersion in virtual reality does not reduce naturally occurring muscle pain or improve motor outputs during prescribed or self-regulated tasks
articles.authors: Callum O'Malley; David Harris; Sam William Hughes; Jack Cottrell; Dan Lloyd; Samuel James Vine; Tom Arthur
articles.abstract: Virtual reality (VR) can reduce experimental and clinical pain, yet it remains unclear whether these effects translate to naturally occurring muscle pain (NOMP) experienced during physical tasks. This study examined whether nature immersion presented through immersive VR attenuates NOMP and enhances motor performance during prescribed and self-regulated exercise. Two within-participant crossover studies were conducted. In Study 1, twelve healthy participants completed a stepped incremental cycling task to exhaustion under three conditions: immersive VR (nature scene), a two-dimensional sham display, and a no-intervention control. Pain onset, NOMP intensity, perceived effort, and time to exhaustion were measured. In Study 2, twenty-four participants performed intermittent isometric handgrip contractions at fixed light (13/100) or strong (50/100) perceived effort under the same conditions, with self-regulated force output and NOMP intensity measured. Immersive VR did not significantly delay pain onset, prolong time to exhaustion, reduce NOMP intensity, or lower perceived effort compared with sham or control conditions. A small reduction in NOMP intensity occurred early in the cycling task, but effects were trivial and not sustained. In Study 2, NOMP intensity, force production, and fatigue-related declines were similar across conditions. Exploratory analyses showed no moderating effects of sex, environmental presence, or fatigue. Overall, these findings demonstrate that nature-based VR immersion has limited effects on NOMP or motor performance. This likely reflects a mismatch between central mechanisms targeted by VR and strong peripheral drivers of NOMP. Consequently, VR hypoalgesia appears pain-type dependent and potentially pain-intensity dependent, with effects limited to lower pain intensities.

articles.title: Populism and the Pandemic: How the Covid-sceptic Movement Mobilised Opposition to Covid Measures.
articles.authors: Yasemin Uluşahin; Stephen Reicher
articles.abstract: The Covid-19 pandemic constituted a radically new social reality in which people were asked to observe an unprecedented set of regulations. This paper uses a Structural Analysis of Group Arguments (SAGA) framework of the speeches and campaign materials of the Covid-Sceptic movement to demonstrate how the movement provided a way for people made sense of this new reality. We provide an explanation drawing on social representations theory, the social identity approach, and identity leadership perspectives on collective mobilisation. Specifically, we show that the Covid-Sceptic movement used populist social representations that framed the pandemic as a tool of elite domination over "the people." We discuss the implications of these findings for understanding the dynamics of social mobilisation, the construction of social representations, and the role of populism as a collective process for making sense of new realities.

articles.title: Hierarchical-Model Correlations Should Replace Sample Correlations in Experimental Tasks
articles.authors: Jeffrey N. Rouder
articles.abstract: Although the sample correlation is a popular statistic for describing the association among variables, it has two well-known flaws when there is much measurement error.  First, estimates are attenuated meaning that the estimated correlation is smaller in magnitude than the true one.  Second, this downward bias is asymptotic meaning that as sample size grows researchers become increasingly confident in a wrong value.  Psychologists are well aware of these flaws and stress the need for high reliability in estimating correlations.  An alternative, advocated here, is to model measurement error and adjust estimates accordingly in a hierarchical framework.  This alternative works well in common settings in experimental psychology where tasks consist of multiple, repeated trials.  In this context, measurement error may be modeled, and as a result, researchers can accurately measure correlation with attenuation.  Also, they can assess the degree of uncertainty in a manner that incorporates rather than ignores measurement error.  The hierarchical alternative is applicable in low- and high-reliability settings.  To speed adoption, I provide a set of user-friendly R functions for calculating and visualizing the association between two variables .  Included are methods for testing critical correlation values of -1, 0, and 1.  The advocated approach is superior to sample correlation and should be adopted for everyday assessment of correlation.

articles.title: Subjective Experience of “Stream of Consciousness Impairment” in Three Patients with Severe Amnesia
articles.authors: Anaïs Servais; Fleur Gérard; Hélène Mirabel; Marie Rafiq; Marie Wolfrum; Chloé Bost; Fabrice Bonneville; Jérémie Pariente; Emmanuel Barbeau
articles.abstract: Patients with severe anterograde amnesia have been described as "stuck in time"; their mental time travel abilities are altered, and they seem to live in the present. Here, we examine an unusual phenomenon in which severely amnesic patients recurrently report the belief that they have just awakened from an unconscious state. A famous case illustrating this phenomenon was that of Clive Wearing who repeatedly wrote “I am now awake” in his diary. This unique cognitive phenomenon has not been specifically studied until now. We report three new patients who described similar subjective experiences. All three patients suffered from autoimmune encephalitis and showed massive bilateral hippocampal inflammation causing severe anterograde amnesia. We propose to call this phenomenon “Stream of Consciousness Impairment”, a reference to William James’s theory according to which, normally, we experience an uninterrupted flow of consciousness. This condition should be added to the spectrum of neurological symptoms that contribute to the study of memory and consciousness in humans.

articles.title: “It’s Just a Dick pic”: (Cyber)Rape Culture and Victim Behavior Predict Perceptions and Responses to Unsolicited Genital Images
articles.authors: Rocío Vizcaíno-Cuenca; Adrian Scott; MONICA ROMERO SANCHEZ; Hugo Carretero-Dios; Fiona Gabbert
articles.abstract: The unsolicited reception of genital images is a prevalent phenomenon, yet the influence of online social norms and victim behavior on judgments surrounding these incidents remains underexplored. In two studies with social media users from the United Kingdom, we examined how victim behavior and acceptance of myths about cyber-sexual violence influence victim-blaming and perceptions of incident severity. Study 1 (N = 131) found that participants attributed more blame to the victim and perceived the incident as less severe when the victim had previously chatted (vs. not chatted) with the perpetrator. This effect was only present among participants with higher acceptance of cyber-sexual violence myths. In Study 2 (N = 202), a similar pattern emerged, with the victim perceived as more blameworthy and the incident as less severe when she had shown sexual interest (vs. no interest) toward the perpetrator before the incident, particularly among participants with higher acceptance of myths about cyber-sexual violence. Moreover, we found an indirect effect of perceived sexual consent on this relationship. Our findings highlight the influence of online social norms on judgments of cyber-sexual violence and emphasize the importance of education on online sexual interactions.

articles.title: Beliefs versus Reality: People Overestimate the Actual Dishonesty of Others
articles.authors: Jareef Martuza; Helge Thorbjørnsen; Hallgeir Sjåstad
articles.abstract: Do people believe that others are similarly, more, or less dishonest than they truly are? The accuracy of dishonesty beliefs is not only important for psychological knowledge, but also has implications for organizations and policymaking. In this paper, Study 1 presents a research program on moral decision-making comprising 31 different effects from 11 experiments, where participants could anonymously lie for personal gain. Crucially, participants were also asked to estimate what percentage of other people would lie in the same situation. An internal meta-analysis summarizing all belief-behavior comparisons revealed that people substantially overestimate others' dishonest behavior (g = 0.61; k = 31; N = 8,126), by 13.6 percentage points on average. This effect holds across study contexts and participants’ own behavior, and 63.5% of participants overestimated dishonesty by 5 percentage points or more (only 25.4% underestimated it). We then examined potential consequences of biased dishonesty beliefs in three pre-registered follow-up studies. Study 2 (N = 981) found that providing correct information about actual honesty levels enhanced general prosocial expectations (e.g., trustworthiness, fairness). Study 3 (N = 285) revealed that professional managers have pessimistic beliefs also about people’s real-world dishonesty (e.g., insurance fraud, workplace theft), and moral pessimism predicted greater support for freedom-restrictive countermeasures to reduce dishonesty (e.g., surveillance). Study 4 (N = 741) demonstrated that providing managers with correct information about actual honesty levels causally reduced their support for freedom-restrictive countermeasures. In conclusion, the pessimistic bias in dishonesty beliefs about others is robust, and it shapes prosocial expectations and policy preferences.

articles.title: On the misuse of prediction in NeuroAI: A case study of Centaur
articles.authors: Jeffrey S Bowers; Guillermo Puebla; Sushrut Thorat; Konstantinos Tsetsos; Casimir Johannes Hendrikus Ludwig
articles.abstract:
In the field of NeuroAI, many strong claims are made about the similarity between artificial neural networks (ANNs) and the human mind.
A recent study by (Binz et al., 2025) is a case in point. The authors transcribed a large database of psychological experiments and fine-tuned a large language model to predict human responses on a trial-by-trial basis. This model, Centaur, outperformed domain-specific state-of-the-art cognitive models. We highlight several major problems with Centaur. Through simulation, we demonstrate that the model is not subject to basic cognitive constraints and that its behaviour is best characterised as a form of role-playing. More generally, we discuss the inferential fallacy of mistaking predictive power for explanatory power and the importance of subjecting models to experimental manipulations to test specific hypotheses. These critical points are often overlooked in NeuroAI, but need to be addressed if the field is to progress.

articles.title: Covert imitation acts as an adaptive reflexive gain control system in non-native speech perception
articles.authors: Hannah Wilt; Bronwen G. Evans; Yutong Ye; Patti Adank
articles.abstract: Current accounts posit that heard speech is covertly imitated to optimise speech perception. Behaviourally, covert imitation is measured using the stimulus-response compatibility (SRC) paradigm. In a speech SRC task, participants produce a prompted speech syllable whilst perceiving a speech distractor that either matches the target response (compatible condition) or does not (incompatible condition). The degree to which the distractor is covertly imitated is captured by the automatic imitation effect, computed as the difference in response times (RTs) between incompatible and compatible trials. Two theoretical accounts disagree on whether whether covert imitation is enhanced when speech perception is challenging or instead when the speech signal is familiar to the listener. To dissociate between these two accounts, we conducted two experiments utilising the speech SRC task (Experiments 1 and 2) and Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) (Experiment 2) to evaluate covert imitation of native speech sounds (perceptually easy and familiar) and of non-native speech sounds (perceptually challenging and unfamiliar). In Experiment 1, participants performed an SRC task with native and non-native sounds, before and after receiving perceptual identification training with the non-native sounds. Automatic imitation was enhanced for non-native sounds compared to native sounds and decreased to a greater extent for non-native sounds than for native sounds following perceptual training. In Experiment 2, participants passively perceived native and non-native speech sounds whilst receiving single pulses of TMS to the tongue area of the primary motor cortex (M1). Tongue M1 showed higher excitability during passive perception of non-native sounds. Our results highlight that covert imitation is enhanced during the perception of perceptually challenging, unfamiliar speech, suggesting that covert imitation acts as an adaptive reflexive gain control mechanism to optimise speech perception.

articles.title: Social Disconnection is Associated with Impaired Social Skills: Evidence from Behavioral Performance and Automated Facial Expression Analysis
articles.authors: Vladimír Ivančík; Valentína Cviková; Sára Majsniarová; Natália Čavojská; Jakub Januška; Alexandra Strakova; Sebastián Petreje; Julia; Alica Hujsová; Radoslav Lošonský
articles.abstract: Social disconnection, consisting of loneliness and social isolation, is associated with many adverse mental and physical health outcomes. However, relatively little is known about whether social disconnection is also reflected in observable social skills. The present study examined differences in social skills and facial emotional expressions between socially disconnected and socially connected healthy individuals. A total of 107 adults were classified as socially disconnected (N = 53) or socially connected (N = 54) based on established cut-offs on measures of loneliness (UCLA-3) and social isolation (LSNS-6). Participants completed the High-Risk Social Challenge Task (HiSoC), a standardized behavioral assessment of social skills, while facial expressions were analyzed using automated facial expression recognition software. Social skills were evaluated by independent blinded raters across three domains (Affect, Odd Behavior and Language, and Social-Interpersonal Skills) and an overall performance rating. Socially disconnected individuals demonstrated significantly poorer performance across all social skill domains and received lower overall performance ratings compared to socially connected individuals. These differences remained significant after controlling for depressive and anxiety symptoms. In contrast, no significant group differences emerged in facial emotional expressions. Exploratory analyses revealed that greater expressions of sadness were associated with poorer social skill ratings, particularly among socially disconnected participants. These findings suggest that social disconnection is reflected in observable social behavior and may represent a distinct interpersonal vulnerability beyond general psychological distress.

articles.title: Patients’ and clinicians’ perspectives on a transdiagnostic Experience Sampling and Ecological Momentary Intervention protocol
articles.authors: Adam Kurilla; Daniel Dančík; Radoslav Lošonský; Alica Hujsová; Sebastián Petreje; Julia; Valentína Cviková; Sára Majsniarová; Petra Brandoburová; Anton Heretik
articles.abstract: Experience Sampling Method (ESM) and Ecological Momentary Interventions (EMI) are promising tools for person-centered mental health care, yet evidence on prolonged transdiagnostic monitoring remains limited. This study explored service users’ and clinicians’ perspectives on the usability and clinical utility of a prolonged transdiagnostic ESM protocol with on-demand EMI content. Within the DISCONNECT project, 84 service users completed debriefing questionnaires following a 30-day protocol with three daily assessments. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with 20 service users and 10 outpatient clinicians. Data were analyzed using content analysis and thematic content analysis. Both stakeholder groups perceived ESM as acceptable and clinically valuable. Participants reported increased self-awareness, improved understanding of symptom patterns, and enhanced opportunities for psychoeducation, treatment planning, and patient–clinician communication. EMI was viewed positively but used infrequently. Participants emphasized the need for personalized assessments, flexible schedules, individualized feedback, and context-sensitive interventions. Key barriers included monitoring burden, clinician time constraints, and organizational challenges. Prolonged transdiagnostic ESM monitoring appears feasible, acceptable, and clinically meaningful, although successful implementation will require greater personalization and integration into routine care.

articles.title: oREV-VN: An extension of the oREV vocabulary test with Rasch-compliant verb comprehension items
articles.authors: Manuel Bohn; Chiara Carraro; Eugenia Rykova; Julia Christin Prein; Natalia Gagarina
articles.abstract: Research on early language development requires high-quality open access instruments to assess individual differences in early language abilities. The oREV is such an instrument to assess receptive vocabulary in three- to eight-year-old German-speaking children. However, it has been limited to nouns. In this paper, we report on the addition of a verb items to the oREV. We constructed a larger initial item pool and collected data from a large sample of children (N = 567) in the target age range. We used data-driven item selection to select Rasch-compliant items which were added to the existing test to form the oREV-VN. There was a strong correlation between performance for verbs and nouns and the combined task showed good reliability. The oREV-VN is freely available via the following link: https://devpsy.web.leuphana.de/orev-vn/.

articles.title: From Car Affinity to Risk Awareness: Understanding Driver Profiles and Shifts in Fatal Five Concerns across West Yorkshire, UK
articles.authors: Ibrahim Ozturk; Phoebe Pitcher; Rosie Revell; Neil Hudson; Esko Lehtonen; Gustav Markkula; Samantha L. Jamson; Natasha Merat
articles.abstract: Road traffic crashes persist as a significant global safety issue, with risky driving behaviours, commonly termed the “Fatal Five” (speeding, driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, distracted driving, careless and reckless driving, and not using seatbelts), continuing to contribute to crashes and fatalities in the UK substantially. This study examines the relationship between driver demographics, attitudes toward driving, driving exposure, and drivers’ perceived level of concern regarding other drivers’ behaviours (in comparison to three years ago) in West Yorkshire. Data were collected from regular drivers (n = 669) and analysed using non-parametric tests and latent class analysis. Overall, distracted driving and careless and reckless driving emerged as the most concerning behaviours, whereas seatbelt non-use exhibited minimal perceived change. Compared to men, women reported that other drivers’ careless and reckless driving, distracted driving, and drink and drug driving are of greater concern. Three distinct profiles of driving attitudes were identified: Car-Positive Utilitarian, Car-Positive Experiential, and Car-Sceptical. While the profiles were mostly similar in terms of demographics, the Car-Positive Experiential profile was more prevalent among men and was more concerned about seatbelt non-use. These findings contribute to understanding drivers’ perceptions of other drivers’ risky behaviours and how driving attitudes relate to these perceptions. The results particularly highlight certain behaviours, such as distracted driving, as future areas of concern in shaping road safety interventions and enforcement strategies.

articles.title: Self-Coherence as a Higher-Order Organizational Process:  Toward an Integrative Theory of Selfhood
articles.authors: Laura Barca
articles.abstract:
How do individuals remain themselves while continuously changing? Contemporary psychological theories have primarily addressed this question through the concept of self-regulation, emphasizing the processes through which organisms adapt to internal and environmental demands. However, adaptation alone does not explain how individuals maintain a coherent sense of self across bodily, relational, and developmental change.
The present paper proposes a theoretical framework of self-coherence that integrates insights from embodied cognition, predictive processing, phenomenology, identity research, and constructivist approaches to personality. Within this framework, self-coherence is conceptualized not as an additional self-construct, but as a higher-order organizational process through which embodied experience, identity processes, personal meaning organization, and self-regulatory mechanisms are dynamically integrated across time. Embodied processes provide a foundational sense of continuity, identity supports the integration of past, present, and future experiences, and personal meaning organizations are reinterpreted as strategies through which continuity is maintained despite change.
Building on this perspective, the paper distinguishes self-coherence from self-regulation. Whereas self-regulation supports adaptive responding, self-coherence concerns the preservation of continuity and intelligibility across experience. Regulatory processes are therefore understood as mechanisms serving a more fundamental organizational task: maintaining a coherent sense of self over time.
By positioning self-coherence as a higher-order organizational principle, the proposed framework offers a conceptual bridge between embodied, developmental, phenomenological, constructivist, and predictive accounts of selfhood, providing a unified framework for understanding how continuity is established, maintained, and restored across change.

articles.title: Construct validity evidence for a Workplace Well-Being Questionnaire Battery adapted to Workers with Intellectual Disabilities (WWQBID)
articles.authors: Elodie Leclerc; Clément Ginoux; Sandrine Isoard-Gautheur
articles.abstract:
Background: Workplace well-being determine job retention among people with intellectual disabilities. No validated instruments exist to measure this construct in this population. This study aimed to validate a battery of questionnaires developed by an expert committee based on JD-R model, DISC-R model, and recovery theories.

Methods: The instrument assesses four well-being indicators, job characteristics, and recovery experiences. Administered to 169 workers in French sheltered employment, data were analysed using confirmatory factor analysis, McDonald’s Omega coefficients, and correlations to examine psychometric validity evidence for the questionnaire battery.

Results: Results provide robust preliminary evidence of the instrument's psychometric validity. Despite removing three items to optimize model fit, construct validity and reliability were confirmed. First evidence of concurrent validity was supported by significant correlations aligning with theoretical expectations.

Conclusions: The three-item dimensions for job characteristics require refinement to ensure validity. Future research with larger-diverse samples is essential to consolidate these findings.

articles.title: On Bias in Detecting Bias: A Signal Detection Analysis of Attributions to Gender Discrimination
articles.authors: Marie Jakob; Anat Shechter; Jimmy Calanchini; Karl Christoph Klauer
articles.abstract: Discrimination is the unjust treatment of people based on their group membership, and detecting discrimination is a necessary first step in countering it. Attributions to discrimination have often been conceptualized in terms of Signal Detection Theory (SDT), with researchers speculating about the role of sensitivity (i.e., observers’ ability to recognize discrimination) and response bias (i.e., their tendency to judge that discrimination is present) in observers’ judgments. Here, we take a first step toward moving beyond conceptual applications to empirical SDT analyses of attributions to discrimination across five studies examining judgments about gender discrimination in corporate pay raise decisions. We find that prototypes (Studies 1 to 3) and their alignment with the actual pattern of decisions (Studies 2 to 4) inform observers’ response bias, reflecting differences in their attribution of unfair outcomes to discrimination (Study 5). In contrast, the extent of harm caused by discriminatory outcomes exerts a small effect on sensitivity and a large effect on response bias (Studies 1 to 3), which reflects observers’ determination of a fair standard for decisions (Study 5). Theoretically, the present work contributes to the causal analysis of central factors shaping attributions to discrimination. Specifically, we shed light on whether effects of these factors are mediated by response bias or sensitivity, and assess their causal origin in either the application of a merit standard or attributional processing.

articles.title: Mega-analysis of the Interoceptive Accuracy Scale (IAS) Structure and its Dispositional Correlates
articles.authors: Ana Neves; Magdalena Pfaff; Robyn Emily Scharte; Raquel Nogueira-Arjona; Giulia Poerio; Dominique Makowski
articles.abstract:
Traditional interoceptive questionnaires show poor convergence, suggesting they tap distinct
constructs. The Interoceptive Accuracy Scale (IAS), developed to separate accuracy from attention, is increasingly popular but psychometrically underexplored. Using a mega-analytic approach across 17 datasets (N > 33,000), we present the most comprehensive evaluation of the IAS to date, covering its factor structure (Study 1) and dispositional correlates (Study 2). Study 1 triangulated results across multiple dimensionality-reduction algorithms, identifying a stable 14-item core organized into 7 distinct item pairs linked to bodily sensations (e.g., Hunger–Thirst). Study 2 examined relationships between the IAS and mood, anxiety, psychopathology, neurodevelopmental traits, personality, and belief-related constructs, finding adaptive traits positively associated and maladaptive traits negatively associated with interoceptive scores. The 14-item version performed comparably to the original 21-item scale, with item-pairing enhancing interpretability; the Hungry-Thirsty and Bruise-Blood-sugar pairs showed the strongest associations. These findings support self-perceived interoceptive ability as a differentiated, system-specific construct.

articles.title: Self-Movement and Other-Movement Estimation Involve Distinct Multisensory Processing
articles.authors: Joshua David Haynes; Andrew P. Jones; Rebecca A. Champion; Boris Otkhmezuri; Graham Bell; Debbie Cane; Rosa Crunkhorn; Monty Silverdale; James Lilleker; Alan Carson
articles.abstract: Integration of multisensory information about self-movement is required both to estimate our own movement, but also to appropriately interpret the movement of other parts of the visual scene. Here we investigated whether the multisensory processes underlying these two tasks are the same or distinct. For a single group of participants, we assessed and compared multi-sensory integration performance in heading estimation and Scene-Relative Other-Movement (SROM) tasks. At the group level, integration was nearer to optimal (i.e. in line with Maximum Likelihood Estimation) in the heading task than the SROM task, though not all participants integrated optimally in either task. We found no evidence for correlation between integration performance across tasks, suggesting multi-sensory processing in the two tasks is distinct. We propose that these results reflect differences in the temporal dynamics of the two tasks and the neural pathways that might support this processing.

articles.title: Is it Reactive Aggression? Using Computational Methods to Explain Evolving Aggression
articles.authors: Fakhra Jabeen; Charlotte Gerritsen; Jan Treur
articles.abstract: Social media is one of the widely used channels for interpersonal communication, and to give personal feedback. However, negative feedback can affect the esteem and mental health of a person. This study presents a computational network model of a humanoid agent who gets (an) inappropriate feedback. It explains how he learns to react with his level of competence in this situation, although he dislikes the feedback. This model can help to detect and handle cyber-aggression and can be used as a building block to provide support to the victims.

articles.title: Item Features Affect How Well People Can Predict Self-Reported Personality Traces of Strangers
articles.authors: Richard Rau; Sebastian Kueppers; Mitja D Back; Michael P. Grosz
articles.abstract: Previous work suggests that self–other agreement in personality judgment is lower for evaluative than for neutral items. We tested whether this pattern generalizes to judgments collected with Who Knows, a mobile app in which users watch brief video clips of strangers and try to predict how targets answered highly concrete personality statements. The dataset comprised >340,000 predictions from >4,000 judges across 296 items and 75 targets. Contrary to prior findings, item evaluativeness did not reduce prediction accuracy with respect to targets’ self-reports. Instead, evaluativeness was positively associated with prediction accuracy for Likert-type items while no effect was observed for binary items. Other coded item features (e.g., base rate and psychological modality) showed no consistent associations with prediction accuracy across formats. We propose two boundary conditions that may help reconcile these results with earlier work: (a) the present item pool is unusually concrete, which alters the distribution and correlates of desirability/evaluativeness, and (b) judges were instructed to predict targets’ self-reports rather than provide their own impressions. Under this instruction, judges may partly rely on between-target differences in self-protective responding when forming predictions. These findings caution against transferring item-feature effects from adjective-based impression paradigms to concrete items and self-report prediction tasks.

articles.title: Predicting Effects of a Computational Empathetic Bot
articles.authors: Fakhra Jabeen
articles.abstract:
Chatbots or virtual agents are getting popular in many domains, for example, in academics, business, or healthcare sectors. They are tailored for specific scenarios, and current studies show that they help in reducing the workload of human experts. This study aims to present an architecture and design for a chatbot named as ‘Leo’. Leo is designed using psychological theories to provide support to people who are seeking emotional support. An experiment was conducted to evaluate the usability of Leo, in which 220 participants provided their feedback after having a conversation with Leo. The selected participants had anxiety and mood related issues reported in the past. Multiple Linear Regression was applied to predict the effects of chatbot towards Multiple Linear Regression was applied to predict the effects of chatbot towards supporting the users by regulating their mental
states. According to the results obtained from the Multiple Linear Regression, using left
a positive influence on the mood of the users, and they recommended Leo.

articles.title: Truth by repetition reliably differs between people over time
articles.authors: Jonas De keersmaecker; Brenton M. Wiernik; Arne Roets; Christian Unkelbach
articles.abstract: Repetition increases the believability of information. Despite the assumedly widespread (harmful) implications of this truth-by-repetition effect (TBRE), little is known about if and how repetition influences people differently. Across two longitudinal experiments (N1 = 113, Belgium; N2 = 430, UK), each consisting of five sessions over 33 days, we collected 108,600 truth judgments. Analyses showed that people differ in their TBRE across time, suggesting a trait-like disposition, which covaried with their Preference for Fluency. Notably, many participants showed stable TBREs that were small or close to zero, whereas relatively few showed large TBREs. We found no evidence for negative TBREs whereby repetition meaningfully decreases believability. The results highlight the value of multiple assessments and challenge the idea of a universal TBRE; repetition increases the believability of information – but not for everyone, and more so for some than others.

articles.title: Sequential Causal Decomposition for Reducing Social Disparities: A Step-by-Step Tutorial Using R
articles.authors: Su Yeon Kim; Soojin Park; Chioun Lee
articles.abstract: Persistent disparities in educational, economic, and health outcomes remain a central challenge for researchers and policymakers. There is a critical need to evaluate how these disparities would reduce if we intervened on specific, modifiable factors. Because social disparities are rarely the result of a single event, they must be analyzed as a sequence. This tutorial provides an accessible guide to implement Sequential Causal Decomposition Analysis in R. We walk applied researchers through the entire analytical steps: formulating research questions, drawing causal diagrams, assessing assumptions, selecting appropriate estimators. Then, we demonstrate implementing the analysis in R with a real-world example. The illustrated method offers users the flexibility to choose between the generalized linear model and machine learning approaches, both of which combined with a triply-robust estimator. As a result, both approaches provide a high-level protection to potential model misspecification. We illustrate this workflow with a simulated data based on the HSLS:09.

articles.title: Receptive Vocabulary Performance Varies with Depression
articles.authors: Michael J. Espero
articles.abstract:
Assessment of health and lifestyle factors in relation with receptive vocabulary across language groups is conducted as parameters are fixed and freed for estimation. Structural equation modeling (SEM) and multi-group structural equation modeling (MGSEM) is used to test the suitability of the model for reproducing the data sample
covariance matrix, the relationships of hypothesized constructs, and the tenability of equivalence across two participant samples. Most demonstrated acceptable global fit indices, however, following group isolated model fitting and re-fitting of MGSEMs (item-factor loadings, covariances, regression paths, and intercepts fixed and freed for estimation) - a parsimonious model predicting the strength of the influence of depression and alcohol experience equally between groups (fixed regression
parameters) on receptive vocabulary was chosen, reflecting structural invariance. The model suggests tenability of the observation of similarly negative impact of depression on receptive vocabulary performance regardless of participant group among included samples.

articles.title: Fixed versus Adaptive Word Lists: Effects on Spelling Outcomes in Adolescents
articles.authors: Dalia Martinez; Danielle Colenbrander; Signy Wegener; Tomohiro Inoue; George K. Georgiou; Anne Castles
articles.abstract: Adaptive word lists, which remove correctly spelled words from practice, may offer an efficient approach by reducing practice time. However, it remains unclear if these potential time-saving benefits may come at a learning cost relative to fixed word lists, in which all words receive equal practice regardless of performance. We examined the cost–benefit trade-off between these two approaches. A total of 113 Grade 7 and 8 students were randomly assigned to either the Adaptive List (AL; n = 58; 55.2% female; Mage= 12.4 years) or the Fixed List group (FL; n = 55; 50.9% female; Mage= 12.4 years) to complete three computer-based spelling practice sessions on 12 words with unpredictable spellings. The number of trials per session differed between conditions: in the AL group, correctly spelled words were dropped from further practice (12 to 36 trials per session); the FL group practiced each word three times per session over 36 trials. Students were assessed at pre-test, post-test, and two weeks later at follow-up on the spelling and reading of trained and untrained items. Results of generalized mixed-effects modeling revealed that practice sessions were significantly shorter for the AL group.
