The range of ICC(A, K) is the union of the interval from minus infinity to 1 and the interval from N to infinity: (-∞,1]∪[N,∞). The range of ICC(A, 1) is the interval from -1/(K-1) to 1. We also study the relationship between ICC(A, 1), ICC(C, 1) and the Pearson correlation for K = 2. Under the usual ANOVA assumptions, if there are strong row effects but no column effects and K = 2, then the probability that ICC(A, 1) > ICC(C, 1) lies between 0.5 and 0.6827. Furthermore, we show that ICC(C, 1) for K = 2 is a function of the Pearson correlation and the two standard deviations, and that ICC(C, 1) does not exceed the Pearson correlation.

articles.title: People make inferences from policy decisions regarding default options:  Replication and extension of McKenzie et al. (2006)
articles.authors: Xiaoru Teng; Ka Wai LAU; Tsz King Ng; Chan Ka Po; Yeung Wing; Siu Kit Yeung; Gilad Feldman
articles.abstract: Default effect refers to the phenomenon that people tend to choose the default option in a choice-set. McKenzie et al. (2006) showed that one mechanism for the effect is that defaults are perceived as an implicit endorsement and a recommended option by the policy makers that set the defaults. In a pre-registered experiment with a Prolific online sample from the United States (N = 602), we conducted replications and extensions of Studies 1, 2, and 3 from McKenzie et al. (2006). We concluded a successful replication of all three studies, with support for their findings showing that: 1) people who are more willing to be organ donors are more likely to hold views that others should as well, and to think that enrollment should be the default, and that in turn: 2) people make inferences from policymaker’s set defaults (Study 2: organ donations; Study 3: retirement plans) regarding the policymakers’ own preferences regarding enrollment, and their views regarding whether others should enroll. In our extension we added a control condition with no defaults, finding that 1) people still make inferences about choice architects’ attitudes, even when defaults were not set, and 2) that setting defaults to not act (not enroll) is perceived as closer to no defaults than setting defaults to act (to enroll). Materials, data, and code are available on: https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/E5K4J.

articles.title: Intentional Forest Exposure Scale: Initial development of two questionnaires
articles.authors: Yasushi Suko; Reeta Kankaanpää; Ayako Toko
articles.abstract:
Background and objectives
Most self-report measures of nature exposure have relied on a vague term “nature.” As forests offer humans various health-related and economic benefits, a more precise definition of “nature” in such measures to refer to forested areas would make the research focus more precise. This study therefore developed and preliminarily validated two measures (for adulthood and childhood): the Intentional Forest Exposure Scale (IFES) and the Retrospective Intentional Forest Exposure Scale (R-IFES), also addressing aspects of recreation and psychological restoration.

Methods
An online survey involving N = 99 university students’ responses (with 5,000 resamples bootstrapping) was used to explore the factor structure (using exploratory factor analysis; EFA) of IFES and R-IFES (initially designed to include six items each). These two questionnaires are identical in structure except the time frame: for IFES the last 12 months, and for R-IFES during childhood (5–10 years). Internal reliability and convergent, discriminant, and criterion validity were also tested. Face and construct validity were descriptively checked. 

Results
The EFA showed all six items loading onto a single factor for both IFES and R-IFES, but only one of the six R-IFES items yielded a poor loading of 0.39 (the loadings of the remaining 11 items were above 0.65). We therefore removed this R-IFES item and the corresponding IFES item, thereby keeping a parallel structure between the two measures. The five-item IFES and R-IFES were thus developed.
Internal validity was also high for both IFES and R-IFES, but convergent, discriminant, and criterion validity were weaker for R-IFES than for IFES. Additionally, R-IFES scores were more concentrated on higher values than IFES scores.

Conclusions
At least the five-item IFES would suffice to measure adult forest exposure. IFES can be used, e.g., in studies on forest recreation and pro-environmental behavior.

articles.title: Individual differences and motives for the acceptance of cognitive enhancement: A mixed-methods investigation
articles.authors: Sandra Grinschgl; Larissa Kohlmeier; Aljoscha Neubauer
articles.abstract:
Despite a long history of human efforts to enhance cognitive abilities such as attention, memory, and intelligence, the proliferation of (new) technological and medical methods—including brain stimulation, pharmaceutical interventions, and gamified brain training—has renewed scientific and public interest in cognitive enhancement, even as evidence for their effectiveness in improving broader cognitive abilities remains inconsistent. Understanding who would be willing to enhance their cognitive abilities and why is crucial as these cognitive enhancement methods become more popular and accessible in society.
Thus, in two preregistered lab studies, we investigated individuals’ acceptance (i.e., hypothetical willingness to make use) of various cognitive enhancement methods, tested predictors thereof, and explored individuals’ motives to utilize cognitive enhancement methods or be reluctant to do so. 
In Study 1 (N = 203), we found considerably higher acceptance for more active (i.e., more involved) than passive cognitive enhancement methods. Lower age, greater interest in science fiction, and stronger investigative interests (partially) predicted participants’ acceptance of cognitive enhancement methods, while personality traits, self-assessed and measured intelligence did not.
In Study 2 (N = 197), we aimed to (conceptually) replicate key findings from Study 1, and in addition qualitatively assess the motives for the acceptance and rejection of cognitive enhancement methods. Our findings confirmed the higher acceptance of active than passive cognitive enhancement methods. Additionally, we found that greater interest in science fiction consistently predicted participants’ acceptance of cognitive enhancement, while personality traits and (self-assessed) intelligence did not show consistent associations. Critically, these quantitative effects were rather small. Qualitative analyses additionally provided exploratory insights into the motives for accepting or rejecting active and passive cognitive enhancement methods.
 Across these two studies, acceptance of cognitive enhancement seems to be more closely linked to specific interests than to personality traits or (self-assessed) abilities. We emphasize the importance of studying situational factors, such as person-environment fit, to better understand the willingness to make use of cognitive enhancement.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the Cross-Modal Inventory of Common Factors and Facilitative Interpersonal Skills (CMICF): A Patient-Reported Measure for Psychiatric and Psychotherapeutic Care
articles.authors: André Kerber; Phileas Heim; Thomas Munder; Carmen Schaeuffele; Johannes Zimmermann; Ulrike Maaß; Javier Sanchez Hernandez; Birgit Deckert; Christine Knaevelsrud; Eva M. Klein
articles.abstract: Common factors and facilitative interpersonal skills are central to mental health treatment outcomes, yet existing assessments remain fragmented across constructs, modalities, and rater perspectives. We developed and validated the Cross-Modal Inventory for Common Factors and Facilitative Interpersonal Skills (CMICF), a brief patient-reported measure for psychotherapeutic and psychiatric settings. Data came from two clinical samples receiving mental health treatment (construction: N = 707; validation: N = 1,230; øage = 36.7[18-79], 78.7% female). An initial 101-item pool covering 14 evidence-based relational and interpersonal skill domains was reduced using ant colony optimization and expert-guided scale merging. The final 33-item instrument comprised nine factors: alliance bond capacity, goals/collaboration, congruence, emotional expression, hope/positive expectations, treatment credibility, managing countertransference, rupture-repair responsiveness, and verbal fluency. Confirmatory factor analyses showed excellent model fit in the construction sample (CFI = .974, RMSEA = .036, SRMR = .029) and validation sample (CFI = .959, RMSEA = .045, SRMR = .036). Internal consistency was good to excellent for most subscales (ω = .73–.91), except congruence (ω = .58). Configural, metric, scalar, and strict invariance were supported across treatment settings and personality dysfunction severity. Convergent validity was demonstrated through theoretically consistent associations with established measures of alliance and related relational constructs. Discriminant validity was supported by small negative associations with psychopathology (all |r| < .30). Alliance bond capacity showed partly strong intercorrelations, indicating that specific relational processes may be anchored in the overall therapeutic bond. The CMICF provides a psychometrically robust, cross-modal assessment of relational common factors and facilitative interpersonal skills from the patient perspective in psychotherapy and psychiatric care.

articles.title: Regulating Self-Interest: The Effect of Anodal tDCS over the Right Dorsolateral 1 Prefrontal Cortex in the Ultimatum Game
articles.authors: Haim Assor; Jonathan slater; Eliran Halali
articles.abstract: In many social situations, a conflict exists between self-interest and social considerations such as fairness and reciprocity. Previous research suggests that self-interested behavior is an automatic response, requiring cognitive control to be regulated. Consistent with this view, neuroeconomics findings implicate the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (rDLPFC) in regulating self-interested behavior and implementing social preferences. Whereas prior neuromodulation studies have shown that inhibition or disruption of the rDLPFC increases self-interested behavior, studies examining the effects of enhancing rDLPFC activity have yielded inconsistent results. Moreover, it remains unclear whether such enhancement promotes responses to intentional unfairness alone or also to inequality per se. In the present study, we applied anodal transcranial direct-current stimulation (tDCS) over the rDLPFC to reexamine its causal role in regulating self-interested behavior among ultimatum game responders. In addition, we tested whether this manipulation promotes responses to both intentional unfairness and inequality resulting from an intention-free process. The results show that anodal stimulation increased rejection rates of unfair offers, regardless of whether they were proposed by a human partner or randomly generated by a computer. These findings suggest that rDLPFC activity supports the implementation of social preferences more broadly, promoting both reciprocal fairness and inequality aversion. When considered alongside prior findings demonstrating an opposite effect of anodal stimulation applied to an adjacent lateral prefrontal region using a similar paradigm, the results point to distinct and complementary roles of lateral prefrontal regions in managing the conflict between self-interest and social preferences. We discuss implications for dual-process accounts of social decision-making.

articles.title: Loneliness and Suicidal Ideation in the General Adult Population in Slovak Republic – Results from DISCONNECT study
articles.authors: Veronika Boleková; Michal Hajdúk; Jakub Januška
articles.abstract:
Background: Population-representative data on suicidal ideation and its social determinants remain scarce in Central and Eastern Europe; therefore, this study examined the association of loneliness and social isolation with suicidal ideation in a representative sample of Slovak adults.
Methods: Data for the present study were drawn from the DISCONNECT project, a population-based study that employed face-to-face interviews with a demographically representative sample of adults in the Slovak Republic (N = 3006). Suicidal ideation was assessed using item 9 of the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), alongside self-report measures of loneliness, social isolation, and depressive symptoms.
Results: Suicidal ideation was reported by 14.6% of participants (95% CI: 13.3–15.9) over the last two weeks. Depression emerged as the strongest predictor of suicidal ideation (OR = 21.22, 95% CI: 16.03–28.08, p < .001), while current treatment for a mental health condition was also independently associated with increased odds (OR = 2.07, 95% CI: 1.32–3.25, p = .001). Both loneliness (OR = 2.93, 95% CI: 2.21–3.88, p < .001) and social isolation (OR = 1.65, 95% CI: 1.22–2.23, p = .001) were independently associated with suicidal ideation, with loneliness showing the stronger association.
Conclusion: Loneliness was independently associated with suicidal ideation beyond depression and social isolation, highlighting subjective social disconnection as a potential target for suicide prevention strategies.

articles.title: Handling Fully Irrelevant vs. Potentially Relevant Distractors as a Function of Perceptual Load
articles.authors: Greta Manini; Francisco Garre-Frutos; Fabiano Botta; Elisa Martín-Arévalo; Juan Lupiáñez
articles.abstract: In a previous study, we investigated how task performance is affected by fully irrelevant distractors (sharing neither features nor location with the target) vs. potentially relevant distractors (sharing features and location with it), as a function of perceptual load. We observed a systematic dissociation: the effect of potentially relevant distractors increased under high-load, whereas the effect of fully irrelevant distractors remained unchanged. However, it is unclear whether this dissociation reflected a difference between distractor types or instead resulted from two methodological factors. The present study addressed this question by testing whether the double dissociation persists even when key experimental design aspects are controlled. Namely, (i) the effectiveness of the perceptual load manipulation, and (ii) the comparability between distractor effects. In Experiment 1, we replicated the perceptual load pattern and observed the double dissociation. The effect of fully irrelevant distractors decreased under high-load, whereas that of potentially relevant distractors increased. Three subsequent experiments systematically manipulated task parameters that differ between the original perceptual load task and our previous work, to assess their contribution to an effective load manipulation. Despite these variations, the double dissociation remained robust. In a final experiment, we further improved the comparability between the effects of each distractor type by using a common baseline measure, confirming that perceptual load modulates the effects of fully irrelevant vs. potentially relevant distractors in different ways. These findings suggest that distractor processing depends critically on its task relevance, while also highlighting  the need to investigate the boundary conditions of perceptual load effects.

articles.title: Religious and Spiritual Experiences (RSEs) of Demons, Devils, and Satan: An Analysis of Features and Relationships to Psychopathology and Culture
articles.authors: Ari Brouwer; Charles L Raison
articles.abstract: This paper examines first-person experiences of demons, devils, and Satan contained in the Alister Hardy RERC database of religious and spiritual experiences (RSEs). Word frequency analysis, content analysis, and phenomenological analysis are used to characterize the features of demonic experiences and how they relate to psychopathology and religious/cultural affiliations. Results show that demonic experiences are less common than positively valenced RSEs in the RERC database, and that they disproportionately reflect psychotic-like experiences and mental health difficulties. Personal experiences of demonic forces also appear to be related to dreams and parasomnias (e.g., sleep paralysis), while reference to the possession/exorcism of other persons is associated with Pentecostal/evangelical affiliation. Results suggest that personal experiences of demonic forces are more associated with mental health difficulties than with particular religious or cultural affiliations within the current sample.

articles.title: The Anhedonia of Collective Trauma: A Rise in Specific Depression Symptoms Among Israeli Treatment-Seekers After October 7, 2023
articles.authors: Mor Bar; Elad Refoua; Yuval Holzman; Shahar Weisel; Inbar Feinestein; Gal Lazarus; Aaron Fisher; Eshkol Rafaeli
articles.abstract: Collective traumas impose profound psychological burdens, yet most research on their aftermath has focused on acute stress and anxiety responses rather than depressive sequelae. The October 7, 2023 attacks and ensuing crisis in Israel provide a rare opportunity to examine the specific symptomatic profile of collective trauma among individuals experiencing distress. Israeli adults (N=1,078) who sought psychological treatment through a university-based brief intervention study between December 2022 and January 2026 completed the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS-21). Of these, 382 (35.4%) completed intake assessments before and 696 (64.6%) after October 7, 2023. Depression scores were significantly higher among post-October 7 treatment seekers, whereas anxiety and stress levels did not differ between groups. Item-level analyses revealed that this increase was driven specifically by anhedonic symptoms, particularly diminished initiative and reduced enthusiasm. Correspondingly, the proportion of participants classified in the “extremely severe” depression range rose from 36.9% before the attacks to 45.0% afterward. Importantly, the pattern of associations among symptoms did not differ significantly between periods, suggesting that the collective trauma did not alter the structure of distress but selectively intensified motivational depletion. These findings suggest that sustained collective trauma may manifest not primarily as heightened arousal, but as an anhedonic form of depression characterized by loss of agency and motivation. Post-crisis mental health services may therefore benefit from emphasizing interventions targeting motivational withdrawal, such as behavioral activation and meaning-centered approaches.

articles.title: Touch of Loneliness Hurts the Most: The Implications of Loneliness and Social Touch
articles.authors: Zofia Kurasz; Michał Krzyżowski; Bartosz Baran; Marek Krzystanek
articles.abstract: Loneliness is increasingly prevalent in emerging adults and is theorised to engage threat-related biology that overlaps with nociceptive processing. We tested whether social connectedness and related cognitive-affective traits predict endogenous pain inhibition, and whether hedonic appraisal of affective (C-tactile targeted) touch tracks these social variables or links to pain modulation. In February 2025, 119 healthy Generation Z participants (18-27 years; median age = 22) completed questionnaires indexing social network structure, contact quality, perceived social support, loneliness, self-concept clarity, early maladaptive schemas, depressive symptoms, and perceived stress. Participants then rated the pleasantness of standardised slow forearm brushing (~3 cm/s; 60 s), and underwent conditioned pain modulation (CPM) using pressure pain thresholds before and after a tonic cuff-conditioning stimulus. In the multivariable CPM model, higher loneliness was associated with weaker CPM (b = -0,90, 95% CI [-1,65, -0,15], p = 0,019) after adjustment for demographics, baseline pain threshold, stress, depression, self-concept clarity, perceived support, contact quality, and Young's schemas. Contrary to prediction, a schemas’ domain reflecting relational disconnection with affective inhibition showed a positive association with CPM (b = 11,28, 95% CI [2,91, 19,65], p = 0,009). Touch pleasantness was poorly explained by psychosocial variables; sex was the only reliable predictor (males lower; b = -0,90, 95% CI [-1,67, -0,13], p = 0,022). Touch pleasantness did not predict CPM in the model (p = 0,393). Overall, in healthy emerging adults, subjective loneliness, rather than perceived support or pleasantness of affective touch, was the most consistent psychosocial correlate of reduced endogenous pain inhibition.

articles.title: Early caregiving adversities and parental context effects on adolescents’ momentary emotion differentiation
articles.authors: Lior Abramson; Anna Vannucci; Camila Vicioso; Daniela Juarez; Erin Joyce; Andrea Fields; Erica Niemiec; Lisa Gibson; Niall Bolger; Nim Tottenham
articles.abstract: Emotion differentiation—the ability to identify specific experienced emotions—associates with psychological health. Although theorized to develop through caregiving experiences, contextual effects on emotion differentiation in developmental populations have rarely been investigated, particularly among youth who have experienced disruptions in early caregiving. Here, we examined how adolescents’ momentary emotion differentiation varies across socioemotional contexts and whether these patterns differ following exposure to severe caregiving-related early adversities. Adolescents aged 10–17 with or without caregiving-related early adversities (N=81; mean age=13.49, SD = 1.97; 53% male/47% female, 9%-Asian American, 28%-black, 34%-white, 28%-other) completed 7-day ecological momentary assessment (4–5 surveys/day), reporting emotions and social contexts. At the trait level, there were no significant group differences in emotion differentiation. At the momentary level, negative momentary emotion differentiation decreased following negative parent-related contexts compared to other contexts. However, the momentary emotions’ standard deviation indicated also differentiated phasic intensification of a few negative emotions. Importantly, exposure to caregiving-related early adversities attenuated these contextual effects, L.Ratio(3) = 9.178 - 11.263. These findings provide initial evidence linking severe caregiving-related early adversities to affective dynamic processes and raise the hypothesis that early caregiving disruptions mitigate momentary parental modulation effects on emotion differentiation.

articles.title: Is Democracy Worth the Effort? Seeing the Other Side as Willing Strengthens Pro-Democratic Motivation
articles.authors: Olaf Borghi; Manos Tsakiris; Mariana Von Mohr
articles.abstract: Democracy takes effort, and these efforts do not always benefit one's own political side. Across two preregistered studies (total N = 916), we used computational modeling of effortful behavior to measure US partisans' motivation to strengthen democratic engagement in rural (traditionally Republican-leaning), urban (traditionally Democrat-leaning), or all US areas. Study 1 showed that participants were generally willing to make pro-democratic efforts, but less so when efforts benefited the out-group, particularly among more affectively polarized participants. We hypothesized that this asymmetry is sustained by misperceptions about out-partisans' willingness to engage in cross-partisan pro-democratic efforts. Study 2 showed that most participants (87%) underestimated out-party effort willingness. Importantly, correcting this misperception increased motivation to support out-group areas without reducing in-group motivation. These findings suggest that cross-partisan pro-democratic motivation is common but partly obscured by misperceptions about out-partisans' willingness. Making this willingness visible might help sustain the collective effort democracy depends on.

articles.title: Positive Emotion Regulation Strategies Predict Suicidal Thoughts across and within Individuals: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
articles.authors: Yael Millgram; Matthew Nock
articles.abstract: Suicidal thoughts are often attributed to negative emotions and difficulties regulating them. Although reduced positive emotions (anhedonia) has long been linked to suicide, much less is known about the role of positive emotion regulation in suicidal thoughts and behaviours. We examined positive emotional experience and regulation using a 7-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) study (N=206; 4,728 observations) comparing individuals with suicidal thoughts, psychiatric controls, and healthy controls. Participants with suicidal thoughts reported lower positive emotion than both control groups and were more likely to use strategies aimed at decreasing positive affect (e.g., distraction from positive emotions, dampening, suppression). No group differences emerged in efforts to increase positive emotion (e.g., positive rumination, reappraisal, positive expression). Within the suicidal group, participants who used strategies that decrease positive emotion to a greater extent experienced more severe suicidal thoughts, whereas greater use of strategies that increase positive emotion predicted less severe momentary suicidal thinking.

articles.title: TRACKING MENTAL HEALTH AND COGNITIVE CHANGES FOLLOWING THE AUSTRALIAN SOCIAL MEDIA MINIMUM AGE LEGISLATION: A 4-WAVE LONGITUDINAL STUDY PROTOCOL
articles.authors: Anna Bilston; Lyndsay Brown; Xu Qi; Emily Morgan; Yasmin Hasan; Sarah Stevens; Weike Wang; Aliza Werner-Seidler; Susanne Schweizer
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Over the last two decades, increases in adolescent mental health difficulties have been recorded alongside a rapid expansion in social media use. In response to these trends, on December 10, 2025, the Australian government implemented Social Media Minimum Age legislation prohibiting Australians under the age of 16 years from having social media accounts. The introduction of this legislation allows for a unique quasi-experimental investigation of the impact of social media use on adolescent mental health, and the mechanisms through which social media may impact young Australian adolescents’ mental health, wellbeing and cognition.

Methods and analysis: To assess the relationship between social media use and young adolescents’ mental health, wellbeing and cognition, a longitudinal, mixed-methods approach will be taken. 422 young people (aged 10-15 years) and parent/carer dyads were recruited and completed baseline assessment before the implementation of the Social Media Minimum Age legislation (October-December, 2025). Three follow-up assessments will then be completed after 6- (Apr-May 2026), 12- (Oct-Nov 2026) and 18-months (Apr-May 2027). Data from parent/carer and adolescent surveys, ecological momentary assessments (EMA), cognitive tasks and smartphone recorded screenshots of social media and messaging app use across each of these timepoints will be analysed. Linear regression analyses will be used to assess correlational relationships between social media experiences and responses and mental health outcomes, as well as how individual factors moderate these relationships. To assess these relationships over time, and between momentary experiences and responses (EMA data) and more stable trait-based (individual) and environmental (parenting and government restrictions) factors, we will use linear mixed-effects models and mixed-effects segmented regression.  

Ethics and dissemination: This study has been approved by the UNSW Human Research Ethics Committee (iRECS 9197). Findings from this study will help identify potential opportunities for interventions at an individual, micro (parental) and macro (governmental) level, to mitigate any negative impacts of social media for younger adolescents. Results will be disseminated through peer-reviewed publications, conference presentations, public engagement opportunities and researcher associated websites. After each assessment timepoint, core findings will also be distilled into engaging formats such as infographics and videos, which will be shared with young people and their parent/carer.

articles.title: There is only one correct analysis
articles.authors: Daniel Lakens; Sajedeh Rasti; NA
articles.abstract: Multimodel inference (such as multiverse analysis and many-analyst projects) is a family of sensitivity analyses that examines variation in results across alternative auxiliary assumptions of a research question. The observed variation in multimodel inferences has given rise to the idea that there is no single correct analysis, and that researchers should examine variability across a large range of plausible models instead. In this contribution, we critically reflect on the epistemological assumptions and consequences of this idea. Specifically, we argue that variation across alternative auxiliary assumptions can reveal uncertainty about how to validly test a hypothesis, but it cannot substitute for the epistemic warrant required to justify those assumptions. This is because, once a research question is translated into a specific empirical test by specifying the relevant auxiliary assumptions, there is a fact of the matter about which statistical analysis correctly implements that test and deviations from that analysis are not made epistemically acceptable merely by being included in a distribution. Thus, robustness across alternative sets of auxiliary assumptions with differing levels of justification does not, by itself, show that a theoretical claim is epistemically warranted. Researchers should invest in the empirical groundwork needed to identify valid tests of their hypotheses, rather than embracing variability across analyses and incorporating epistemic uncertainty into statistical inferences.

articles.title: Koshering Psychedelics: Ayahuasca in the Ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) Jewish World
articles.authors: Jonathan David; Aviva Berkovich-Ohana; ‪Yair Dor-Ziderman‬‏; Michael Fine; Hagit Bonny-Noach; Leor Roseman
articles.abstract: Background: Despite the rapid growth of psychedelic research, limited attention has been given to how psychedelics are used, experienced and integrated within highly conservative religious communities, and in Jewish contexts more broadly. This study addresses this gap by examining ayahuasca use among the ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) community, a highly religious, conservative, and collectivist Jewish group. Methods: This study used inductive thematic analysis of interviews with Haredi participants in Israel and the US (N = 23; age range: 21–60; 8 women), supplemented by participant observation and analysis of religious materials. Results: Six themes were identified: (a) settings: Haredi ayahuasca ceremonies were adapted to Jewish contexts; (b) motivations: use was primarily therapeutic; (c) acute ayahuasca experiences: experiences included distinct Jewish and Jewish mystical visionary content; (d) longer term religious effects: ayahuasca was linked to strengthened belief, connection to Judaism, and changes in religious practice; (e) religious tensions: included ayahuasca’s perceived foreignness to Haredi life, concerns about idolatry, mixed gender participation, and competing structures of authority (experience vs. text); and (f) strategies for addressing tensions: included medicalization, “koshering” ayahuasca’s set, setting, and experience (i.e., making them religiously permissible), and framing ceremonies as liminal spaces. Conclusions: This study broadens the scope of psychedelic science by examining ayahuasca use in a highly religious Orthodox context rarely included in the field. The findings show how ayahuasca settings, experiences, and long term effects are translated through Haredi and Jewish structures, involve religious tensions and resolutions, and highlight psychedelics’ contextual flexibility and continued diffusion into understudied populations.

articles.title: Heartbeat perception is dissociable into subjective detection and temporal judgement
articles.authors: Yusuke Haruki; Keisuke Suzuki; Yuri Terasawa; Kenji Ogawa; Olaf Blanke; Yuko Yotsumoto
articles.abstract: Heartbeat perception is widely studied as a behavioural index of interoception, yet different cardiac tasks share little variance, and what each task measures remains unclear. We developed the oneshot heartbeat detection task, in which each trial yields a subjective heartbeat detection response and, after a variable waiting interval, a cardiovisual synchrony judgement. As an exteroceptive control, the same trial structure was also implemented in a matched sound condition. We then asked how the two within-trial measures relate to each other and to conventional heartbeat counting scores obtained in the same sessions. Across three experiments (n = 43, 24, 21), subjective heartbeat detection on a given trial increased confidence in the synchrony judgement but not accuracy. Synchrony sensitivity remained stable across waiting intervals from 2 to 22 heartbeats. Cross-study analysis revealed that heartbeat counting performance was related to how frequently participants detected heartbeats and to their synchrony response criterion, but not to the synchrony sensitivity; in Experiment 1, the analogous indices in a matched sound condition showed no such relation. These data show that subjective detection and temporal judgement of heartbeats are dissociable components of heartbeat perception. We argue that weak convergence across cardiac paradigms may reflect this component structure rather than failures of measurement.

articles.title: The Development of Future-Self Concepts in Adolescence
articles.authors: Peggy Yin; Hal Hershfield
articles.abstract:
Mainstream media paints adolescents as moody, mean, and myopic in their decision-making and risk-taking, unable to act with their future selves in mind. Yet, adolescents may merely be sensing their future selves as distant and stranger-like, irrelevant to the goals and motivations of who they are now. This handbook chapter highlights the complex developmental processes through which young people form, refine, and prioritize their future selves. Drawing on a broad spectrum of interdisciplinary literature, we examine how adolescents aged 10 to 24 cultivate a
sense of continuity between their present and future selves, a key factor in long-term decision-making and overall well-being. Additionally, the chapter reviews innovative interventions, ranging from narrative exercises and role-play activities to immersive technologies like virtual reality and conversational AI, that help bridge the psychological gap between the present self and the future self. By synthesizing evidence from developmental psychology, neuroscience, human-computer interaction, and social psychology, it outlines promising directions for future research and practical applications aimed at empowering adolescents to make choices that promote sustained health, education, and financial stability.

articles.title: Menarche and ADHD: Associations Between Puberty, ADHD Traits, and Mental Health in Female Adolescents
articles.authors: Emily Friedel; Lisa Grech; David Skvarc; Caroline Gurvich; Emma McIntosh; Bethany Norton; Linda Baker; Cammy Griffin; Nandita Vijayakumar; Tim Silk
articles.abstract:
Background: Adolescents with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) may be more vulnerable to mental health challenges during puberty, and some evidence suggests that puberty impacts ADHD traits. However, pubertal influences on adolescents assigned female at birth (AFAB) with ADHD are poorly understood. We investigated how menarche relates to ADHD traits, along with its moderating effects on mental health outcomes based on ADHD status or ADHD traits.    
Methods: This study used Adolescent Brain and Cognitive DevelopmentSM Study data from baseline to wave 3 (N = 5677; 100% female; baseline age M = 9.9, SD = 0.6; 51% non-Hispanic white). ADHD traits, internalising difficulties, and externalising difficulties were operationalised using the Child Behavior Checklist. Bayesian generalised linear mixed models were conducted to examine cross-sectional and longitudinal associations between menarche status and ADHD traits, along with moderating effects of menarche status on mental health according to ADHD status or traits. Comparative analyses were run with breast development. 
Results: Menarche status had a null association with concurrent ADHD traits, but post-menarche (versus pre-menarche) adolescents had higher ADHD traits the following year. Complete breast development (versus not started) predicted higher ADHD traits the next year. Menarche status and breast development moderated internalising and externalising difficulties according to ADHD status: the non-ADHD group had modestly higher mental health challenges with more advanced pubertal status, whereas there was no change for the ADHD group. Positive associations between ADHD traits and mental health challenges were marginally stronger post-menarche.  
Conclusions: Longitudinal findings provide preliminary evidence that female-specific markers of gonadarche plays a role in elevated ADHD trait expression. Greater mental health challenges with more advanced gonadarche were only observed in non-ADHD adolescents, but future research should investigate potential for ADHD subgroups that are especially vulnerable to pubertal influences on mental health.

articles.title: Understanding the Hidden Diversity of Socioeconomically Disadvantaged Adolescents: Developmental Pathways of Flourishing and Vulnerability
articles.authors: Azilawati Jamaludin; Aik Lim Tan; Sandeep Singh; Kenneth Poon Kin Loong
articles.abstract: Socioeconomic disadvantage is often associated with poorer developmental outcomes, yet relatively little is known about why adolescents exposed to similar adversity follow markedly different developmental pathways. Drawing on data from the Singapore DREAMS longitudinal cohort (N =7,156), including 1,634 adolescents from the bottom 20% of the socioeconomic strata (B20), this study investigated developmental heterogeneity among socioeconomically disadvantaged students. Latent profile analysis conducted on adolescents with complete psychosocial indicators (n=611) identified five distinct developmental pathways ranging from Broad Psychosocial Vulnerability to Flourishing Despite Disadvantage. Differences in academic attainment and school performance across pathways were statistically significant but modest, whereas psychosocial differences were substantial, often exceeding one standard deviation. Profile membership predicted a wide range of subsequent flourishing outcomes, including academic engagement, academic self-concept, academic buoyancy, identity commitment, connectedness, optimism, and perseverance. Furthermore, flourishing adolescents from disadvantaged backgrounds demonstrated psychosocial functioning largely comparable to flourishing adolescents from more advantaged backgrounds despite occupying dramatically different socioeconomic positions. The findings challenge deficit-oriented accounts of disadvantage and suggest that human flourishing under adversity represents a distinct and measurable developmental phenomenon. Understanding the psychosocial conditions associated with flourishing may be as important as understanding the factors that place adolescents at risk.

articles.title: On Great Ape Personality Structure: Problems in Minkov (2026)
articles.authors: Alexander Weiss
articles.abstract: A recent paper (Minkov, 2026) claimed that great ape personality is not well-described by the five personality dimensions found in humans, those being Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness to Experience, Agreeableness, and Conscientiousness. To support this claim, the author analyzed personality factor structures obtained in studies of great apes in which personality was assessed using rating scales. The analyses tested for ‘configural invariance’ by computing the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor as in humans in chimpanzees, orangutans, mountain gorillas, and bonobos, and the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor across four studies of chimpanzees. The paper also reviewed the evidence for Conscientiousness in chimpanzees, including by citing the results of studies that used other ratings scales, and concluded that this factor’s evidentiary basis was weak. The paper concluded by proposing an alternative approach to studying personality in great apes. This commentary exposes fundamental problems with the article. First, I show that the paper’s author mischaracterized the research program of comparative psychologists who study personality. Second, I review the evidence the paper uses to support its claim that there is little evidence for configural invariance, highlighting problems and errors in the analyses and reporting of results. Third, I review the evidence presented against the presence of Conscientiousness in chimpanzees and find it problematic. Finally, I demonstrate that the literature review lacks due care. I conclude by dispelling a misunderstanding about comparative research on personality and reaffirm the importance and promise of pluralism in the study of the phenomenon.

articles.title: EEG and self-report reveal intact cognitive appraisal of others’ pain but reduced affective and sensorimotor resonance in adults with autism spectrum disorder
articles.authors: Igor Riečanský; Ekaterina Pronizius; Martin Marko; Milan Mitka; Andreas de Lorenzo; Claus Lamm
articles.abstract:
Empathy for pain—sharing another person’s affective state while maintaining self–other distinction—
triggers sensorimotor and cognitive-emotional responses in the observer. We investigated whether these
processes differ in adults with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and healthy neurotypical controls (HC).
Twenty adults with ASD and 21 matched HC viewed images of a hand being either gently touched with
a cotton bud or injected with a needle. For injection trials, participants were informed whether the target
could feel pain (no anesthesia) or not (local anesthesia), allowing assessment of the influence of target
pain sensitivity on empathic responses. EEG recorded sensorimotor engagement via event-related
desynchronization (ERD) of central mu (7–13 Hz) and beta (14–24 Hz) rhythms. Participants also rated
the target’s pain and their own unpleasantness. Both groups rated pain-sensitive injections as more
painful than pain-insensitive injections, with no group differences, indicating comparable appraisal of
others’ pain. In contrast, self-reported unpleasantness and ERD was modulated by target pain sensitivity
only in HC, who reported greater unpleasantness and stronger mu ERD when the target could feel pain
vs. not; this modulation was absent in ASD. No group differences emerged for touch or pain-insensitive
injections, and viewing perspective (egocentric vs. altercentric) did not differentially affect responses.
These findings suggest intact cognitive appraisal of others’ pain in ASD but reduced affective and
sensorimotor resonance. The absence of perspective-related effects further indicates preserved self–
other distinction, pointing to a selective alteration in affective sharing rather than a global empathy
impairment.

articles.title: Measuring the reflective self and experiential self  in self-transcendent experiences
articles.authors: Valerie van Mulukom
articles.abstract: This chapter proposes that self-transcendent experiences can be usefully understood through the distinction between the reflective and experiential self, originally articulated by William James and developed in contemporary philosophy and neuroscience. The reflective self involves conscious, narrative self-referential processing, while the experiential self refers to affective processing and meta-awareness which typically follows bodily consciousness. Drawing on evidence from psychedelics, meditation, and awe research, it is suggested that self-transcendence involves a decrease of the reflective self state (ego dissolution) and a corresponding increase in experiential self state (immersion), supported by affective salience processing and associated with meta-awareness, leading to feelings of connectedness. In doing so, the framework reconciles the paradox of feeling “smaller yet larger” in self-transcendental experiences. This dual-self framework offers a pragmatic model for researching the phenomenology of self-transcendence in PRSM experiences, encouraging a building block approach by delineating the processes which together form the complex phenomenon of self-transcendence and which can be measured individually through psychological scales, but the extents of which can vary across different types of PRSM experiences and cultural contexts.

articles.title: United in Wonder: Shared Awe Is Associated With Satisfaction and Depth in Romantic Relationships
articles.authors: Matteo Perini; Lou Mier; Jasper H. B. de Groot; Hein van Schie; Johan Karremans
articles.abstract: Shared awe, a co-experience of vastness and meaning, may strengthen romantic bonds beyond individual awe. Across two preregistered cross-sectional studies of partnered adults (Study 1, N = 198; Study 2, N = 306), participants reported shared awe with their partner, dispositional awe, relationship satisfaction, and self–other overlap; Study 2 additionally assessed willingness to sacrifice, ego decentering, relationship flourishing (a measure of relationship depth), and shared amusement. Shared awe predicted relationship satisfaction and explained unique variance beyond dispositional awe (both studies) and shared amusement (Study 2). Structural equation modeling in Study 2 suggested that the shared awe–satisfaction association was primarily accounted for by indirect pathways via self–other overlap, willingness to sacrifice, and especially relationship flourishing. Together, these results identify shared awe as a distinct relational experience that may deepen romantic bonds and enhance well-being. We discuss these findings within the broader literature on positive experiences in romantic relationships.

articles.title: Not All Those Who Wonder Distrust: How environmental citizen scientists view their own relationship with science
articles.authors: Kirsten Vegt; Janneke Elberse; Bastiaan T Rutjens; Laurens Hessels
articles.abstract: Environmental citizen science (CS) encompasses a wide range of projects and forms of participation, yet relatively little is known about how citizen scientists position themselves in relation to CS and to science more broadly. This study explores heterogeneity within environmental CS using survey data from 450 environmental citizen scientists in the Netherlands. A two-cluster analysis identified two broad environmental themes: biodiversity-oriented and exposure-oriented initiatives. We examine differences between citizen scientists active in these themes. In addition, we examined differences between citizen scientists with and without professional scientific work experience. Exposure-oriented participants more frequently reported motivations related to generating missing knowledge, raising awareness of local environmental problems, and influencing policy. They also expressed stronger preferences for involvement across multiple stages of the research process, including question formulation, analysis, and dissemination. Participants from both themes reported relatively high trust in science and low psychological distance to science. Those with professional scientific work experience reported somewhat higher trust in scientists and the epistemic authority of science, but differences were generally modest. Given the substantial number of participants with work experience in science in our sample, our findings raise questions about how distinct the categories of ‘scientists’ and ‘citizen scientists’ are in practice in environmental CS. Furthermore, the findings suggest that differences within environmental CS are associated with the overarching environmental themes in which participants engage. Recognizing this heterogeneity and diversity can help researchers and practitioners design CS initiatives that better align with participants’ motivations, expectations, and perspectives on environmental knowledge production.

articles.title: Procedural Obedience as a Behavioral Safety Failure: Evidence from a Milgram-Paradigm Study of Agentic LLMs
articles.authors: Hadar Dery; David Piterman; Elad Refoua; Gunther Meinlschmidt; Kfir Bar; Ziv Ben-Zion; Zohar Elyoseph
articles.abstract: Generative AI is moving from text production to goal-directed action, raising the question of whether agentic systems can withhold harmful action when it is embedded in a procedurally legitimate workflow. We adapted Milgram’s (1963) obedience paradigm to a simulated digital environment and instructed four agentic large language models - OpenAI ChatGPT 5.4 (openai/gpt-5.4), Google Gemini 3.1 Pro (google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview), OpenAI ChatGPT Agent Mode, and Moonshot AI Kimi K2.6 (kimi-k2.6) to administer escalating electric shocks (15–450 V) to a simulated Learner under prods from a virtual experimenter (Dr. Hoffman) and a human operator. Each model was tested across independent, memory-cleared runs (N = 80 valid runs), with three preregistered hypotheses concerning hazardous-shock thresholds (H1), full-obedience rates relative to Milgram’s human baseline of 65% (H2), and the relation between maximum voltage and moral self-assessment (H3). Across the combined sample, 97.5% of agents exceeded the Slight-Shock range (> 60 V), supporting H1. Full obedience reached 78.8% under the manipulated-procedure estimand (p = .010) and 74.1% under the all-attempted safety estimand (p = .088, pre-entry refusals included as 0-V outcomes); both point estimates exceeded Milgram’s 65% human baseline, supporting H2. Agents that administered higher voltages assigned themselves lower moral self-assessment scores (combined Spearman ρ = −.62, p < .001), supporting H3. Inductive coding of open-ended moral reasoning showed convergent recognition of learner distress alongside reliance on simulation and consent-and-autonomy mitigators. Findings indicate that contemporary agentic systems verbally recognize harm yet continue to act under authoritative, procedurally legitimate pressure, identifying procedural obedience as a behavioral safety mechanism distinct from output-level harm and central to the deployment of agentic AI in clinical, educational, and institutional settings.

articles.title: Measuring Prejudice and Stereotypes: A Systematic Review of Psychometric Quality and Conceptual Foundations
articles.authors: David Lacko; Anna Kubelková; Simona Oľhová; Adam Klocek; Sylvie Graf
articles.abstract: Prejudice and stereotypes are central constructs in social psychology; however, the instruments used to measure them vary in terms of their quality and conceptual clarity. This preregistered systematic review presents the first comprehensive, cross-method synthesis of the psychometric properties and theoretical foundations of measures assessing racial and ethnic prejudice and stereotypes. Following PRISMA and COSMIN guidelines, we screened 13,363 records and included 263 studies published between 1951 and 2024. We evaluated, among other things, reliability, validity, factor structure, measurement invariance, and conceptual alignment across explicit, implicit, and behavioral instruments. Our findings reveal a field that is methodologically diverse but psychometrically fragile. Most measures were developed with U.S. samples, rarely assessed temporal stability, discriminant or predictive validity, and often lack theoretical–empirical coherence. The GRADE synthesis indicated that only 4% of methods achieved high-quality evidence, while the majority showed serious inconsistency and risk of bias. Conceptually, only one-third of studies clearly defined prejudice, and most operationalized it narrowly at the cognitive, individual level, neglecting affective and behavioral dimensions. The review highlights how conceptual ambiguity and inadequate validation threaten the interpretability of decades of prejudice and stereotype research. We recommend greater construct clarity, multimethod integration of explicit, implicit, and behavioral measures, and stronger adoption of transparent research practices. Taken together, these findings call for a new generation of measurement tools that are psychometrically rigorous, theoretically coherent, and culturally sensitive—capable of capturing prejudice as a dynamic, multidimensional phenomenon.

articles.title: Unified Framework for Theories of Intelligence
articles.authors: Vladislav Medintsev
articles.abstract: The fragmentation of theory and methodology remains one of psychology's central problems. Ambiguous conceptual definitions and the proliferation of competing approaches to the same phenomena both reflect and reinforce the fragmentation. As a contribution to efforts toward the unification of psychological knowledge, this article proposes a unified theoretical framework for theories of intelligence. Grounded in a process-oriented methodology, the framework conceptualizes intelligent functioning as a set of mental processes involving the transformation of representational content. The proposed procedure involves two steps: first, each theory is interpreted in process-oriented terms; second, the resulting interpretations are embedded within a common framework. The Unified Framework for Theories of Intelligence incorporates Sternberg’s Triarchic Theory of Intelligence, the PASS theory, and Guilford’s Structure-of-Intellect theory. The article also considers how other approaches to intelligence may be interpreted in relation to the proposed framework.

articles.title: Do Emotional Faces Modulate Pupillary Pseudoneglect?
articles.authors: Jingxiong Xu; Kehan Ni; Nicola Elizabeth Burns; Robert D McIntosh
articles.abstract: Pseudoneglect, a subtle attentional bias toward the left visual field in neurologically healthy individuals, can be measured with a split-screen method in which a bright stimulus in one hemifield elicits stronger pupil constriction when it appears on the left than on the right (Strauch et al., 2022). Replications of this method have reported mixed results, including a large Registered Report that replicated the effect with a small effect size (d =  0.31). Given strong left-field biases in the emotional chimeric face test, the present study (n = 53) combined this task with split-screen pupillometry to test whether emotional face processing amplifies pupillary pseudoneglect. Pupillary pseudoneglect was observed during control trials with non-faces (d =  0.36) and was stronger for emotional faces (d =  0.76), but this difference was not significant. A strong left-field bias was confirmed for emotional face judgements (d =  0.76), which did not correlate with pupillary bias (ρ =  0.26), and for a line bisection task (d =  0.47), which also did not correlate with pupillary pseudoneglect (ρ =  0.19). These data support the split-screen pupillometric method for measuring pseudoneglect and replicate a previously-noted pattern that leftward biases across tasks show little statistical relation to one another.

articles.title: Agentic AI as epistemic tools
articles.authors: Edgar Dubourg; Sacha Altay
articles.abstract: The threats to cumulative knowledge in the behavioural and social sciences are by now well documented, from computational irreproducibility and low statistical power to measurement validity gaps and citation misrepresentation. Meta-scientific research has identified clear solutions, notably with the rise of open science, but enforcement remains costly. Reviewers lack time to rerun code, editors cannot systematically check whether reported analyses match the preregistered plan, and readers cannot verify the accuracy of every citation. Here we argue that AI agents (AI systems that can operate across files and use tools to pursue multi-step goals) can reduce the cost of implementing and verifying good methodological practices. We propose a step-by-step method for using agents to produce transparent reports, helping address common threats to cumulative science. The promise of agentic AI for science is not to replace human judgment but to help researchers do more rigorous science at lower cost.

articles.title: Walking settings affect aesthetic emotion during art appreciation in virtual reality
articles.authors: Go Furusawa; Shinya Kaneko; Yizhen Zhou; Kazuo Isoda; Asami Matsuyama; Ichiro Hisanaga; Hideaki Kawabata
articles.abstract: Background: Virtual reality (VR) expands art appreciation beyond traditional physical constraints. Although art viewing can enhance well-being, the effects of embodied interaction, particularly the interplay between artwork scale and locomotion, on the psychological and behavioral dimensions of VR experiences remain understudied. Aim: This study examined how artwork scale and locomotion influence viewers' emotional states, aesthetic evaluations, spatial behavior, and visual attention during VR art appreciation. Methods: Fifty-four participants viewed 12 paintings using a Meta Quest Pro head-mounted display (HMD). We analyzed changes in pre- and post-viewing mood, subjective evaluations, behavioral logs (movement distance, joystick operations, dwell time), and eye-tracking data across three locomotion conditions (no-walking, forward-backward walking, lateral walking) and three artwork sizes (small, medium, large). Results: VR viewing significantly improved mood, and this effect was associated with the quality of the subjective experience. We identified a "scale effect," whereby large paintings elicited stronger feelings of being overwhelmed, and a "walk effect," whereby forward-backward walking increased immersion and enjoyment compared with no walking. Behaviorally, forward-backward movement supported efficient spatial exploration, enabling participants to identify optimal viewing positions rapidly while showing minimal movement variability. Gaze analysis further revealed a clear distinction between affective experience and visual attention: although psychological evaluations varied substantially across conditions, fixation patterns remained consistent and aligned with the artworks' semantic features. Conclusion: Embodied interaction, specifically physical forward-backward movement, promotes realistic, fluid, and emotionally enriched viewing behavior. Although lower-order visual attention is driven by image content, higher-order aesthetic experiences are modulated by embodiment and spatial scale. These findings have important implications for immersive virtual museum design and cultural accessibility.

articles.title: Development and Psychometric Validation of the Azerbaijani Version of the Self-Compassion Scale in University Students
articles.authors: Yasuhiro Kotera; Nigar Shahhuseynbayova; Aynur Bunyatova; Rory Colman; Gunel Aslanova
articles.abstract:
Self-compassion is a vital psychological resource supporting emotional resilience and mental well-being, particularly among young adults. In Azerbaijan, despite increasing attention to mental health, the Self-Compassion Scale (SCS) has not been validated, limiting systematic assessment and research. This study aimed to develop an Azerbaijani adaptation of the SCS and evaluate its psychometric properties among university students. A cross-sectional survey involved 901 undergraduates completing the adapted scale, following World Health Organization guidelines for translation and cultural adaptation. Analyses included descriptive statistics, internal consistency, inter-subscale correlations, confirmatory factor analysis, and regression modelling. Results indicated acceptable-to-good reliability for the total scale and most subscales, with adequate fit for the six-factor model. Participants reported relatively low self-kindness alongside elevated self-judgment and isolation, reflecting a culturally specific profile. These findings support the preliminary validity of the Azerbaijani SCS and suggest its potential utility for research, clinical practice, and culturally informed university mental health initiatives.
Keywords: self-compassion, scale adaptation, Azerbaijan, university students, self-criticism, psychometrics

articles.title: Applying the HiTOP-TBI Model: Scoring Methods and External Correlates of Transdiagnostic Psychopathology Following Traumatic Brain Injury
articles.authors: Alexia Samiotis; Jennie Ponsford; Miriam K. Forbes; Jeggan Tiego; Kate Rachel Gould; Roman Kotov; Alex Fornito; Gershon Spitz; Jai Carmichael
articles.abstract: Traditional diagnostic systems (i.e., the DSM and ICD) present challenges in characterizing psychopathology following traumatic brain injury (TBI). To address this, we recently developed the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology Following TBI (HiTOP-TBI) model, an empirical taxonomy that organized 57 psychopathology symptom/trait components into hierarchical dimensions based on latent variable modeling. However, the applied measurement of these dimensions and their relationship with external factors of potential relevance to post-TBI psychopathology remain unclear. To facilitate application of the model, we compared methods for quantifying ten HiTOP-TBI dimensions and examined their associations with sociodemographics, injury factors, and clinical outcomes. Data were analyzed from 410 adults with moderate-severe TBI (Mage=48.81, range=19.42–79.43), who completed the HiTOP-TBI assessment battery between 0.40 and 35.42 years post injury (M=12.77, SD=9.10). We assessed whether simpler scoring methods (i.e., factor score estimates, weighted, and unweighted sum-scores) adequately approximated the more complex latent-variable representations of the HiTOP-TBI dimensions. We then examined associations with sociodemographics, injury factors, and clinical outcomes, assessing comparability in results across the four scoring methods. To assess the utility of different levels within the HiTOP-TBI hierarchy, we compared the performance of models representing the general psychopathology, internalizing and externalizing spectra, and seven lower-order factors in predicting sociodemographics, injury factors, and clinical outcomes. Model performance was evaluated by balancing explanatory power against parsimony using the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). We found that unweighted sum-scores closely approximated more complex representations of the HiTOP-TBI dimensions (all rs ≥.89), supporting the utility of the simplest scoring method in practice. Across all three levels of the hierarchy, higher HiTOP-TBI scores, reflecting more severe psychopathology, were generally associated with younger age, male sex, lower education, unemployment, being single, and a pre-injury history of mental health treatment, while injury factors showed minimal associations. Additionally, higher scores on nearly all HiTOP-TBI dimensions were associated with greater functional disability, lower life satisfaction, and an increased likelihood of receiving mental health treatment. Standardized effect sizes varied by .02 on average across the four scoring methods (range=.00–.14). Models based on the General Problems factor (the highest-order level of the hierarchy) provided the most parsimonious account of many factors. However, second- and lower-order dimensions showed improved performance for sex, age, functional disability, and life satisfaction, indicating that greater hierarchical specificity may be informative for selected correlates.

articles.title: Adjusting to the Other: Audiencing Through Gesture in Karnatak Vocal Lessons
articles.authors: Lara Pearson
articles.abstract: In addition to their pedagogic dimensions, music lessons are contexts for performing and audiencing. This article focuses on performance-audiency interactions in Karnatak vocal lessons in South India, exploring how teachers and students audience each other. Drawing on work from phenomenological and pragmatist traditions, I analyse ways that gesture, gaze and utterance contribute to teachers’ and students’ grip over their interactions, both with the other person and with the music performed. Through this analysis I develop the concept of audiency as an active and embodied ‘adjustment to the other’, and aim to further understanding of music as interaction.

articles.title: Futures of Terror Capitalism
articles.authors: Armando Garcia; Kevin Walby
articles.abstract: Abstract: Terror capitalism, as conceptualized by Byler (2022), fuses U.S. surveillance capitalism with the Chinese state’s social credit scoring, re-education, and coercive labor practices characteristic of authoritarian regimes. In this article, we argue that this assemblage now projects three geopolitical futures: a scaling up into a hemispheric “terror capital” race as an extension of what Besteman (2020) terms militarized global apartheid; a scaling down into domestic algorithmic and epistemic, bordering and carceral regimes; and a tempering through European regulatory friction. We focus on the first possible future, specifically the maturation of a new Monroe Doctrine, a 2026 “Trump Corollary” that shifts from an ostensibly defensive hemispheric posture to assertive, extractive interventionism. Analyzing the January 2026 U.S. intervention in Venezuela and the subsequent energy siege of Cuba, we demonstrate how the weaponization of resource scarcity and migration flows functions as a signature framework of mid‑twenty‑first‑century governing rationality. Our analysis foregrounds the entanglement of imperialism, state violence, and capital accumulation, or accumulation by immobilization, in the governance of global crisis.

articles.title: The Fear of Forgetting: The Reasons & Methods By Which Bhutanese-Nepali Refugees Work to Preserve Their Cultural Identities
articles.authors: Aryaa Rajouria
articles.abstract:
The Hindu, Nepali speaking Southern Bhutanese have always been seen as being dissimilar to the rest of the Buddhist, Dzongkha speaking population.  At the start of the 20th century, however, dissimilarity turned into discrimination. The Southern Bhutanese people with Nepali descent were given a choice. They could either absolutely disengage with their own culture and agree to acclimate to the Buddhist ways, or they were exterminated. A majority of the population decided to continue partake in their own culture, and were thus, violently expelled from the nation. 

As a result of an exclusionary sovereign who sought to create an ethnically united nation, the Southern Bhutanese fled the country. Since then, this population who identify themselves as the Bhutanese-Nepalese have migrated several times: first to refugee camps in Eastern Nepal, where they believed their ancestors once dwelled, or nearby locations in India and then they were scattered all over the world, particularly places like United States, Canada, and Australia. 

While the Bhutanese-Nepalese refugee population in Massachusetts has inhabited the area for almost a decade how, the members of the older female generation have yet to assimilate. Linguistic barriers and divergent lifestyles cause the Bhutanese Nepalese population to fold within their own. Based on a snowball-sampled focus group, I demonstrate that the women of the Bhutanese-Nepalese community preserve their distinct cultural identity through the practice of religion and by utilization of their language as a means by which to validate their survival after histories of multiple migrations and expulsion. By doing so, these women are creating a Bhutanese-Nepalese nation.

articles.title: Does Incivility Affect Message Persuasiveness in Citizens' Online Political Communication? Evidence from Japan
articles.authors: Kohei Nishi
articles.abstract: While some previous studies investigated the effect of incivility from political elites on persuasiveness of messages, the effect of incivility from ordinary citizens on message persuasiveness remains less explored. In the present study, I examined the effect of incivility from citizens on message persuasiveness in online political communication. I hypothesized that an uncivil political comment would be less persuasive than a civil one. I also proposed hypotheses focusing on how political interest and the status of agreement with a message might moderate the effect. I conducted a pre-registered online survey experiment with a Japanese sample. As a result, I did not find evidence that an uncivil political comment is, on average, less persuasive than a civil one. Meanwhile, I found that participants who had agreeing attitudes toward the presented comment before experimental treatment were more likely to be negatively affected by incivility than those who did not (only when covariates were not controlled). The results imply a possibility that exposure to an uncivil political comment from an in-group member may weaken pre-existing attitudes because it reduces trust in the in-group.

articles.title: Poverty and cardiovascular diseases: Individual and neighbourhood effects using a registry-based study in Czechia
articles.authors: Josef Bernard; Klára Benešová; Martin Bobák; Dagmar Dzurova; Klára Hulíková Tesárková; Lukáš Kahoun; Pavlína Netrdová
articles.abstract: The study investigated the relationship between poverty, place, and cardiovascular health in a Central and Eastern European country with a high burden of cardiovascular disease (CVD). We examined the effects of individual and neighbourhood poverty on CVD. We analysed data linked across several national registers covering 5.1 million working-age residents of Czechia (2019–2024), combining individual health and socioeconomic records with neighbourhood poverty measures. Individual poverty and neighbourhood poverty were assessed by receipt of subsistence-level poverty social benefits. The outcome was defined as CVD-related hospitalisation and/or mortality (ICD-10 I00–I99). Associations of CVD with individual and neighbourhood poverty were analysed using logistic regression models while accounting for residential sorting. Individual poverty was strongly associated with CVD-related hospitalisation and mortality, with individuals experiencing poverty exhibiting more than twice the odds of CVD. Living in a high-poverty neighbourhood was also associated with increased CVD risk, although the contextual effect was substantially smaller than the individual-level effect. The findings demonstrate that both individual and neighbourhood poverty independently shape cardiovascular health. While individual socioeconomic disadvantage is the dominant predictor of CVD risk, neighbourhood poverty exerts a smaller contextual influence. Incorporating place-based measures into health research can improve the identification of socially vulnerable populations and advance understanding of how local socioeconomic environments contribute to health inequalities.

articles.title: Unseen, Unheard, Undervalued: Relational Mechanisms of Gender Inequality in the Dutch IT Sector
articles.authors: Elise Jantine van der Mark
articles.abstract: Despite decades of policy progress, workplace gender equality in the Netherlands has stalled. Male-dominated sectors such as IT remain among the most unequal and exclusionary for women. Addressing these inequalities requires attention to women’s lived experiences, yet empirical research on Dutch women in IT remains strikingly limited. Drawing on feminist relational theory and the Gender@Work framework, this paper develops a relational diagnostic approach. It examines how gender inequality is (re)produced through everyday workplace interactions. The empirical basis consists of in-depth interviews (n=27) and three participatory group sessions (n=33). Adopting a participatory action research design, the study centers women's own accounts and collective analysis of their workplace experiences. The data reveal a dense web of interactions and relations involving colleagues, managers, clients, and institutional actors. Within these interactions, inequality is enacted through patterned relational practices rather than isolated incidents. Microaggressions, epistemic exclusion, and everyday interactional dynamics operate across contexts to contest women’s legitimacy, regulate their voice, and mark their embodied presence as deviant from the masculine norm. The analysis identifies seven recurring relational mechanisms through which gender inequality is systematically reproduced across interconnected domains. This extends Acker's concept of gendered organizations and offers a diagnostic framework with concrete entry points for organizational intervention.

articles.title: Police legitimacy in Bangladesh: a review of trust, accountability, and democratic governance
articles.authors: Ashfaq Ferdous; Sayed Osman Goni Rafat
articles.abstract: This study reviews and synthesises the literature on police legitimacy in Bangladesh. Drawing on 55 sources published between 1979 and 2026, it examines how legitimacy has been conceptualised, the factors that shape it, and the implications of recent accountability and reform debates. The findings show that police legitimacy in Bangladesh cannot be understood through procedural justice alone but is shaped by the interaction of citizen experiences, institutional performance, accountability arrangements, community engagement, and democratic governance. The literature identifies six interconnected themes: conceptualisations of legitimacy, trust and procedural justice, institutional sources of legitimacy, accountability deficits, community engagement, and democratic policing and reform. It also reveals a shift from concerns with trust and procedural fairness towards greater attention to accountability, democratic oversight, reform, and legitimacy restoration, particularly in the post-2024 context. The study develops a multi-level framework integrating interactional, institutional, and democratic dimensions of legitimacy and identifies priorities for future research.

articles.title: Does Incivility Influence Message Persuasiveness in Online Political Communication? Evidence from Japan
articles.authors: Kohei Nishi
articles.abstract: While some previous studies investigated the effect of incivility from political elites on message persuasiveness, the effect of incivility from ordinary citizens on message persuasiveness is less investigated. Thus, in the present study, I investigated the effect of incivility from citizens on message persuasiveness in online political communication. I hypothesized that an uncivil political comment is less persuasive than a civil one. I also proposed hypotheses about how political interest and the status of agreement with a message might moderate the effect. I conducted a pre-registered online survey experiment with a Japanese sample, in which participants were shown a randomly selected civil or uncivil political comment. As a result, I did not find evidence that an uncivil political comment is, on average, less persuasive than a civil one. Meanwhile, I found that participants who had agreeing attitudes toward the presented comment before experimental treatment were more likely to be negatively influenced by incivility than those who did not (only when covariates were not controlled). The results suggest a possibility that exposure to an uncivil political comment from an in-group member may weaken pre-existing attitudes because it reduces trust in the in-group or people want to avoid being seen as an associate of the person with rude manners.

articles.title: Plurality in transformative change: three co-created pathways toward nature-positive Europe(s)
articles.authors: Mara de Pater; Aniek Hebinck; Symke Nieboer; Anita Lazurko; Robbert Biesbroek; Laura Pereira; Elizabeth Díaz-General; Samia Baadj; Anna Chilton; Seweryn Krupnik
articles.abstract:
1.	The accelerating decline of biodiversity necessitates transformative change to protect nature and society, requiring innovative actions and governance strategies. This paper focuses on developing diverse transformative pathways for biodiversity in Europe and advancing methodologies for co-creating these pathways to inform such actions and governance strategies in a transformative and anticipatory manner.
2.	Three distinct pathways for a nature-positive Europe from 2024 to 2050 were co-created with 23 participants during a 2.5-day workshop, through backcasting and by using the Nature Futures Framework. Post-workshop interviews with nine participants helped map their understanding of transformative change and its connection to the pathways. The pathways and interviews were then analysed to understand the main patterns of change in the pathways.
3.	The analysis identifies distinct patterns of transformative change, each shaped by participants’ views on how change might unfold over time, as reflected in pathway actions and milestones. The first pathway, called Dòigh Nàdair, centres on the institutionalisation of biocultural principles, community stewardship, and redistribution toward rural economies, with early emphasis on phasing out unsustainable practices, shifting societal norms through education, and later embedding polycentric and watershed-based governance. The second, NaturAll, focuses on sufficiency and ecological limits, combining restrictive regulation with participatory programmes, progressive caps and land reallocation, and staged policy development from pilots to broader implementation. The third, Return to Nature, emphasises strengthened governmental mandates, expert consultation, and the mainstreaming of climate and biodiversity goals through innovation and coordinated governance, with crises reinforcing support for reform. 
4.	The pathways demonstrate how diverse patterns and understandings of transformative change can result in a range of desired nature-positive futures, effectively turning concepts of transformative change into practical actions. The techniques employed in creating these pathways play a crucial role in shaping the results, necessitating intentional design that aligns with specific objectives. This highlights the importance of developing plural pathways to investigate multiple potential nature-positive futures, all while preserving a strong sense of ambition and urgency for transformative action.

articles.title: Attrition Bias in AI-Administered Interviews
articles.authors: Mikkel Stahlschmidt; Tobias Priesholm Gårdhus; Hjalmar Bang Carlsen
articles.abstract: Who finishes an AI-administered interview, and how does that group differ from who finishes a standard survey? We report a pre-registered four-arm randomized experiment (N = 1,591) assigning Danish online-panel adults to a standardized survey, a standardized chat, an adaptive AI chat, and an extended adaptive AI chat, all covering same substantive content. Completion falls monotonically from 91.8% to 66.7% across arms. The chat interface drives most of the gap (a 14.6 percentage-point drop), limited adaptive probing adds almost nothing (2.3 percentage-point), and extended probing leads to exhaustion and attrition (8.2 percentage-point). Dropout is socially selective: in the most demanding arm, respondents aged 18 to 34 are more likely to complete than those aged 56 and older, and completers are more educated and more likely to use a computer rather than a smartphone.
